name: Butterfree
route: Viridian Forest, Route 2, Ilex-like deep woods, flower meadows, orchard belts, grave meadows, river valleys, bloom fields, honey sites, and migration corridors after Metapod emergence
habitat: Flower-rich forests, rain-tolerant canopy edges, orchards, meadows, grave fields, sickroom gardens, and nectar sites within several miles of safe roosting trees
behaviorTemperament: Gentle and avoidant when calm, but chemically dangerous when trapped, swarming, mating, or defending Caterpie. It does not need malice to harm anyone breathing its wing powder.
diet: Nectar, honey, flower fluids, fruit sugars, tree sap, mineral moisture, and salt from sweat or tears
activityPattern: Diurnal and crepuscular. Most active during bloom, after rain, and around dawn emergence. Mating flights and migration clouds may occur near dusk.
bestTimeToFind: Dawn near fresh Metapod shells, warm mornings in flower fields, after rain in orchards, or at dusk along nectar-rich migration corridors
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Easy to see in open air but difficult to approach safely because wing powder may drift without obvious warning.
conservationStatus: Valuable pollinator; protected in many orchard and forest districts
populationStatus: Locally common after healthy Caterpie and Metapod seasons; vulnerable to Metapod mishandling, pesticide use, loss of flowering corridors, and fear-driven extermination of larvae.
description: The final winged Bug/Flying stage of the Caterpie line, known for compound eyes, nectar tracking, rain-repellent wing scales, and powders that may poison, stun, confuse, or induce sleep when released in enclosed air.
lore:
Butterfree is the reason some villages covered their mirrors during spring.

Not because the PokÃ©mon was ugly.

Because it was beautiful enough to make grief answer.

After Caterpieâ€™s hunger and Metapodâ€™s stillness, Butterfree feels like mercy. It rises from the split shell wet, trembling, and fragile, then opens wings that seem too large for the small body that carries them. Children cheer when they see the first one of the season. Farmers take it as a good sign. Old people smile, because a flying Butterfree means at least one helpless Metapod survived the waiting.

That is the kind part of the story.

The cruel part is that Butterfree does not emerge empty.

It carries the forest with it.

Its wings are covered in microscopic scales and chemical powders. These powders can soothe, stun, poison, confuse, or put living things to sleep depending on the species, season, diet, and stress state of the individual. A calm Butterfree dusting a flower patch is harmless to most people. A frightened Butterfree trapped in a bedroom can turn the air into a soft, glittering hazard.

The powder is beautiful in lamplight.

That is why people breathe it in.

Victims usually do not panic at first. They become calm. Their eyelids lower. Sound feels distant. Pain softens. Memory loosens. Many report smelling childhood gardens, fresh bread, old rain, dead relativesâ€™ clothing, or flowers that never grew anywhere near the place they collapsed. Some laugh. Some cry. Some follow the Butterfree with outstretched hands.

This is how the old stories of wing-led sleepwalkers began.

A person under heavy Butterfree powder influence may walk without understanding where they are going. They may step into roads, rivers, old wells, burning hearths, or open windows. The Butterfree is not leading them in the human sense. It is simply flying toward moonlight, nectar, migration paths, or other Butterfree. The poisoned human follows because the powder has made everything feel gentle.

There are worse ways to die.

That is not comfort.

Butterfree are drawn to flowers, fruit, tree sap, sweat, tears, and certain medicinal herbs. During heavy bloom years, they gather around sickrooms, funeral houses, battlefields, and plague tents because those places often contain cut flowers, exposed bodies, sweet medicines, damp cloth, and salt from human grief. This created one of Kantoâ€™s oldest and darkest beliefs:

When Butterfree gathers at a window, someone inside is leaving.

Science explains the attraction.

Science does not make the sight less terrible.

In rural records, Butterfree appear again and again beside the dying. A child with fever sees wings at the curtain. An old woman reaches toward the rafters and says her husband has come in white and violet. A soldier bleeds in tall grass while Butterfree settle on the wildflowers around him, their wings opening and closing in silence.

Sometimes they are simply feeding.

Sometimes their powder eases pain.

Sometimes the dying inhale too much and slip away quietly.

This is why Butterfree became both blessing and omen. Families wanted them near enough to comfort the sick, but not close enough to fill the room. Some houses hung thin cloth screens over windows during bloom season. Others placed bowls of sugared water outside so Butterfree would land there instead of on the sill. In old villages, funeral flowers were shaken before being brought indoors.

If white powder fell from them, the flowers were burned.

Butterfreeâ€™s eyes deepen the unease. Their compound structure allows them to detect motion, color shifts, and polarized light with extraordinary sensitivity. A Butterfree can see a person crying from across a garden, not because it understands sorrow, but because tears catch light. It can find bruised fruit, fresh wounds, and damp soil by visual and chemical cues. It can sense tiny movements from predators and flee before the watcher realizes they have been seen.

To humans, this gives Butterfree the appearance of knowing too much.

A swarm may turn all at once toward a hidden observer.

A single Butterfree may hover outside a closed door where someone is ill.

A group may rise from a field just before riders discover a body beneath the flowers.

There is no proof Butterfree understand death.

There is proof they attend it.

Their mating flights are among the most beautiful natural events in Kanto and among the most dangerous to witness up close. Hundreds may gather in open fields, river valleys, orchards, or grave meadows, filling the air with soft wingbeats and drifting powder. The ground glitters afterward. Flowers bloom heavily the following week. Bees avoid the area for days. Small mammals sleep in the grass without waking until morning.

Unprotected humans may experience euphoria, paralysis, temporary blindness, hallucinations, or memory gaps.

Several old disappearance accounts describe people walking into Butterfree mating clouds at dusk and not returning until sunrise. Those who survived remembered music, though no instruments were present. One man claimed he danced with his dead sister beneath violet snow. Another insisted he had spent seven years in a garden and came back to find only one night had passed.

Rangers classify these as neurochemical hallucinations.

Local families still call them borrowed funerals.

A Butterfree swarm can also defend itself with startling coordination. Individually, they are fragile. Their bodies break easily. Their wings tear. Rain can ground them. But when threatened, many release powder at once, creating a drifting wall of sleep, paralysis, and confusion. Predators that rush a swarm often stagger, collapse, or turn on one another. Humans firing nets or weapons into a swarm may forget why they came, drop equipment, or sit down smiling until the Butterfree pass.

There are battlefield stories of soldiers refusing to advance through clouds of wings.

Commanders called it cowardice.

The soldiers called it seeing home.

Despite all this, Butterfree is not malevolent. Like Caterpie, it is driven by simple needs: nectar, warmth, safety, mates, migration, and egg-laying sites. It pollinates forests, restores orchards after infestation years, and carries life from place to place on powdered wings. Without Butterfree, the Green Veil districts would have suffered even worse crop collapse after Caterpie stripped the leaves. The same line that devoured the orchards returned later to pollinate the surviving flowers.

That is the unsettling balance of the species.

Caterpie takes.

Metapod endures.

Butterfree returns something, but not always safely.

The old field rules are written like prayers:

Do not chase Butterfree indoors.

Do not sleep in a closed room where wing powder has settled.

Do not follow one at dusk unless you know the ground ahead.

Do not enter a mating flight without a mask and guide rope.

If Butterfree gather at a sickroom window, open another window across the room and let the air move.

If one lands on your hand, stay still.

If many land on you, sit down before your legs forget they belong to you.

And if you see a Butterfree emerge from a Metapod shell, do not touch its wings.

It has survived being eaten, sealed, silenced, and remade.

Let the beautiful thing decide when it is ready to fly.
survivalUse: Can reveal nearby flower patches, honey, fresh water corridors, recent emergence sites, and healthy pollination zones. Calm individuals may guide a ranger toward bloom, but following one at dusk is unsafe.
handlingWarning: Do not chase indoors, clap near wings, trap in closed rooms, or enter mating clouds without a mask and guide rope. Powder exposure can cause sleep, paralysis, confusion, hallucinations, or eye injury.
encounterAdvice: Stay upwind, keep your mouth and eyes covered, and allow the Butterfree a clear route to open air. If a swarm gathers, sit or kneel before dizziness begins and leave by rope or partner guidance.
firstAid: Move exposed people to clean air, rinse eyes, remove powder-contaminated clothing, monitor breathing and alertness, and seek medical care for paralysis, confusion, severe drowsiness, or suspected poison exposure.
companionSuitability: Good for skilled calm handlers with open-air space; poor for enclosed homes, hospitals, nurseries, or anyone prone to respiratory sensitivity
fieldBehaviors: Sheds powder from wings when startled
Locates tiny pollen patches at long distance
Defends Caterpie from birds with scales
Gathers around flowering sickrooms and funeral plants
Forms dusk mating clouds over bloom fields
resistances: Rain on wings due water-repellent powder
signsOfPresence: Empty Metapod shells split from inside
Flower patches heavily visited at dawn
Butterfly wing shadows at sickroom curtains
equipment: Mask or respirator for powder clouds
Light net for emergency relocation only
Field partner during dusk swarm observation
fieldNote: Beauty is not safety. A calm Butterfree in open air is usually gentle; a frightened Butterfree in a closed room can make the room itself hazardous.
originStory:
Butterfree was formally linked to the Caterpie line in 1802, six years after Caterpie was recorded during the Green Veil Summer and five years after Metapod was confirmed as its chrysalis stage.

The delay was caused by two failures.

The first was scientific impatience.

The second was beauty.

Early researchers saw Caterpie become Metapod, but they did not immediately prove Metapod became Butterfree. Too many specimens died in cabinets, jars, heated rooms, and transport boxes. Too many emerged malformed. Too many were dissected before they could finish changing. When winged forms appeared in the same districts after mass Metapod seasons, naturalists argued that Butterfree were separate pollinators attracted by the damaged orchards.

Local villagers knew better.

They had watched the shells split.

But early science often trusted dead specimens more than living witnesses.

The formal proof began with the fence-rail Metapod observed by Iven Carrow in 1797. That individual emerged successfully, but its dye mark from the Caterpie stage was lost when the outer shell split. Carrow recorded the emergence, but critics claimed he had not proven continuity. They argued that a Butterfree may have landed on the shell after some other creature escaped or died inside.

It was a ridiculous objection.

It still delayed the record.

Carrow spent the next five years trying to prove the line cleanly. He changed his methods after repeated failures. He stopped removing Metapod from chosen sites. He marked nearby bark instead of the shell. He sketched shell scars, cluster positions, and individual anchoring threads. He hired local children, farmers, and widows to watch known Metapod sites at dawn, when emergence was most likely.

The final proof came in 1802 during the Second Veil Bloom.

A marked Caterpie from a controlled orchard plot had transformed into Metapod on the underside of a chapel gate. The gate was not moved. The area was watched continuously by rotating observers. On the twelfth morning, the shell split under direct observation. A Butterfree emerged from it, bearing a tiny surviving trace of mineral dye near the base of one leg where the old larval skin had marked deeper tissue.

The same creature was observed drying its wings, feeding from clover, and releasing a faint powder when startled by a dog.

The Caterpie line was finally complete in the formal record:

1796: Caterpie documented during the Green Veil Summer.

1797: Metapod confirmed as Caterpieâ€™s living chrysalis.

1802: Butterfree confirmed as the winged adult stage after controlled emergence observation.

The villagers were not impressed.

They had known for generations.

What changed after 1802 was not local understanding, but official fear. Once Butterfree was proven to come from the same line that caused orchard infestations and silk-sealed homes, authorities realized the entire cycle had to be managed as one ecological event. Killing Caterpie reduced Metapod numbers. Disturbing Metapod reduced Butterfree emergence. Reducing Butterfree harmed pollination, which weakened orchards, which made remaining trees more vulnerable to the next Caterpie outbreak.

The species was not a pest.

It was a cycle that punished anyone who only understood one stage.

Butterfreeâ€™s origin is now explained as the final solution to Caterpie vulnerability. The larval stage eats aggressively because it must gather enough energy to remake itself. The Metapod stage trades mobility for protection during reconstruction. Butterfree emerges light, winged, and chemically defended, able to disperse across wide areas and locate flowering sites through extraordinary vision and scent.

Its powder likely began as loose wing scales that irritated predators. Over time, those scales became chemically active. Some caused numbness. Some caused drowsiness. Some confused predators long enough for escape. In modern Butterfree, these powders vary by individual and condition. A relaxed specimen may shed almost harmless dust. A stressed one may produce enough compound to incapacitate a small room.

This explains why Butterfree was both loved and feared in old communities.

It saved orchards.

It haunted sickrooms.

It pollinated graves.

It turned battlefields into flower meadows.

It made children smile and widows close their windows.

One of the earliest post-1802 studies recorded Butterfree gathering over a mass burial site from a winter fever outbreak. The official explanation was simple: wildflowers had grown heavily in the disturbed soil, attracting pollinators. The field notes include another detail. The Butterfree did not feed at first. They circled the site in widening spirals, then settled on the grave markers in rows.

No one knew what to do with that observation.

It was removed from the published version.

Local tradition kept it.

In some villages, Butterfree wings became symbols of release. Empty Metapod shells were placed near graves. Butterfree carvings were hung in rooms where someone had died after long illness. During spring, families opened windows briefly at dawn to â€œlet the winged ones carry the name out.â€ This was not official religion. It was field grief, built from years of watching something soft crawl away, seal itself, vanish, and return changed.

The darker origin theories focus on the powderâ€™s effect on humans. Some researchers argue that Butterfree unintentionally shaped mourning rituals because its compounds made people near death seem calmer, more accepting, or more dreamlike. Families exposed to low doses around sickbeds may have interpreted chemical sedation as spiritual peace. Others suggest Butterfree gathered around funerary flowers so often that humans began associating the species with souls, transformation, and afterlife passage.

Both theories can be true.

Nature does not become less sacred because chemistry is involved.

Carrowâ€™s final writing on Butterfree was less dismissive than his early work. He described the species as â€œthe gentle adult of a destructive childhood, carrying on its wings both apology and warning.â€ His private journal was stranger. In his final years, he kept the empty shell from the first fence-rail Metapod beside a pressed Butterfree wing found after the Second Veil Bloom.

Under them he wrote:

â€œThe forest does not forgive. It transforms.â€

Modern field guides describe Butterfree as a valuable pollinator, a beginner-friendly evolved Bug-type, and a powder hazard requiring ventilation and eye protection. All of that is true.

But old Kanto families remember more.

They remember sealed houses.

They remember clicking shells.

They remember pale wings at windows where fever burned.

They remember waking after a swarm passed with tears on their faces and no memory of why they had been crying.

Butterfree is the final mercy of the Caterpie line.

Mercy, in nature, is not the same as safety.

evolutionLine.name: Caterpie
evolutionLine.note: Leaf-feeding larval stage. Soft, fast-growing, and swarm-dependent. Silk output increases before hardening into Metapod.

evolutionLine.name: Metapod
evolutionLine.note: Living chrysalis stage. Motion is surrendered for shell defense while the body reorganizes toward winged emergence.

evolutionLine.name: Butterfree
evolutionLine.note: Winged adult pollinator and powder hazard. Its scales can carry sleep, paralysis, poison, and confusion risk in enclosed air.

mainLine.name: Caterpie
mainLine.note: Soft larval form. Eats leaves aggressively, climbs with suction-pad feet, releases defensive odor, and builds silk before hardening.

mainLine.name: Metapod
mainLine.note: Armored chrysalis. Its shell protects a soft, vulnerable internal body that is rebuilding itself for emergence.

mainLine.name: Butterfree
mainLine.note: Final winged pollinator. Locates nectar at great distance and sheds toxic, sleep-inducing, or stunning powders when stressed.

target.name: Gigantamax Butterfree
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat scale output, wind shear, and toxic powder dispersal as disaster-scale hazards.

domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated by skilled Bug-type handlers; valued as pollinator but restricted in enclosed public spaces
prey: Nectar, honey, pollen traces, fruit sugars, sap, mineral moisture, and flower fluids
territoryRange: Often forages within several miles of nest or roost; strong nectar tracking may draw it over six miles toward bloom fields
lifeCycle: Emerges from Metapod starting at Level 10, dries and fills its wings, begins nectar feeding, pollinates flowering areas, mates in seasonal flights, and lays eggs on suitable host leaves to begin the Caterpie stage again.
containmentRequirements: Large open-air flight enclosure, strong ventilation, nectar sources, roost branches, powder filtration for indoor observation rooms, no tight nets, and emergency air-clearing procedures
ecologicalRole: Major pollinator after Caterpie feeding years, nectar transporter, flower locator, bird deterrent for larvae, and chemical defense species that influences predator movement through bloom fields.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Powder can irritate respiratory tissue; wing mites, fungal scale damage, and contaminated nectar sources can create health concerns in managed colonies.
reproductionNesting: Pairs during bloom-season flights and lays eggs on suitable host leaves near food-rich forest edges. Adults may revisit Caterpie feeding areas but do not provide mammal-like parental care.
predators: Pidgeotto, Fearow, Golbat, larger Bug-types, web-building predators, humans harvesting scales, and weather that damages wings
emergencyResponse: Ventilate enclosed spaces immediately, move people upwind, avoid stirring settled powder, guide Butterfree toward open light, and treat exposed humans as possible poison, sleep, or paralysis cases.
legalStatus: Generally legal to train, but indoor public display, powder harvesting, and Gigantamax containment may be regulated due to toxic-scale risk
lifespan: Wild adult stage varies from weeks to seasons depending on migration, predation, and bloom conditions. Managed care may extend survival with protected flight space and clean nectar.
socialStructure: Usually solitary or loose feeding groups, with dense seasonal mating flights and migration clouds. Swarm defense appears coordinated through shared chemical release rather than planning.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Hazard comes from toxic, stunning, sleep-inducing, or confusing wing powder.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Powder hazard can fill a small room or drift downwind across a field during swarm release.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast fluttering escape with strong short-distance climb
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Good aerial evasion in open air, poor if wings are wet, torn, or confined
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Launches from ground into immediate flight if wings are dry
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent on flowers and branches; vulnerable on smooth indoor surfaces

trainingRisk: Powder exposure, accidental sleep or paralysis, respiratory irritation, wing damage from rough handling, swarm panic, and Gigantamax-scale toxic dispersal
physiology: Butterfree has large compound eyes, delicate rain-repellent wings, fine chemical scales, antennae for sensory navigation, leg hairs that carry honey, and a nectar-feeding mouth structure suited for long-distance flower tracking.

keyFacts.tailLength: No true tail; broad wings dominate body profile
keyFacts.habitat: Flower fields, forests, orchards, and migration corridors
keyFacts.lifespan: Adult stage usually weeks to seasons in the wild
keyFacts.groupSize: Single adults, feeding groups, or large mating swarms

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate to high. Calm individuals bond well, but safe care requires open air, powder control, careful wing handling, and respect for mating-swarm behavior.
physicalCharacteristics: White-bodied butterfly-like Pokemon with large veined wings, purple limbs, antennae, large compound red eyes, fine wing scales, and a light but hazardous powder coating.
dietRestrictions: Avoid pesticide-treated flowers, contaminated honey, smoke-heavy rooms, sticky syrups that foul leg hairs, enclosed jars, and wet handling that clumps wing scales.

there are battlefield stories of soldiers refusing to advance through clouds of wings.

commanders called it cowardice.

the soldiers called it seeing home.

despite all this, butterfree is not malevolent. like caterpie, it is driven by simple needs: nectar, warmth, safety, mates, migration, and egg-laying sites. it pollinates forests, restores orchards after infestation years, and carries life from place to place on powdered wings. without butterfree, the green veil districts would have suffered even worse crop collapse after caterpie stripped the leaves. the same line that devoured the orchards returned later to pollinate the surviving flowers.

that is the unsettling balance of the species.

caterpie takes.

metapod endures.

butterfree returns something, but not always safely.

the old field rules are written like prayers:

do not chase butterfree indoors.

do not sleep in a closed room where wing powder has settled.

do not follow one at dusk unless you know the ground ahead.

do not enter a mating flight without a mask and guide rope.

if butterfree gather at a sickroom window, open another window across the room and let the air move.

if one lands on your hand, stay still.

if many land on you, sit down before your legs forget they belong to you.

and if you see a butterfree emerge from a metapod shell, do not touch its wings.

it has survived being eaten, sealed, silenced, and remade.

let the beautiful thing decide when it is ready to fly.",

  "originstory": "butterfree was formally linked to the caterpie line in 1802, six years after caterpie was recorded during the green veil summer and five years after metapod was confirmed as its chrysalis stage.

the delay was caused by two failures.

the first was scientific impatience.

the second was beauty.

early researchers saw caterpie become metapod, but they did not immediately prove metapod became butterfree. too many specimens died in cabinets, jars, heated rooms, and transport boxes. too many emerged malformed. too many were dissected before they could finish changing. when winged forms appeared in the same districts after mass metapod seasons, naturalists argued that butterfree were separate pollinators attracted by the damaged orchards.

local villagers knew better.

they had watched the shells split.

but early science often trusted dead specimens more than living witnesses.

the formal proof began with the fence-rail metapod observed by iven carrow in 1797. that individual emerged successfully, but its dye mark from the caterpie stage was lost when the outer shell split. carrow recorded the emergence, but critics claimed he had not proven continuity. they argued that a butterfree may have landed on the shell after some other creature escaped or died inside.

it was a ridiculous objection.

it still delayed the record.

carrow spent the next five years trying to prove the line cleanly. he changed his methods after repeated failures. he stopped removing metapod from chosen sites. he marked nearby bark instead of the shell. he sketched shell scars, cluster positions, and individual anchoring threads. he hired local children, farmers, and widows to watch known metapod sites at dawn, when emergence was most likely.

the final proof came in 1802 during the second veil bloom.

a marked caterpie from a controlled orchard plot had transformed into metapod on the underside of a chapel gate. the gate was not moved. the area was watched continuously by rotating observers. on the twelfth morning, the shell split under direct observation. a butterfree emerged from it, bearing a tiny surviving trace of mineral dye near the base of one leg where the old larval skin had marked deeper tissue.

the same creature was observed drying its wings, feeding from clover, and releasing a faint powder when startled by a dog.

the caterpie line was finally complete in the formal record:

1796: caterpie documented during the green veil summer.

1797: metapod confirmed as caterpieâ€™s living chrysalis.

1802: butterfree confirmed as the winged adult stage after controlled emergence observation.

the villagers were not impressed.

they had known for generations.

what changed after 1802 was not local understanding, but official fear. once butterfree was proven to come from the same line that caused orchard infestations and silk-sealed homes, authorities realized the entire cycle had to be managed as one ecological event. killing caterpie reduced metapod numbers. disturbing metapod reduced butterfree emergence. reducing butterfree harmed pollination, which weakened orchards, which made remaining trees more vulnerable to the next caterpie outbreak.

the species was not a pest.

it was a cycle that punished anyone who only understood one stage.

butterfreeâ€™s origin is now explained as the final solution to caterpie vulnerability. the larval stage eats aggressively because it must gather enough energy to remake itself. the metapod stage trades mobility for protection during reconstruction. butterfree emerges light, winged, and chemically defended, able to disperse across wide areas and locate flowering sites through extraordinary vision and scent.

its powder likely began as loose wing scales that irritated predators. over time, those scales became chemically active. some caused numbness. some caused drowsiness. some confused predators long enough for escape. in modern butterfree, these powders vary by individual and condition. a relaxed specimen may shed almost harmless dust. a stressed one may produce enough compound to incapacitate a small room.

this explains why butterfree was both loved and feared in old communities.

it saved orchards.

it haunted sickrooms.

it pollinated graves.

it turned battlefields into flower meadows.

it made children smile and widows close their windows.

one of the earliest post-1802 studies recorded butterfree gathering over a mass burial site from a winter fever outbreak. the official explanation was simple: wildflowers had grown heavily in the disturbed soil, attracting pollinators. the field notes include another detail. the butterfree did not feed at first. they circled the site in widening spirals, then settled on the grave markers in rows.

no one knew what to do with that observation.

it was removed from the published version.

local tradition kept it.

in some villages, butterfree wings became symbols of release. empty metapod shells were placed near graves. butterfree carvings were hung in rooms where someone had died after long illness. during spring, families opened windows briefly at dawn to â€œlet the winged ones carry the name out.â€ this was not official religion. it was field grief, built from years of watching something soft crawl away, seal itself, vanish, and return changed.

the darker origin theories focus on the powderâ€™s effect on humans. some researchers argue that butterfree unintentionally shaped mourning rituals because its compounds made people near death seem calmer, more accepting, or more dreamlike. families exposed to low doses around sickbeds may have interpreted chemical sedation as spiritual peace. others suggest butterfree gathered around funerary flowers so often that humans began associating the species with souls, transformation, and afterlife passage.

both theories can be true.

nature does not become less sacred because chemistry is involved.

carrowâ€™s final writing on butterfree was less dismissive than his early work. he described the species as â€œthe gentle adult of a destructive childhood, carrying on its wings both apology and warning.â€ his private journal was stranger. in his final years, he kept the empty shell from the first fence-rail metapod beside a pressed butterfree wing found after the second veil bloom.

under them he wrote:

â€œthe forest does not forgive. it transforms.â€

modern field guides describe butterfree as a valuable pollinator, a beginner-friendly evolved bug-type, and a powder hazard requiring ventilation and eye protection. all of that is true.

but old kanto families remember more.

they remember sealed houses.

they remember clicking shells.

they remember pale wings at windows where fever burned.

they remember waking after a swarm passed with tears on their faces and no memory of why they had been crying.

butterfree is the final mercy of the caterpie line.

mercy, in nature, is not the same as safety.",

name: Caterpie
route: Viridian Forest, Route 2, Route 24, Route 25, forest-edge farms, mulberry districts, wet hedgerows, orchard borders, and abandoned rooflines near heavy leaf growth
habitat: Humid broadleaf forests, orchards, mulberry rows, brushy paths, nursery trees, rafters near warm stored cloth, and sheltered high places with fresh leaves and anchor points for silk
behaviorTemperament: Timid, food-driven, and avoidant alone. In groups it becomes a seasonal hazard through silk buildup, defensive odor signaling, and mass upward movement before evolution.
diet: Fresh leaves, soft shoots, tender foliage, young orchard growth, mulberry leaves, and moisture-rich plant matter
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular in mild weather, with heavy feeding after rain. Pre-evolution individuals climb higher near dusk, storms, or mass hardening events.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning after wet nights, beneath leaf lace, near silk bridges, along shaded hedgerows, or above doors and rafters during heavy Caterpie years
observationDifficulty: Easy to identify but easy to underestimate. Green camouflage, small size, and overhead silk make large groups more dangerous than single specimens.
conservationStatus: Common protected wild bug species
populationStatus: Stable to outbreak-prone. Local populations can surge after wet springs, logging, storm damage, crop failure, fire regrowth, or abandoned buildings near forest edges.
description: A small green larval Bug-type Pokemon with suction-pad feet, a red scent antenna, voracious feeding habits, and silk production that becomes hazardous when many individuals gather before evolution.
lore:
Caterpie is the first thing children are allowed to catch.

That should tell you how little adults understand forests.

It looks harmless because it is small. It moves slowly, eats leaves, clings to branches, and raises its red antenna when frightened. It does not have claws worth fearing. It does not have teeth meant for meat. It does not stalk humans, break fences, burn fields, or drag livestock into water.

Caterpie survives by being overlooked.

That is its true defense.

A single Caterpie is almost pathetic. Its body is soft enough that a careless boot can end it. Bird PokÃ©mon eat them by the dozen. Rattata raid their nesting leaves. Children scoop them into jars. Farmers brush them from crops. Trainers catch them early because they seem like living practice.

But Caterpie is not dangerous as one creature.

Caterpie is dangerous as a season.

The first sign is leaf lace: wide green leaves chewed into delicate veins, still hanging from trees like tiny skeletons. Then comes the silk. Thin white threads appear between branches, fence posts, roof beams, window latches, and tool handles. At first the threads seem harmless. Dew catches on them in the morning. Children run sticks through them. Farmers complain and wipe them away.

By the third week, doors begin sticking.

By the fourth, the barn rafters look wrapped for burial.

Caterpie silk is stronger than it appears. Young specimens use it to anchor themselves, slow falls, block predators, and prepare for evolution. When many gather in one place, their silk becomes a creeping architecture. It binds leaves into tents, seals hollows, suspends eggs, and turns ordinary forest edges into soft green nurseries.

Small animals caught in fresh silk usually escape.

Old silk is different.

It tightens when wet. It shrinks in cold dawn air. It clings to fur, feathers, eyelashes, fingers, and breathing mouths. Rangers have found dead Pidgey hanging upside down in Caterpie trees, not killed by venom or hunger, but by panic. The bird struggled, tangled its own wings, and exhausted itself while dozens of soft green larvae fed quietly around it on leaves.

Caterpie did not kill the bird.

The silk did.

That is how Caterpie survives. It makes the world slightly harder to move through until predators begin dying by accident.

Its red antenna is another misunderstood feature. Children think it is cute. Field handlers know better. When threatened, Caterpie can release a sharp, bitter odor from the antenna and body surface. In small doses it repels birds and small mammals. In enclosed spaces, it causes nausea, watering eyes, headaches, confusion, and a crawling sensation under the skin. Old farm records describe families waking at night convinced insects were moving through their bedding, only to find Caterpie clustered above the rafters, antennae raised, silently filling the room with defensive musk.

The smell was once called green rot.

It does not smell like death.

It smells like leaves dying before autumn.

Caterpie infestations are especially feared in nurseries, grain lofts, schoolhouses, and sickrooms. Not because the PokÃ©mon want to hurt people, but because they seek warmth, quiet, and high places before molting. In old Kanto villages, winter blankets were beaten outside before use because Caterpie sometimes nested in folded cloth. Several historical suffocation cases involved infants wrapped in blankets that had been threaded internally with silk. The silk was almost invisible until it tightened.

After those incidents, rural midwives began checking swaddling cloth by candlelight.

If the flame bent toward the cloth without wind, they burned it.

Caterpie are also known for following grief.

This is not supernatural, despite what old stories claim. Houses in mourning often leave food untouched, windows closed, bedding undisturbed, and lamps burning late. Those conditions attract small insects, then Caterpie, then more silk. Still, the pattern created folklore. People began saying Caterpie came to measure the dead.

In some villages, if a Caterpie was found on a windowsill after a funeral, the family would not remove it. They believed it was counting the days before the soul changed shape.

That belief came from the way Caterpie prepare to evolve.

A Caterpie near evolution becomes restless. It climbs higher than normal. It stops feeding. Its skin dulls. Its silk output increases. It begins fastening itself to surfaces with obsessive care, as if terrified of falling before the transformation begins. A healthy Caterpie will choose bark, stone, beam, or branch.

A stressed Caterpie may choose anything warm.

Backpacks.

Horse tack.

Bedposts.

A sleeping personâ€™s hair.

There are documented cases of Caterpie attaching themselves to humans during overnight forest travel. The victim wakes with silk in their collar, behind the ear, or threaded between fingers. Removing the Caterpie too roughly can rupture its soft body, coating the skin in irritant fluid. Leaving it in place risks the beginning of Metapod hardening against flesh or clothing.

That is why rangers teach new trainers not to sleep beneath chewed leaves.

Caterpie is not malicious. That matters. It is one of the least cruel PokÃ©mon in the field record. It does not hate, plot, stalk, or punish. It wants only to eat, hide, climb, and become something else.

But there is horror in that innocence.

A Caterpie swarm can strip an orchard overnight.

A Caterpie nursery can turn a farmhouse into a silk trap.

A single frightened Caterpie can blind a child long enough for them to fall into a creek.

A dozen evolving Caterpie can seal a corpse so cleanly that the family mistakes the white wrapping for burial linen.

The old field rule is gentle but firm:

Do not crush Caterpie without reason.

Do not let them nest above where people sleep.

Do not ignore silk indoors.

If one climbs onto you, remove it calmly before sunset.

If many climb upward at once, leave the grove before they begin fastening themselves.

And if the trees go quiet except for chewing, cover your mouth and do not look up with open eyes.
survivalUse: Can indicate fresh leaf growth, nearby forest edges, humidity shifts, coming mass evolution, and potential bird-predator activity. Silk can be used as emergency thread only after safe collection and drying.
handlingWarning: Do not crush unless necessary, do not trap in closed rooms, and do not allow clusters above bedding, cradles, stored blankets, or food stores. Defensive odor can irritate eyes and cause nausea in enclosed air.
encounterAdvice: Approach calmly from the side, avoid touching the red antenna, and lift with a leaf or soft brush rather than bare fingers. If many individuals are climbing upward at once, leave the grove before fastening begins.
firstAid: For antenna musk exposure, move to fresh air, rinse eyes with clean water, and wash skin. For silk near mouth or nose, remove carefully without pulling against skin. Seek care for breathing trouble or severe eye irritation.
companionSuitability: Beginner-friendly only as a single supervised specimen; poor for homes with infants, enclosed sleeping spaces, or unmanaged indoor plants
fieldBehaviors: Chews wide leaves into lace-like veins
Anchors itself with silk before molting
Climbs higher before storms or evolution
Clusters in warm rafters, cloth folds, hedges, and nursery trees
Responds to nearby alarm odor from disturbed Caterpie
resistances: Mild falls when anchored by silk
Small predator bites when odor is released
signsOfPresence: Tiny suction tracks on bark or painted wood
equipment: Lantern or flashlight to reveal silk
fieldNote: A lone Caterpie is usually a low threat. Fresh silk indoors, climbing groups, green rot odor, or leaf lace spreading toward homes means the season is turning into a nursery.
originStory:
Caterpie was formally recorded in 1796, making it one of the earliest Bug-type PokÃ©mon accepted into Kanto natural history.

This early date does not mean Caterpie was rare or newly discovered. Quite the opposite. Caterpie had lived beside humans for so long that most communities did not consider it worth scientific attention. It was a garden pest, a childâ€™s first catch, a birdâ€™s meal, a silk nuisance, and a seasonal fact of life.

Science ignored it because everyone already knew it.

That changed during the Green Veil Summer of 1796.

The eastern Kanto mulberry districts had suffered a warm, wet spring followed by a sudden increase in leaf-eating larvae. Farmers expected crop loss. They had dealt with Caterpie before. What they did not expect was coordination.

Across several villages, Caterpie gathered in numbers too large to dismiss as ordinary infestation. They stripped mulberry rows, sealed irrigation gates with silk, nested inside thatched roofs, and moved along hedgerows in slow green bands. Children were sent to collect them in baskets. The children came back with their sleeves webbed shut.

At first, the infestation was treated as an agricultural problem.

Then the first house was found sealed.

The family inside survived, but barely. Silk had thickened over the shutters, chimney screen, and door latch during the night. The household woke to foul air, green odor, and faint scratching in the rafters. When neighbors cut them free, hundreds of Caterpie dropped from the roof beams like soft fruit.

No one had seen them enter.

That event drew the attention of naturalist Iven Carrow, who traveled to the district expecting to document an unusual caterpillar plague. His first notes are almost dismissive. He described the PokÃ©mon as â€œsimple leaf-feeding larvae of no great intelligence, notable only for their red scent organ and excessive thread production.â€

Within two weeks, he changed his tone.

Carrow observed that Caterpie were not acting as one mind, but they were responding to shared conditions so efficiently that they appeared organized. They followed humidity gradients. They gathered where leaves were most nutritious. They climbed before storms. They silked over gaps that exposed them to bird attack. When disturbed in one tree, nearby groups raised their antennae minutes later, suggesting chemical warning signals carried through the air.

Most important, he discovered that the silk was not merely for defense.

It was preparation.

The Green Veil Summer was not only an infestation. It was a mass pre-evolution event.

Caterpie were feeding at extreme rates because thousands were nearing transformation at once. They were not trying to destroy villages. They were trying to build enough safe surfaces to become Metapod.

This discovery led to the first accepted Caterpie record in 1796, followed by an emergency study of the hardened forms appearing throughout the district. Metapod would be formally recognized the following year, not because it had never been seen before, but because people had assumed it was a separate shell insect rather than Caterpieâ€™s next stage.

The early Caterpie-line chronology began there:

1796: Caterpie formally recorded during the Green Veil Summer.

1797: Metapod confirmed after marked Caterpie hardened into living chrysalis forms.

1802: Butterfree formally linked to the line after mass emergence from tagged Metapod shells.

Caterpieâ€™s deeper origin remains debated. The most accepted theory is that it descended from ancient forest larvae that survived by accelerating growth in dangerous environments. Instead of becoming heavily armored or venomous, early Caterpie ancestors specialized in three things: rapid feeding, silk production, and sensory warning. This made them vulnerable as individuals but successful as populations.

Their green coloration allowed them to vanish among leaves.

Their antenna odor discouraged small predators.

Their silk let them reshape unsafe spaces into temporary nurseries.

Their true evolutionary advantage was not strength.

It was becoming something else quickly enough that predators could not eat them all.

This explains why Caterpie populations explode after storms, fires, logging, war, abandonment, and crop failure. Disturbed environments create new growth. New growth feeds larvae. Larvae create silk shelters. Silk shelters increase survival. The result is a sudden wave of transformation that can overwhelm any settlement careless enough to build directly against forest edge.

Old Kanto farmers understood this before scientists did.

They left buffer strips between houses and mulberry rows. They kept rafters smoked. They stored blankets in cedar chests. They taught children to carry Caterpie gently away from doors, not kill them, because crushed Caterpie attracted more insects and sometimes triggered stronger odor release from the swarm.

Most importantly, they watched the direction of the climb.

If Caterpie climbed outward toward the forest, the year would be normal.

If Caterpie climbed inward toward houses, the village had built too close to the nursery.

One of Carrowâ€™s assistants recorded a final superstition from the Green Veil villages. During heavy Caterpie years, families placed small green cords on cradles and door handles. These were called little promises. They symbolized a request that Caterpie pass over the household without fastening it into the seasonâ€™s change.

Carrow mocked the custom.

Then he found one of his specimen cabinets sealed shut with silk from the inside.

After that, he stopped mocking local practices in his notes.

Caterpie remains one of the most common and underestimated PokÃ©mon in the Kanto field record. Its body is soft. Its life is brief. Its mind is simple. But it represents a truth many trainers forget:

Not every dangerous creature looks dangerous.

Some are dangerous because they are small, hungry, afraid, and numerous.

Some are dangerous because the world changes around them before anyone notices.

Caterpie is not the monster in the forest.

Caterpie is the warning that the forest has begun preparing the room.

evolutionLine.name: Caterpie
evolutionLine.note: Leaf-feeding larval stage. Soft, fast-growing, and swarm-dependent. Silk output increases before hardening into Metapod.

evolutionLine.name: Metapod
evolutionLine.note: Living chrysalis stage. Motion is surrendered for shell defense while the body reorganizes toward winged emergence.

evolutionLine.name: Butterfree
evolutionLine.note: Winged adult pollinator and powder hazard. Its scales can carry sleep, paralysis, poison, and confusion risk in enclosed air.

mainLine.name: Caterpie
mainLine.note: Soft larval form. Eats leaves aggressively, climbs with suction-pad feet, releases defensive odor, and builds silk before hardening.

mainLine.name: Metapod
mainLine.note: Armored chrysalis. Its shell protects a soft, vulnerable internal body that is rebuilding itself for emergence.

mainLine.name: Butterfree
mainLine.note: Final winged pollinator. Locates nectar at great distance and sheds toxic, sleep-inducing, or stunning powders when stressed.

target.name: Gigantamax Butterfree
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat scale output, wind shear, and toxic powder dispersal as disaster-scale hazards.

domesticationStatus: Commonly caught but poorly suited for unsupervised indoor keeping
prey: Leaves, shoots, orchard foliage, mulberry growth, and soft garden plants
territoryRange: Usually a few trees, one hedgerow, or a small orchard edge; outbreak seasons can spread across entire village forest borders
lifeCycle: Hatches among leaves, feeds rapidly, molts repeatedly, produces silk, and evolves into Metapod starting at Level 7 when enough stored energy and safe anchoring surface are available.
containmentRequirements: Ventilated terrarium, fresh unsprayed leaves, climb-safe branches, removable silk anchors, low crowding, no cloth bedding, and daily inspection for pre-evolution climbing behavior
ecologicalRole: Consumes excess foliage, feeds birds and small predators, creates silk nursery structures, and supplies the first stage of a pollinating adult line.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Silk can trap dust and spores; crowded larvae may carry mites, mold, or irritant residue from defensive musk.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are laid on leaves and sheltered foliage by adult Butterfree. Larvae gather where food, humidity, and silk anchor points are plentiful.
predators: Pidgey, Spearow, Rattata, Weedle, larger insects, small mammals, children collecting carelessly, and farm pest-control measures
emergencyResponse: For indoor silk buildup, ventilate, protect eyes and mouth, cut silk instead of pulling it, relocate Caterpie gently, and inspect bedding, rafters, shutters, and stored cloth before reuse.
legalStatus: Generally legal for novice trainers, but large indoor infestations may require ranger removal in settled areas
lifespan: Wild larval stage is brief, usually days to weeks before evolution or predation. Managed individuals may remain larval longer if growth conditions are poor.
socialStructure: Loose feeding clusters without true leadership. Groups appear coordinated because individuals respond to humidity, food density, vibration, and chemical warning signals.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Hazard comes from silk entanglement, defensive odor, and swarm density.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Odor and silk hazard is usually within 3 to 15 ft of clusters, farther in enclosed rafters.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Slow crawl; faster escape climbing on bark and walls using suction-pad feet
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Poor on open ground, better when hidden in foliage
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Cannot jump meaningfully; can drop while anchored by silk
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent clinging on leaves, walls, bark, and overhead beams

trainingRisk: Eye irritation, nausea from defensive musk, accidental crushing, indoor silk hazards, and mishandled pre-evolution attachment
physiology: Caterpie has a soft green body, suction-pad feet, a red defensive scent organ, and silk glands used for anchoring, slowing falls, protecting nursery sites, and preparing for Metapod hardening.

keyFacts.tailLength: No tail; compact larval body about 1 ft high
keyFacts.habitat: Humid forests, orchards, hedgerows, and warm rafters
keyFacts.lifespan: Brief larval stage before Level 7 evolution or predation
keyFacts.groupSize: Single specimens to seasonal swarms

domesticationDifficulty: Low for a single specimen, high for groups. The main challenge is preventing silk buildup, indoor nesting, and unsafe Metapod attachment.
physicalCharacteristics: Small green segmented body, cream underside, red Y-shaped antenna, black eyes, many suction-tipped feet, soft skin, and hidden silk glands.
dietRestrictions: Avoid pesticide-treated leaves, dry stale foliage, moldy greens, enclosed jars without ventilation, and high heat that dehydrates the body before evolution.

  "observationdifficulty": "easy to identify but easy to underestimate. green camouflage, small size, and overhead silk make large groups more dangerous than single specimens.",

  "populationstatus": "stable to outbreak-prone. local populations can surge after wet springs, logging, storm damage, crop failure, fire regrowth, or abandoned buildings near forest edges.",
  "description": "a small green larval bug-type pokemon with suction-pad feet, a red scent antenna, voracious feeding habits, and silk production that becomes hazardous when many individuals gather before evolution.",
  "lore": "caterpie is the first thing children are allowed to catch.

that should tell you how little adults understand forests.

it looks harmless because it is small. it moves slowly, eats leaves, clings to branches, and raises its red antenna when frightened. it does not have claws worth fearing. it does not have teeth meant for meat. it does not stalk humans, break fences, burn fields, or drag livestock into water.

caterpie survives by being overlooked.

that is its true defense.

a single caterpie is almost pathetic. its body is soft enough that a careless boot can end it. bird pokã©mon eat them by the dozen. rattata raid their nesting leaves. children scoop them into jars. farmers brush them from crops. trainers catch them early because they seem like living practice.

but caterpie is not dangerous as one creature.

caterpie is dangerous as a season.

the first sign is leaf lace: wide green leaves chewed into delicate veins, still hanging from trees like tiny skeletons. then comes the silk. thin white threads appear between branches, fence posts, roof beams, window latches, and tool handles. at first the threads seem harmless. dew catches on them in the morning. children run sticks through them. farmers complain and wipe them away.

by the third week, doors begin sticking.

by the fourth, the barn rafters look wrapped for burial.

caterpie silk is stronger than it appears. young specimens use it to anchor themselves, slow falls, block predators, and prepare for evolution. when many gather in one place, their silk becomes a creeping architecture. it binds leaves into tents, seals hollows, suspends eggs, and turns ordinary forest edges into soft green nurseries.

small animals caught in fresh silk usually escape.

old silk is different.

it tightens when wet. it shrinks in cold dawn air. it clings to fur, feathers, eyelashes, fingers, and breathing mouths. rangers have found dead pidgey hanging upside down in caterpie trees, not killed by venom or hunger, but by panic. the bird struggled, tangled its own wings, and exhausted itself while dozens of soft green larvae fed quietly around it on leaves.

caterpie did not kill the bird.

the silk did.

that is how caterpie survives. it makes the world slightly harder to move through until predators begin dying by accident.

its red antenna is another misunderstood feature. children think it is cute. field handlers know better. when threatened, caterpie can release a sharp, bitter odor from the antenna and body surface. in small doses it repels birds and small mammals. in enclosed spaces, it causes nausea, watering eyes, headaches, confusion, and a crawling sensation under the skin. old farm records describe families waking at night convinced insects were moving through their bedding, only to find caterpie clustered above the rafters, antennae raised, silently filling the room with defensive musk.

the smell was once called green rot.

it does not smell like death.

it smells like leaves dying before autumn.

caterpie infestations are especially feared in nurseries, grain lofts, schoolhouses, and sickrooms. not because the pokã©mon want to hurt people, but because they seek warmth, quiet, and high places before molting. in old kanto villages, winter blankets were beaten outside before use because caterpie sometimes nested in folded cloth. several historical suffocation cases involved infants wrapped in blankets that had been threaded internally with silk. the silk was almost invisible until it tightened.

after those incidents, rural midwives began checking swaddling cloth by candlelight.

if the flame bent toward the cloth without wind, they burned it.

caterpie are also known for following grief.

this is not supernatural, despite what old stories claim. houses in mourning often leave food untouched, windows closed, bedding undisturbed, and lamps burning late. those conditions attract small insects, then caterpie, then more silk. still, the pattern created folklore. people began saying caterpie came to measure the dead.

in some villages, if a caterpie was found on a windowsill after a funeral, the family would not remove it. they believed it was counting the days before the soul changed shape.

that belief came from the way caterpie prepare to evolve.

a caterpie near evolution becomes restless. it climbs higher than normal. it stops feeding. its skin dulls. its silk output increases. it begins fastening itself to surfaces with obsessive care, as if terrified of falling before the transformation begins. a healthy caterpie will choose bark, stone, beam, or branch.

a stressed caterpie may choose anything warm.

backpacks.

horse tack.

bedposts.

a sleeping personâ€™s hair.

there are documented cases of caterpie attaching themselves to humans during overnight forest travel. the victim wakes with silk in their collar, behind the ear, or threaded between fingers. removing the caterpie too roughly can rupture its soft body, coating the skin in irritant fluid. leaving it in place risks the beginning of metapod hardening against flesh or clothing.

that is why rangers teach new trainers not to sleep beneath chewed leaves.

caterpie is not malicious. that matters. it is one of the least cruel pokã©mon in the field record. it does not hate, plot, stalk, or punish. it wants only to eat, hide, climb, and become something else.

but there is horror in that innocence.

a caterpie swarm can strip an orchard overnight.

a caterpie nursery can turn a farmhouse into a silk trap.

a single frightened caterpie can blind a child long enough for them to fall into a creek.

a dozen evolving caterpie can seal a corpse so cleanly that the family mistakes the white wrapping for burial linen.

the old field rule is gentle but firm:

do not crush caterpie without reason.

do not let them nest above where people sleep.

do not ignore silk indoors.

if one climbs onto you, remove it calmly before sunset.

if many climb upward at once, leave the grove before they begin fastening themselves.

and if the trees go quiet except for chewing, cover your mouth and do not look up with open eyes.",

  "originstory": "caterpie was formally recorded in 1796, making it one of the earliest bug-type pokã©mon accepted into kanto natural history.

science ignored it because everyone already knew it.

that changed during the green veil summer of 1796.

the eastern kanto mulberry districts had suffered a warm, wet spring followed by a sudden increase in leaf-eating larvae. farmers expected crop loss. they had dealt with caterpie before. what they did not expect was coordination.

across several villages, caterpie gathered in numbers too large to dismiss as ordinary infestation. they stripped mulberry rows, sealed irrigation gates with silk, nested inside thatched roofs, and moved along hedgerows in slow green bands. children were sent to collect them in baskets. the children came back with their sleeves webbed shut.

at first, the infestation was treated as an agricultural problem.

then the first house was found sealed.

the family inside survived, but barely. silk had thickened over the shutters, chimney screen, and door latch during the night. the household woke to foul air, green odor, and faint scratching in the rafters. when neighbors cut them free, hundreds of caterpie dropped from the roof beams like soft fruit.

no one had seen them enter.

that event drew the attention of naturalist iven carrow, who traveled to the district expecting to document an unusual caterpillar plague. his first notes are almost dismissive. he described the pokã©mon as â€œsimple leaf-feeding larvae of no great intelligence, notable only for their red scent organ and excessive thread production.â€

within two weeks, he changed his tone.

carrow observed that caterpie were not acting as one mind, but they were responding to shared conditions so efficiently that they appeared organized. they followed humidity gradients. they gathered where leaves were most nutritious. they climbed before storms. they silked over gaps that exposed them to bird attack. when disturbed in one tree, nearby groups raised their antennae minutes later, suggesting chemical warning signals carried through the air.

most important, he discovered that the silk was not merely for defense.

it was preparation.

the green veil summer was not only an infestation. it was a mass pre-evolution event.

caterpie were feeding at extreme rates because thousands were nearing transformation at once. they were not trying to destroy villages. they were trying to build enough safe surfaces to become metapod.

this discovery led to the first accepted caterpie record in 1796, followed by an emergency study of the hardened forms appearing throughout the district. metapod would be formally recognized the following year, not because it had never been seen before, but because people had assumed it was a separate shell insect rather than caterpieâ€™s next stage.

the early caterpie-line chronology began there:

1797: metapod confirmed after marked caterpie hardened into living chrysalis forms.

1802: butterfree formally linked to the line after mass emergence from tagged metapod shells.

name: Cattail
route: Ponds, marshes, ditches, lake edges
habitat: Wetlands, marshes, slow-moving water, and muddy pond edges.
weight: Varies by stalk and root mass
behaviorTemperament: Stationary plant. Usually found in dense stands near water.
diet: Photosynthetic plant. Requires wet soil or shallow water.
activityPattern: Seasonal growth. Most visible during warm months.
bestTimeToFind: Spring through fall near wetlands and pond edges.
observationDifficulty: Low, but safe use depends on correct identification.
conservationStatus: Common in many wetland areas.
description: A wetland plant commonly found near ponds, marshes, and slow-moving water.
lore: A classic survival plant because it can signal water nearby and has multiple traditional field uses.
survivalUse: Can indicate nearby water. Some parts are traditionally used as emergency food when properly identified.
handlingWarning: Never eat wild plants unless identification is certain. Avoid plants growing in polluted water.
encounterAdvice: Use as a water indicator, but check the water source for pollution, fuel, algae blooms, or runoff.
firstAid: If a wild plant causes nausea, burning, swelling, rash, or breathing issues, stop exposure and seek medical help.
companionSuitability: Not applicable.
fieldNote: Never eat wild plants unless you are fully confident in the identification.

name: Charizard
route: Mountain nests, volcanic ridges, high cliffs, old fortress ruins, and remote burned valleys
habitat: High warm terrain with strong updrafts, cave roosts, open flight lanes, and limited human access
behaviorTemperament: Independent, proud, and highly territorial. It may ignore small prey while actively challenging strong creatures or intruders near its roost.
diet: Large mammals, birds, fish, carrion, high-fat prey, charred vegetation, and managed high-protein feed
activityPattern: Diurnal flight and dusk hunting. Roosts during storms and extreme cold.
bestTimeToFind: From far away at sunrise near cliff roosts when warm air begins rising
observationDifficulty: High. Easy to spot in flight, hard to survive if too close to a nesting adult.
conservationStatus: Strictly protected apex species with public safety restrictions
populationStatus: Rare. Stable only in remote protected ranges.
description: A large flying fire predator capable of sustained flight, intense flame projection, and territorial attacks. In survival terms, Charizard is not a campfire helper. It is an aerial apex hazard.
lore: Ancient settlements painted Charizard silhouettes over gates to warn travelers which valleys had no law after sunset.
survivalUse: Can provide aerial evacuation, predator deterrence, signal fire, and heat in extreme emergencies. The risk of attracting or causing disaster is severe.
handlingWarning: Never approach a roost, egg, injured wing, or feeding adult. Do not shine lights into its eyes at night.
encounterAdvice: Do not stand in open ground. Move under rock cover, stay low, avoid smoke plumes, and leave the territory without running toward cliffs.
firstAid: For burns, cool immediately and seek emergency care. For bite or claw wounds, control bleeding and treat as contaminated trauma. For smoke exposure, move to clean air.
companionSuitability: Poor for homes. Possible working partnership only with elite handlers and protected range access.
resistances: Ground immunity in game type logic
fieldNote: If a Charizard circles without attacking, it may be measuring wind, escape paths, and whether you are worth the energy.
originStory: The Charizard line appears to have evolved from heat-cave predators that traded camouflage for fire. Its darkest adaptation is psychological: a healthy adult often refuses weak prey, seeking stronger opponents because combat itself selects mates and territory.

evolutionLine.name: Charizard
evolutionLine.note: Charizard does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms should be treated as temporary high-risk phenomena.

mainLine.name: Charizard
mainLine.note: Charizard does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms should be treated as temporary high-risk phenomena.

variants.method: Special catalyst, battle phenomenon, or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Charizard X; Mega Charizard Y; Gigantamax Charizard
target.note: Charizard does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms should be treated as temporary high-risk phenomena.

domesticationStatus: Not domesticated. Historical riding and military use required heavy training and high casualty risk.
prey: Large mammals, fish, birds, smaller Pokemon, livestock, and carrion
territoryRange: 20 to 100 square miles depending on prey, cliffs, and thermal lift
lifeCycle: Final standard form of the Charmander line after Charmeleon reaches level 36. May access Mega or Gigantamax forms under rare special conditions.
containmentRequirements: A managed flight range with stone roosts, firebreaks, remote feeding systems, veterinary restraint bay, and aviation hazard coordination
ecologicalRole: Apex aerial predator, carrion remover, fire-cycle driver in adapted scrublands, and population control for large herbivores
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Burn wounds, respiratory soot, wing mites, salmonella-like reptile bacteria, and carrion-borne pathogens may affect handlers.
reproductionNesting: Nests on cliffs, lava-warmed stone, or high ruins. Eggs require warmth, ventilation, and long guarding periods.
predators: Few natural predators. Rival Charizard, large rock-types, severe wing injury, disease, and humans are primary threats.
emergencyResponse: Treat as wildfire plus large predator incident. Evacuate downwind, notify fire and wildlife teams, and do not attempt capture without aerial support.
legalStatus: Restricted apex fire-flying Pokemon. Private ownership usually prohibited or heavily regulated.
lifespan: Wild: 15 to 30 years. Managed sanctuary: 40 years or more.
socialStructure: Usually solitary. May form temporary breeding pair or loose dominance network around rich territory.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Fire output is extreme and capable of structural ignition.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Flame hazard 50 ft or more, smoke and wildfire hazard far beyond line of sight
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast ground lunge and high-speed takeoff
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High in air, moderate on ground
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Wing-assisted launch from cliffs or open ground
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent in air, tail flame affects tight ground turns

trainingRisk: Fatal burns, aerial drops, crushing bites, wing-strike trauma, wildfire, and dominance attacks
physiology: Charizard uses powerful lungs, internal fuel chemistry, and a heat-resistant throat to project flame. Its wings demand high calories, and injured adults can become desperate near livestock, camps, and roads.

keyFacts.habitat: Mountains, volcanic cliffs, remote highlands
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 15 to 30 years. Sanctuary: 40 years or more.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary, temporary pairs only

domesticationDifficulty: Extreme. Requires aviation-scale space, fire control, and specialist handlers.
physicalCharacteristics: Orange dragon-like body, cream underside, broad wings, horned head, heavy tail flame, powerful jaws, large claws, and heat-hardened hide. Wing membranes are vulnerable to tears despite the animal strength.
dietRestrictions: Avoid low-calorie feeding, confinement without flight, wet cold roosts, and overuse of flame in enclosed areas.

name: Charmander
route: Dry foothills, volcanic scrub, sun-warmed caves, rocky badlands, abandoned urban-edge shelters, and old charcoal pits
habitat: Warm rocky shelters with dry bedding, low humidity, airflow, access to small prey, ash nests, and protected cave mouths away from heavy rain
behaviorTemperament: Social, loyal, and curious when raised young. Wild Charmander are nervous pack juveniles that warn with tail flicks, hisses, short flame bursts, and sudden retreat. Cornered juveniles may panic-burn, especially near nests or shelter chambers.
diet: Insects, small rodents, lizards, eggs, berries, charred plant matter, and high-energy protein meals
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular. Basks in morning sun and shelters during cold rain.
bestTimeToFind: Warm mornings near cave mouths, ash beds, abandoned shelters, sunlit rocks, or old charcoal pits marked by small claw scratches
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Tail flame is visible at night, but wild juveniles hide quickly and den sites are often mistaken for old fire damage.
conservationStatus: Protected starter-class fire species
populationStatus: Uncommon and declining near human development due to illegal capture, fire-control conflicts, den destruction, and fear after early violent encounters
description: A small juvenile fire-bearing saurian Pokemon first mistaken for a moving torch or fire shadow in Kanto's dry foothills. Its tail flame reflects health, stress, and stored fuel, making the animal both easy to notice and dangerous to mishandle.
lore: Before Charmander was officially cataloged as PKMN-0004, rural Kanto settlements spoke of fire shadows moving between old charcoal shelters, volcanic scrub, and dry cave mouths after sundown. Charcoal burners and night travelers described small orange shapes with lantern-like tails, but most reports were dismissed as heat shimmer, unattended embers, or superstition. The first confirmed encounter occurred during a late 20th century Kanto field survey near abandoned urban-edge shelters built into a burned hillside. Investigators found ash bedding, cracked eggshell fragments, warm claw marks, and a chamber that held heat long after the surrounding stone had cooled. When the team disturbed the rear den, several juveniles fled, but one wild Charmander remained cornered beside the clutch site. A handler mistook the tail flame for an external fire and tried to smother it. The Charmander panicked, its flame flashed white-blue, and the shelter ignited. The violent first contact injured several people and proved that the flame was not a carried torch, but a living part of the Pokemon tied to survival, fear, and health. Modern starter programs grew out of this failure: never smother the flame, never corner the animal, and never forget that the small fire lizard in front of you is the first stage of an apex line.
survivalUse: Can provide heat, ignite emergency fire, deter small predators, signal at night, and reveal dry shelter routes. Must never be used inside low-ventilation shelter.
handlingWarning: Never cover the tail flame with a blanket, dirt, foam, or water. Never carry near fuel, dry grass, tents, aerosol cans, or stored charcoal. Smothering attempts can trigger panic ignition.
encounterAdvice: Approach from the front at low height. Offer space, keep hands away from the tail, and avoid water spray. If the tail flame flares white-blue or the animal curls around eggs, retreat and clear dry fuel from the area.
firstAid: Cool burns for 20 minutes with clean water. Do not apply grease. For smoke inhalation, move to clean air and seek care. Tail flame collapse is veterinary emergency.
companionSuitability: Good for careful handlers with fire-safe housing
fieldBehaviors: Panics when the tail flame is grabbed or smothered
signsOfPresence: Cracked eggshell fragments near warm stone
equipment: Water for humans, not for tail flame
fieldNote: A steady orange tail flame usually means calm health. A sputtering flame means illness, exhaustion, or wet conditions. A sudden white-blue flare means fear and has historically preceded shelter fires during first-contact events.
originStory: The Charmander line likely began as small geothermal cave reptiles living along Kanto's volcanic foothills, dry badlands, and charcoal-rich shelters. Cold nights, damp caves, nest predators, and competition for warm den space favored juveniles that could produce internal heat. Early tail-flame mutations gave hatchlings warmth, light, and a defensive warning, but also made them visible to predators and humans. Survival therefore favored social juveniles, guarded ash nests, flame-resistant skin, strong heat regulation, and panic fire as a last defense. Charmander evolves into Charmeleon starting at Level 16 because the juvenile body must become large enough to hunt alone, defend den corridors, and survive drought competition. Charmeleon evolves into Charizard starting at Level 36 because flight is the line's final answer to ground-level threats: floods, cave collapse, rival predators, human expansion, and loss of lowland dens. The result is an evolutionary path from vulnerable fire lizard, to violent adolescent territory fighter, to aerial apex predator.

evolutionLine.name: Charmander
evolutionLine.note: Juvenile fire-bearer. Tail flame is a health indicator and ignition source. Evolves under growth, stress, and heat-regulation maturity.

evolutionLine.name: Charmeleon
evolutionLine.note: Aggressive adolescent predator. Highest handler-conflict stage of the line, shaped by den competition and territorial pressure.

evolutionLine.name: Charizard
evolutionLine.note: Aerial apex predator. Flight solves ground-level threats, but roosts require wildfire and aviation-style safety planning.

mainLine.name: Charmander
mainLine.note: Juvenile fire-bearer. Small, social, and dependent on warm dens. Tail flame signals health and stress.

mainLine.name: Charmeleon
mainLine.note: Aggressive adolescent territory fighter. Built for den defense, drought competition, and solitary hunting.

mainLine.name: Charizard
mainLine.note: Final aerial apex predator. Flight adapts the line to floods, cave collapse, rival predators, and human expansion.

domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated under fire-type starter programs
prey: Insects, small mammals, carrion scraps, eggs, and roasted plant matter
territoryRange: Usually 1 to 3 square miles around den, water, basking rocks, and hunting lanes
lifeCycle: Hatches with a weak tail flame in a warm ash nest or geothermal pocket, grows through feeding and heat regulation, evolves into Charmeleon starting at Level 16, and later evolves into Charizard starting at Level 36 if it survives the dangerous adolescent stage.
containmentRequirements: Fireproof den, ceramic or stone flooring, spark screen, dry bedding placed away from the flame, ventilation, protected basking area, and Class ABC fire extinguisher nearby
ecologicalRole: Controls small pests, clears dry brush with low flame, recycles carrion in arid habitats, and maintains warm den pockets that may be reused by other fire-tolerant species.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Smoke irritation, scale mites, fuel-gland infection, and respiratory illness in damp housing are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are kept in warm ash nests, geothermal pockets, abandoned shelters, or protected charcoal pits. Adults or dominant pack members guard eggs from damp, predators, and human disturbance.
predators: Fearow, larger water-types, Arbok, humans, and stronger fire Pokemon competing for dens
emergencyResponse: For uncontrolled flame, remove fuel sources, guide Charmander to bare ground, lower light and noise, and cool nearby human burns with clean water without soaking the tail flame.
legalStatus: Fire-type handling permit recommended or required in many settled areas after early den-fire incidents and illegal capture pressure
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years before evolution or predation. Managed care: 12 to 20 years if kept healthy.
socialStructure: Pack juveniles shelter under a dominant adult, older Charmeleon, or guarded den hierarchy. Lone juveniles are more fearful and more likely to panic-burn when cornered.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Fire output is juvenile but capable of serious burns.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Flame and heat hazard about 3 to 10 ft, farther if dry grass ignites
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast short run about 12 to 18 mph
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Good juvenile agility
fieldCapabilities.balance: Tail improves balance but becomes liability in brush

trainingRisk: Accidental burns, panic ignition, smoke inhalation, fuel-gland strain, and tail injury
physiology: Charmander appears to generate flame through volatile biological fuel, oxygen intake, heat-resistant skin, and a specialized tail structure. The tail flame is not decorative. It functions as warmth, warning, defense, and a visible health indicator. If it weakens severely, the animal is in medical danger.

keyFacts.habitat: Dry rocky caves and warm badlands
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years. Managed: 12 to 20 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually small packs or guarded juveniles

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Strong bonding potential, but constant fire safety management is required. Wild-caught juveniles from den incidents are far more reactive than captive-raised starter lines.
physicalCharacteristics: Orange bipedal body, cream underside, blue eyes, blunt claws, long balancing tail, and a persistent open flame at the tail tip. Skin is smooth, warm, and resistant to brief heat exposure.
dietRestrictions: Avoid wet bedding, low-calorie diets, alcohol vapors, aerosol sprays, stored fuel, and overfeeding fatty scraps that can foul its fuel glands.
