  "description": "a small juvenile fire-bearing saurian pokemon first mistaken for a moving torch or fire shadow in kanto's dry foothills. its tail flame reflects health, stress, and stored fuel, making the animal both easy to notice and dangerous to mishandle.",
  "lore": "before charmander was officially cataloged as pkmn-0004, rural kanto settlements spoke of fire shadows moving between old charcoal shelters, volcanic scrub, and dry cave mouths after sundown. charcoal burners and night travelers described small orange shapes with lantern-like tails, but most reports were dismissed as heat shimmer, unattended embers, or superstition. the first confirmed encounter occurred during a late 20th century kanto field survey near abandoned urban-edge shelters built into a burned hillside. investigators found ash bedding, cracked eggshell fragments, warm claw marks, and a chamber that held heat long after the surrounding stone had cooled. when the team disturbed the rear den, several juveniles fled, but one wild charmander remained cornered beside the clutch site. a handler mistook the tail flame for an external fire and tried to smother it. the charmander panicked, its flame flashed white-blue, and the shelter ignited. the violent first contact injured several people and proved that the flame was not a carried torch, but a living part of the pokemon tied to survival, fear, and health. modern starter programs grew out of this failure: never smother the flame, never corner the animal, and never forget that the small fire lizard in front of you is the first stage of an apex line.",

name: Charmeleon
route: Dry canyons, lava-tube caves, burned pine slopes, abandoned mines, and isolated badland ridges
habitat: Hot dry terrain with high perches, hunting corridors, and den sites shielded from rain
behaviorTemperament: Proud, irritable, territorial, and easily provoked. It may seek fights to establish rank and may ignore a handler it once obeyed as a Charmander.
diet: Small mammals, birds, eggs, reptiles, carrion, charred plant matter, and high-protein managed feed
activityPattern: Late afternoon, evening, and night hunting with daytime basking
bestTimeToFind: At dusk near warm rocks, cave exits, and fresh scorch trails
observationDifficulty: Hard and dangerous. Charmeleon often detects observers first and may circle downwind.
conservationStatus: Restricted protected species
populationStatus: Rare and usually solitary outside breeding season
description: An aggressive adolescent fire Pokemon with stronger claws, a cranial horn, and a more intense tail flame. This stage is the most likely in the line to injure handlers.
lore:
After Charmander was officially cataloged, Kanto rangers assumed the larger fire shadows reported near mines and dry canyons were adult members of the same hidden den groups. They were wrong. The first confirmed Charmeleon discovery came months after the burned hillside incident, when a radio-marked Charmander from the original clutch vanished from its ash shelter and was later tracked to an abandoned mine above a lava-tube system.

Miners had already abandoned the lower tunnels after repeated reports of a red, horned lizard scratching stone walls, stealing carcasses, and leaving scorch marks too high for any known Charmander. Early witnesses said it did not behave like the nervous juveniles from the foothills. It did not flee immediately. It watched, circled downwind, and defended the warmest tunnel mouths as if it owned them.

When the survey team entered the mine, they found deep claw gouges, charred bones, melted sap from burned support beams, and a strong hot-metal smell near the den chamber. The missing Charmander was found inside, larger, redder, and no longer social. Its tail scar and scale pattern matched the juvenile from the original Kanto den, but its behavior had changed completely. It challenged lights, lunged at reflections, and attacked when a handler blocked the tunnel exit.

That incident proved Charmeleon was not a separate monster from mining folklore. It was Charmander's evolved adolescent form, a dangerous survival stage between vulnerable juvenile and future apex predator. Modern ranger doctrine now treats Charmeleon as the highest handler-conflict stage of the line: no direct staring, no blocked exits, no cramped housing, no hand-feeding during growth spurts, and no assumption that a loyal Charmander will remain obedient after evolution
survivalUse: Strong heat source, predator deterrent, cauterization risk tool in last-resort survival, and camp defense. Use is dangerous and can escalate wildfire risk.
handlingWarning: Do not challenge eye contact, grab the tail, block the den exit, or approach while it is feeding.
encounterAdvice: Give it an exit path. Do not run unless already behind cover. Place rock, dirt, or water barrier between you and dry vegetation.
firstAid: Cool burns, control bleeding, irrigate bites, avoid sealing deep punctures, and seek urgent care for any Charmeleon wound.
companionSuitability: Poor to fair. Suitable only for experienced fire-type handlers.
fieldNote: If Charmeleon lowers its head and the tail flame narrows, it is choosing a target. Backing away slowly is safer than shouting.
originStory:
Charmeleon evolved as the Charmander line's dangerous adolescent survival stage. Young Charmander survive by staying near warm ash nests, guarded dens, and social groups, but that strategy fails once the body grows larger and requires more fuel. At the Level 16 growth threshold, the animal's muscles, claws, jaw strength, fuel glands, and heat-control tissues expand rapidly. The result is a solitary, territorial hunter built to leave the juvenile den, claim hotter shelter, fight rivals, and survive drought competition in Kanto's dry canyons, lava tubes, burned slopes, abandoned mines, and badland ridges.

Its aggression is not random cruelty. It is a biological pressure stage. A Charmeleon that cannot defend a warm corridor, secure prey, and withstand rival scent rarely survives long enough to become Charizard. Its hotter tail flame, sharper claws, cranial horn, stronger lungs, and developing shoulder musculature all point toward the final aerial form, but before flight develops, Charmeleon must dominate ground territory.

This makes it useful as a heat source and predator deterrent, but dangerous around people, livestock, camps, wooden structures, and dry vegetation. The discovery of a marked Charmander evolving into Charmeleon in an abandoned Kanto mine forced researchers to rewrite the entire Charmander line: cute fire-bearing juvenile, violent adolescent territory fighter, then aerial apex predator.

evolutionLine.name: Charmeleon
evolutionLine.note: The transition to Charizard is physically extreme. Cramped captivity can deform wings and produce permanently unstable adults.

mainLine.name: Charmeleon
mainLine.note: The transition to Charizard is physically extreme. Cramped captivity can deform wings and produce permanently unstable adults.

variants.method: Final stage only, special catalyst or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Charizard X; Mega Charizard Y; Gigantamax Charizard
target.note: The transition to Charizard is physically extreme. Cramped captivity can deform wings and produce permanently unstable adults.

domesticationStatus: Captive-trained only, not fully domesticated
prey: Rattata, Pidgey, small reptiles, eggs, rodents, and carrion
territoryRange: 3 to 10 square miles, larger in poor hunting territory
lifeCycle: Evolves from Charmander at level 16 and can evolve into Charizard at level 36 after major muscular and respiratory development.
containmentRequirements: Stone den with double gates, flameproof walls, heat venting, remote feeding chute, and reinforced observation shield
ecologicalRole: Mid-level predator that controls rodents and scavenges carrion but can start brush fires in dry seasons
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Bite infections, smoke exposure, scale parasites, and fuel-gland inflammation are common captive issues.
reproductionNesting: Usually not a stable nesting stage. Competes for mates and may scavenge around established Charizard territories.
predators: Charizard, larger water-types, rock-types, rival Charmeleon, and humans
emergencyResponse: If it ignites brush, prioritize evacuation and fuel breaks. For bites or burns, treat as contaminated trauma and seek emergency care.
legalStatus: Restricted fire predator. Transport in settled areas usually requires fire marshal or wildlife approval.
lifespan: Wild: 7 to 15 years. Managed care: 20 years or more with expert housing.
socialStructure: Mostly solitary. Temporary dominance interactions with packs or breeding groups are volatile.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Fire output is strong enough to ignite brush and cause deep burns.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Flame hazard commonly 10 to 25 ft, indirect wildfire risk much farther
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast ground burst about 20 to 25 mph
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High lunge and side-step speed
fieldCapabilities.balance: Good, tail counterbalances lunges

trainingRisk: Deep claw wounds, serious burns, biting, panic fire, and handler challenge behavior
physiology: The flame system intensifies after evolution. Internal fuel stores and heat-control tissues expand, raising burn output while also making the animal prone to agitation when overheated or underfed.

keyFacts.habitat: Dry canyons, lava tubes, burned slopes
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 7 to 15 years. Managed: 20 years or more.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary except brief breeding contact

domesticationDifficulty: High due to dominance behavior and fire risk
physicalCharacteristics: Red-orange biped with sharper claws, narrow eyes, stronger jaws, head crest or horn, long tail, and hotter tail flame. Developing shoulder and back musculature foreshadows Charizard wings.
dietRestrictions: Avoid low-protein diets, sugary human food, damp bedding, enclosed smoke exposure, and forced fasting during growth spurts.

  "lore": "after charmander was officially cataloged, kanto rangers assumed the larger fire shadows reported near mines and dry canyons were adult members of the same hidden den groups. they were wrong. the first confirmed charmeleon discovery came months after the burned hillside incident, when a radio-marked charmander from the original clutch vanished from its ash shelter and was later tracked to an abandoned mine above a lava-tube system.\n\nminers had already abandoned the lower tunnels after repeated reports of a red, horned lizard scratching stone walls, stealing carcasses, and leaving scorch marks too high for any known charmander. early witnesses said it did not behave like the nervous juveniles from the foothills. it did not flee immediately. it watched, circled downwind, and defended the warmest tunnel mouths as if it owned them.\n\nwhen the survey team entered the mine, they found deep claw gouges, charred bones, melted sap from burned support beams, and a strong hot-metal smell near the den chamber. the missing charmander was found inside, larger, redder, and no longer social. its tail scar and scale pattern matched the juvenile from the original kanto den, but its behavior had changed completely. it challenged lights, lunged at reflections, and attacked when a handler blocked the tunnel exit.\n\nthat incident proved charmeleon was not a separate monster from mining folklore. it was charmander's evolved adolescent form, a dangerous survival stage between vulnerable juvenile and future apex predator. modern ranger doctrine now treats charmeleon as the highest handler-conflict stage of the line: no direct staring, no blocked exits, no cramped housing, no hand-feeding during growth spurts, and no assumption that a loyal charmander will remain obedient after evolution",
  "survivaluse": "strong heat source, predator deterrent, cauterization risk tool in last-resort survival, and camp defense. use is dangerous and can escalate wildfire risk.",
  "handlingwarning": "do not challenge eye contact, grab the tail, block the den exit, or approach while it is feeding.",
  "encounteradvice": "give it an exit path. do not run unless already behind cover. place rock, dirt, or water barrier between you and dry vegetation.",
  "firstaid": "cool burns, control bleeding, irrigate bites, avoid sealing deep punctures, and seek urgent care for any charmeleon wound.",
  "companionsuitability": "poor to fair. suitable only for experienced fire-type handlers.",

  "fieldnote": "if charmeleon lowers its head and the tail flame narrows, it is choosing a target. backing away slowly is safer than shouting.",
  "visualsymbol": "ðÿ”¥",
  "image": "images/pokemon/charmeleon.png",
  "audio": "audio/pokemon/charmeleon.mp3",
  "entryauthor": "ranger field survival office",
  "originstory": "charmeleon evolved as the charmander line's dangerous adolescent survival stage. young charmander survive by staying near warm ash nests, guarded dens, and social groups, but that strategy fails once the body grows larger and requires more fuel. at the level 16 growth threshold, the animal's muscles, claws, jaw strength, fuel glands, and heat-control tissues expand rapidly. the result is a solitary, territorial hunter built to leave the juvenile den, claim hotter shelter, fight rivals, and survive drought competition in kanto's dry canyons, lava tubes, burned slopes, abandoned mines, and badland ridges.\n\nits aggression is not random cruelty. it is a biological pressure stage. a charmeleon that cannot defend a warm corridor, secure prey, and withstand rival scent rarely survives long enough to become charizard. its hotter tail flame, sharper claws, cranial horn, stronger lungs, and developing shoulder musculature all point toward the final aerial form, but before flight develops, charmeleon must dominate ground territory.\n\nthis makes it useful as a heat source and predator deterrent, but dangerous around people, livestock, camps, wooden structures, and dry vegetation. the discovery of a marked charmander evolving into charmeleon in an abandoned kanto mine forced researchers to rewrite the entire charmander line: cute fire-bearing juvenile, violent adolescent territory fighter, then aerial apex predator.",

name: Fearow
region: Kanto, Johto, Sinnoh, Unova, Kalos, Alola, and long-route aerial corridors
route: Kanto Routes 9, 16, 17, 18, 23 and Sevii Island paths; Johto Routes 9, 10, 16, 17, 18, 22, 42, 47, and 48; Sinnoh Routes 225, 226, 227, and Stark Mountain; Unova Route 15 records; Kalos Victory Road records; Alola Route 10, Route 17, Mount Hokulani, and Poni Plains
habitat: Open grassland, canyon roads, riverbanks, coastlines, mountain slopes, cliff thermals, long route corridors, farms, rocky islands, and high perches overlooking prey movement
classification: Beak Pokemon / Final Stage Endurance Raptor
behaviorTemperament: Aggressive, watchful, and stamina-driven. Fearow patrol broad ranges, dive from height, snatch food, and retreat skyward when threatened. They are less noisy than Spearow but far more dangerous on contact.
diet: Fish, insects, rodents, small birds, eggs, grubs, field prey, carrion, stolen food, and smaller Pokemon taken from grass, soil, or shallow water
activityPattern: Diurnal with dawn patrols and long midday soaring. Coastal and mountain individuals may ride thermals for hours while scanning for prey.
bestTimeToFind: Morning or late afternoon above long roads, cliffs, route signs, coastal shallows, river bends, and fields where small prey flushes into open air
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Large silhouette is visible, but distance and altitude make age, intent, and target hard to judge until it dives.
conservationStatus: Locally protected as an aerial predator, but controlled near airports, livestock, poultry yards, and heavily traveled route markets
populationStatus: Stable in open country and route corridors. Less common than Spearow due to larger territory needs and competition with Pidgeot and other raptors.
description: A Normal and Flying-type beak Pokemon with broad wings, strong stamina, and a long piercing beak used to strike prey from air, soil, and water.
lore: Old route maps in Kanto marked certain cliffs not with warning signs for falling rock, but with painted beaks. Those were Fearow perches. Caravans learned not to carry uncovered food through Route 17 and Route 18 when high shadows circled overhead. Unlike Spearow, Fearow does not need a flock to make travelers retreat. It rises, watches, chooses a target, and drops. Johto records add another detail: Fearow remember profitable paths. A bird that steals fish from a bridge today may return tomorrow at the same hour. In Alola, fish Pokemon and coastline traffic made Fearow a problem around open markets and shore paths. Ancient murals resembling Fearow suggest humans have watched this body plan for a very long time. In real-world field terms, Fearow is not just a big Spearow. It is the line's answer to distance, altitude, and hunger. If Spearow is the alarm, Fearow is the spear falling from it.
survivalUse: Can reveal prey movement, thermal columns, water edges, and unsafe open corridors. Following Fearow from a distance may lead to fish, carrion, or river shallows, but food theft risk is high.
handlingWarning: Do not handle without raptor equipment. Keep food covered, protect eyes and face, and never stand under a low circling Fearow with exposed small animals or shiny objects.
encounterAdvice: Move under cover, secure food, and avoid running in open ground. If it dives, crouch beside a tree, vehicle, rock, or pack frame rather than waving arms in open air.
firstAid: For beak punctures, control bleeding, irrigate thoroughly, dress the wound, and seek urgent care for face, eye, neck, tendon, or deep muscle injuries. Treat falls caused by panic as secondary trauma.
companionSuitability: Poor for casual companionship; suitable only for expert aerial handlers, conservation teams, or licensed long-range field scouts
fieldBehaviors: Soars for long periods without landing
Plucks prey from soil or shallow water
Uses a long beak for precise strikes
fieldNote: Fearow that circle high and wide are usually searching. Fearow that fly low, silent, and straight have already chosen a route or target.
originStory: Fearow evolves from Spearow when short-winged territorial aggression becomes too costly for a growing bird. Evolution at Level 20 expands the wing area, lengthens the neck, sharpens the beak into a deep probing tool, and shifts the animal from grass defense to aerial patrol. The line likely thrived along old travel routes where insects, rodents, fish, and human food were concentrated. Cliff paths, canyon roads, river crossings, and islands all rewarded birds that could fly longer than rivals and strike from above before prey reached cover.

evolutionLine.name: Spearow
evolutionLine.note: Base stage tiny bird Pokemon. Evolves when wing endurance, beak strength, and territorial drive become strong enough for long patrols.

evolutionLine.name: Fearow
evolutionLine.note: Adult beak Pokemon. Built for distance flight, high dives, riverbank hunting, and aggressive aerial territory defense.

mainLine.name: Spearow
mainLine.note: Small territorial grassland bird. Loud alarm calls, rapid wingbeats, and reckless pecking protect its range.

mainLine.name: Fearow
mainLine.note: Long-beaked endurance raptor. Patrols roads, cliffs, fields, and coastlines while hunting insects, fish, and small prey.

domesticationStatus: Mostly wild; licensed use in aerial scouting and pest control is possible but demanding
prey: Fish, rodents, insects, grubs, small birds, Rattata, nestlings, eggs, carrion, and exposed traveler food
temperamentTriggers: Small animals exposed in open routes
territoryRange: Usually 5 to 20 square miles depending on cliffs, food, and wind. Coastal or mountain birds may patrol longer corridors following thermals.
lifeCycle: Spearow evolves into Fearow starting at Level 20. Adults claim high perches, broad hunting lanes, and nesting ledges, then patrol by sight over fields, rivers, roads, and shorelines.
containmentRequirements: Large licensed aviary or mews, high perches, reinforced flight space, safe weathering area, raptor-grade gloves, controlled feeding stations, injury monitoring, and no public access to feeding zones
ecologicalRole: Controls rodents, insects, fish overpopulation in shallow areas, and carrion waste. Also pressures smaller bird Pokemon and shapes open-route animal movement.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Main concerns are feather parasites, beak injuries, contaminated prey, and puncture wound infection after attacks.
reproductionNesting: Nests on cliffs, tall trees, route bluffs, canyon ledges, and high human structures. Adults defend airspace with dives and may abandon nests if repeated human disturbance continues.
predators: Pidgeot, large electric types, stronger aerial predators, humans, rock-type ambush predators near cliffs, and rival Fearow during territorial conflict
emergencyResponse: For repeated dive attacks, move under cover, cover the head, secure food, and leave the territory. Do not fire projectiles upward unless legally authorized and necessary for immediate defense.
legalStatus: Often protected as a native aerial predator, but controlled permits may apply around airports, food markets, poultry yards, and high-traffic route corridors.
stressSicknessSigns: Loss of balance on high perch
lifespan: Wild: 6 to 12 years. Managed care: 18 to 25 years with expert raptor housing and flight conditioning.
socialStructure: Mostly pairs or solitary hunters outside breeding season. Juveniles may linger near Spearow flocks, but adults defend separate hunting lanes.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Main hazards are dive force, beak puncture, talon grip, and food snatching.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Dive hazard can begin from hundreds of feet above, with contact risk at the final 3 to 10 ft.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast dive and pursuit flight, roughly 45 to 70 mph in field conditions
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent in open sky, weaker in dense brush or tight indoor spaces
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Ground jump is limited, but takeoff is powerful from perches and ledges
fieldCapabilities.balance: Strong perch grip and long tail control during dives and high wind

trainingRisk: Severe puncture wounds, face strikes, food aggression, escape, wing injuries, stress from cramped housing, and handler overconfidence
physiology: Fearow has a long neck, broad endurance wings, strong flight muscles, keen eyes, and a long beak shaped for probing soil or water and piercing prey. Its stamina allows all-day flight, making simple distance an unreliable safety measure.

keyFacts.wingspan: Approx. 5 to 7 ft in field specimens
keyFacts.lifestyle: Diurnal aerial predator and route patrol bird
keyFacts.diet: Carnivorous-leaning omnivore with fish and small prey focus
keyFacts.habitat: Cliffs, open routes, rivers, coastlines, farms, canyons, and mountain roads
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 6 to 12 years. Managed: 18 to 25 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary adult or mated pair

domesticationDifficulty: Very high. Requires expert raptor handling, large flight space, strict feeding control, and public-safety planning.
physicalCharacteristics: Large brown bird Pokemon with broad wings, cream flight-feather tips, shaggy neck base, long pink spear-like beak, red head crest, narrow eyes, strong legs, and long tail feathers.
dietRestrictions: Avoid spoiled fish, lead-contaminated prey, high-salt human food, excessive fatty scraps, cramped feeding cages, and sudden diet changes that reduce flight condition.

attacks.name: Drill Run
attacks.fieldUse: Diving beak-and-body attack used to strike grounded targets or dig prey from soft soil.

attacks.name: Peck
attacks.fieldUse: Precise beak strike for insects, fish, or warning jabs.

attacks.name: Pluck
attacks.fieldUse: Snatch attack used on exposed food, berries, and small prey.

attacks.name: Fury Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Repeated stabbing strikes at close range.

attacks.name: Aerial Ace
attacks.fieldUse: Clean aerial cut from a sudden dive or side pass.

attacks.name: Wing Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Forceful wing blow during low passes.

attacks.name: Drill Peck
attacks.fieldUse: Signature deep piercing attack from the long beak.

attacks.name: Roost
attacks.fieldUse: Perch recovery behavior after long patrols or wing strain.

Kanto: Routes 9, 16, 17, 18, and 23 in various records
Sevii Island areas including Canyon Entrance, Cape Brink, Kindle Road, Mt. Ember, Ruin Valley, Sevault Canyon, Treasure Beach, and Water Path
Open roads and cliff-adjacent route corridors
Johto: High route edges and open grassland hunting lanes
Alola: Routes 10 and 17, Mount Hokulani, and Poni Plains

officialEvolutionSummary.spearowToFearow: Spearow evolves into Fearow starting at Level 20.
officialEvolutionSummary.finalStage: Fearow is the final stage of the Spearow line.
officialEvolutionSummary.specialForm: No standard Mega Evolution or regional form is currently part of the main line.

sourceUseNote: Core typing, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, game location anchors, and move names are based on official-style Pokedex and game database records. Lifespan, ecology, first aid, containment, and real-world field behavior are interpretive survival-guide material for this passion project.

name: Gastly
route: Abandoned towers, graveyards, old buildings, cave mouths, fog-heavy ruins, and quiet places with low airflow
habitat: Dark enclosed spaces, abandoned structures, caves, ruined towers, cemeteries, sealed basements, and stagnant rooms where its gas body will not be scattered by wind.
behaviorTemperament: Mischievous, elusive, territorial around nesting vapor pockets, and dangerous when approached in enclosed spaces. It often watches silently before testing intruders with cold spots, whispers, or mild hallucinations.
diet: Feeds on trace life energy, fear response chemicals, decaying organic vapors, fungal gases, and small insects stunned inside its vapor cloud.
activityPattern: Nocturnal and fog-active. Most active after sunset, during cold damp weather, and in sealed spaces where air movement is weak.
bestTimeToFind: Late night, pre-dawn, or during heavy fog near old buildings, burial grounds, caves, and abandoned towers.
observationDifficulty: High. Its body can thin out, drift through cracks, hide in shadows, and blend into ordinary mist or smoke.
conservationStatus: Locally stable in historic ruins and cave systems. Populations decline quickly when abandoned habitats are ventilated, demolished, or heavily cleansed with industrial air systems.
description: A Ghost and Poison-type gaseous creature made from a dark core surrounded by toxic purple vapor.
lore: Gastly is often mistaken for a wandering spirit because its body has almost no solid mass. In reality, it behaves more like a living toxic vapor colony with intelligence, memory, and a predatory sense for fear.
survivalUse: Can warn of sealed rooms, hidden crypts, dead air pockets, and chemically unstable areas. Trained ghost-type specialists may use Gastly to scout through cracks or detect hidden movement, but this is unsafe for ordinary travelers.
handlingWarning: Do not inhale its vapor. Do not enter sealed rooms where a Gastly has been seen without ventilation, respiratory protection, and a clear exit path.
encounterAdvice: Stay calm, move toward fresh air, avoid running through its gas cloud, and do not swing weapons or throw objects through it. Use airflow, bright light, and open space to force distance.
firstAid: For suspected exposure, leave the area immediately, breathe fresh air, remove contaminated outer clothing, rinse irritated eyes or skin with clean water, and seek emergency medical help if dizziness, numbness, confusion, fainting, or breathing trouble occurs.
companionSuitability: Poor for most people. Possible only for experienced ghost-type handlers with controlled ventilation, protective equipment, and strict containment practices.
fieldBehaviors: Drifts silently through cracks, vents, and broken walls
Condenses around warm breath, candle flames, and frightened prey
May create cold spots before becoming visible
Can dim lanterns or make flames gutter by displacing air
Avoids strong wind, fans, open fields, and heavy airflow
Uses sleep-inducing gas or hypnotic motion to weaken targets
weaknesses: Bright moving light that disrupts concealment
resistances: Small openings and barriers that stop solid creatures
signsOfPresence: Purple or black vapor moving against normal airflow
Sudden sweet-chemical smell in stale air
Lantern flames shrinking or turning unstable
Condensation forming on walls without clear cause
Small insects found unconscious or dead near cracks
Unexplained dizziness, numb lips, or pressure behind the eyes
fieldNote: A quiet room with no wind is Gastly's advantage. If the air starts to taste sweet or metallic, leave before investigating.
originStory: Early field accounts describe Gastly appearing after mass illness events in sealed monasteries, plague houses, and forgotten burial tunnels. The oldest stories claim they were spirits of the dead, but later ranger dissections of abandoned nests suggested something stranger: a living vapor organism born where decaying matter, mineral gas, and psychic residue collected in airless spaces. The first confirmed capture ended badly when the specimen escaped its jar, seeped under a barracks door, and left twelve guards unconscious before sunrise. Since then, most regions classify wild Gastly as a confined-space hazard rather than a normal animal.

evolutionLine.name: Gastly
evolutionLine.method: Evolves into Haunter starting at level 25
evolutionLine.note: Mostly gaseous body. High inhalation and fear-response risk.

evolutionLine.name: Haunter
evolutionLine.method: Evolves into Gengar when traded; some regions report Linking Cord evolution conditions
evolutionLine.note: More defined hands and predatory behavior. Higher direct-contact danger.

evolutionLine.name: Gengar
evolutionLine.note: Dense shadow body, advanced ambush behavior, and extreme haunting risk.

evolutionTree.subtitle: Evolution Tree Ã¢â‚¬Â¢ Ghost / Poison Type

mainLine.name: Gastly
mainLine.note: Gaseous, evasive, and dangerous in enclosed areas.

mainLine.name: Haunter
mainLine.note: More aggressive and able to interact with victims directly.

mainLine.name: Gengar
mainLine.note: Fully developed shadow predator with high intelligence.

variants.method: Linking Cord in some regional records

target.name: Gengar
target.note: Observed in special regional evolution systems rather than standard trade handling.

humanConflictRisk: High in enclosed spaces. Gastly can cause panic, poisoning symptoms, fainting, accidents during flight, and false haunting reports that lead people deeper into unsafe structures.
domesticationStatus: Not domesticated. Captive specimens are maintained only by licensed ghost-type researchers, occult hazard units, and specialized battle handlers.
prey: Small insects, cave rodents, weakened birds, sleeping animals, and occasionally larger creatures overcome by vapor exposure or fear paralysis.
temperamentTriggers: Bright lights aimed directly into its core
Fire waved through its vapor body
Removing old bones, relics, or decayed organic matter from its territory
Loud chanting, bells, or high-frequency sound
territoryRange: A single Gastly may haunt one room, tunnel section, or ruin chamber. A colony can occupy an entire abandoned structure if airflow is low and prey traffic is steady.
lifeCycle: Begins as a loose toxic vapor knot around a dark spectral core. As it feeds and stabilizes, it develops stronger cohesion, better memory, and more controlled movement. Mature Gastly may evolve into Haunter after reaching sufficient density and predatory skill.
containmentRequirements: A sealed ghost-rated containment vessel with controlled negative pressure, filtered exhaust, backup airflow control, low-light monitoring, and no open cracks wider than a few millimeters.
ecologicalRole: Breaks down carrion vapors, controls insect populations in caves and ruins, and discourages larger predators from nesting in contaminated dead-air spaces.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate to high. Main risks are toxic inhalation, eye irritation, fainting, panic injury, respiratory distress, and possible contamination of clothing or masks after exposure.
reproductionNesting: Poorly understood. Field reports suggest Gastly colonies form vapor nurseries in sealed rooms rich in decay gases, mineral seepage, and psychic residue. No normal egg-laying behavior is confirmed in the field guide record.
populationStatus: Uncommon but persistent. Populations are easiest to miss because many sightings are dismissed as fog, smoke, mold exposure, or ghost stories.
predators: Stronger ghost-types, psychic hunters, dark-type scavengers, specialized exorcist PokÃƒÂ©mon, and trained containment teams using forced ventilation.
emergencyResponse: Evacuate upwind or toward moving air, ventilate the space if safe, keep exposed people awake and breathing fresh air, avoid entering alone, and call a ghost-type hazard team for enclosed infestations.
legalStatus: Wild capture restricted in most managed regions. Entry into known Gastly structures may require ranger clearance because ordinary masks and flashlights may not be enough.
stressSicknessSigns: Failure to hold a spherical form
Avoidance of shadows it normally uses
Erratic whispers or repeated loops of sound
lifespan: Unknown. Individual field specimens may persist for decades if undisturbed, but unstable young vapor forms can disperse quickly in open air.
socialStructure: Usually solitary in small spaces, but multiple Gastly may gather in large ruins or cave systems. Colonies do not show a clear hierarchy, though denser individuals control the darkest and least ventilated chambers.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: Not electrical; uses spectral and toxic vapor pressure
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Not applicable
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast drifting movement over short distances
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Very high in low airflow
fieldCapabilities.balance: Maintains cohesion through its central spectral core

trainingRisk: Primary training risks include inhalation injury, sleep exposure, panic accidents, containment failure, and the handler mistaking playful haunting behavior for obedience.
physiology: Gastly appears to be a semi-coherent toxic gas mass held together by a dark spectral core. Its vapor can irritate skin, eyes, and lungs, while the core seems to coordinate movement, perception, and predatory response. Because its body lacks normal organs, cutting, striking, or grabbing it is usually ineffective.

keyFacts.diet: Fear response energy, decay gases, trace life energy, insects, and stunned small prey
keyFacts.habitat: Abandoned buildings, caves, towers, graveyards, and stagnant enclosed spaces
keyFacts.lifespan: Unknown; possibly decades in stable enclosed habitats
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary; colonies possible in large haunted structures

domesticationDifficulty: Extreme. Gastly cannot be handled like a normal companion animal and may drift through barriers, contaminate rooms, or unintentionally poison its handler.
physicalCharacteristics: Gastly has a dark floating spherical core surrounded by a shifting purple vapor cloud. It has large white eyes, a wide mouth, small fangs, and no stable limbs. Its outline changes constantly, making size estimates difficult in poor light.
dietRestrictions: Avoid exposing Gastly to strong chemical cleaners, high-speed fans, open flames, and purified airflow systems except during emergency containment. In managed care, feeding should never involve live human fear exposure.

name: Hypothermia
rarity: Common in cold or wet exposure
region: Cold, wet, windy, or high-altitude areas
route: Rainy trails, mountains, rivers, winter roads, exposed camps
habitat: Any environment where cold, wind, wet clothing, or exhaustion can drop body temperature.
behaviorTemperament: Progressive and easy to underestimate. Confusion can make self-rescue harder.
diet: Not applicable.
activityPattern: Most likely during cold, wet, windy, exhausted, or poorly sheltered conditions.
bestTimeToFind: Night, storms, winter travel, river crossings, and wet windy exposure.
observationDifficulty: Medium. Early signs can be subtle.
conservationStatus: Not applicable.
description: A dangerous drop in body temperature caused by cold, wind, wet clothing, or exposure.
lore: A quiet hazard that can worsen before the victim realizes how much danger they are in.
survivalUse: Recognizing it early helps you prioritize shelter, dry clothing, insulation, and warmth.
handlingWarning: Severe symptoms require emergency help. Do not ignore confusion, stumbling, or uncontrollable shivering.
encounterAdvice: Stop exposure early. Get dry, block wind, insulate from the ground, and warm the person gradually.
firstAid: Call emergency help for severe symptoms. Remove wet clothing when possible, insulate, protect from wind, and avoid rough handling.
companionSuitability: Not applicable.
fieldNote: Seek emergency medical help when symptoms are severe or worsening.

name: Ivysaur
route: Deep forest clearings, abandoned greenhouses, overgrown ruins, canyon shade, and humid preserve zones
habitat: Heavily vegetated areas with enough sunlight for the dorsal flower and enough cover for ambush feeding
behaviorTemperament: More territorial than Bulbasaur and less tolerant of handling. It may appear sleepy, then lash out when a person steps inside its root zone.
diet: Sunlight, water, berries, woody shoots, insects, small rodents, nutrient-rich soil, and decaying plant matter
activityPattern: Crepuscular and morning active. Roots itself during hot afternoons and during heavy bud growth.
bestTimeToFind: Morning after rain, at the edges of sunlit clearings where the bud can warm without drying out
observationDifficulty: Hard. It hides low among ferns and can remain rooted and motionless for hours.
conservationStatus: Protected and monitored due to high illegal capture risk
populationStatus: Uncommon in wild areas, mostly found in guarded territories
description: The adolescent stage of Bulbasaur. The bulb has opened into a heavy bud that drives the host toward heavier feeding, territorial behavior, and longer periods of rooted rest.
lore: Old ranger warnings say an Ivysaur clearing is quiet because everything small has already learned not to breathe there.
survivalUse: Can identify fertile soil, locate water, clear thorn brush with vines, and produce shade. Powder risk limits use in enclosed survival shelters.
handlingWarning: Never stand behind the head near the bud. Do not cut vines. Do not transport in a sealed car or tent.
encounterAdvice: Circle wide, stay upwind, and do not step on root trails. If the bud flexes open, retreat without turning your back.
firstAid: Use eye wash and soap-water decontamination. Give clean air for powder exposure. Seek medical help for weakness, wheezing, severe rash, or confusion.
companionSuitability: Fair with expert handlers, poor for casual homes
fieldNote: A healthy Ivysaur smells green and damp. A sour-sweet smell means the bud is sick, stressed, or about to vent chemicals.
originStory: Ivysaur likely represents the point where the ancient plant parasite learned to steer its host instead of simply riding it. Some wild specimens act less like animals and more like walking gardens defending a buried root network.

evolutionLine.name: Ivysaur
evolutionLine.note: Evolution into Venusaur is dangerous in cramped captivity because the flower and root system expand rapidly.

mainLine.name: Ivysaur
mainLine.note: Evolution into Venusaur is dangerous in cramped captivity because the flower and root system expand rapidly.

variants.method: Final stage only, special catalyst or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Venusaur and Gigantamax Venusaur are special battle forms, not normal field evolution
target.note: Evolution into Venusaur is dangerous in cramped captivity because the flower and root system expand rapidly.

domesticationStatus: Captive-trained only under advanced grass-poison license
prey: Large insects, small mammals, berries, vines, tender bark, and fallen fruit
temperamentTriggers: Being forced to move while rooted
territoryRange: 1 to 4 square miles around water, shade, and sun access
lifeCycle: Evolves from Bulbasaur at level 16. Evolves into Venusaur at level 32 after the dorsal bud reaches full flowering mass.
containmentRequirements: Large humid outdoor pen with filtered sunlight, root barriers, air movement, washable surfaces, and emergency spore extraction.
ecologicalRole: Controls insects and small herbivores, spreads seeds, recycles leaf litter, and creates shaded microhabitats
diseaseParasiteRisk: High. Flower mites, bud rot, fungal bloom, spore allergy, and soil bacteria may affect handlers and the Pokemon.
reproductionNesting: Not normally the primary breeding stage. May guard nests made by Venusaur or defend juvenile Bulbasaur clusters.
predators: Charmeleon, Fearow, Arbok, large fire-type predators, and humans seeking rare starter lines
emergencyResponse: Evacuate downwind personnel, ventilate the area, rinse exposed skin and eyes, and treat respiratory symptoms as urgent.
legalStatus: Restricted due to toxic powder and invasive root risk
stressSicknessSigns: Standing still too long in shade
lifespan: Wild: 3 to 7 years. Managed care: 10 to 18 years if evolution is delayed responsibly.
socialStructure: Usually solitary or loosely attached to a Bulbasaur family territory. Avoids other Ivysaur unless breeding or competing for clearings.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Elemental output is vine force, sleep powder, poison powder, and solar growth.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Spore and pollen hazard about 15 to 40 ft in still air, farther with wind
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Short ground burst about 15 mph
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Low to moderate, relies on bracing
fieldCapabilities.balance: Stable but top-heavy when the bud is wet

trainingRisk: Vine crushing, sleep powder, poison powder, bud strain, and handler sensitization to spores
physiology: The plant partner has expanded deeper into the host, drawing more sugars, minerals, and nerve input. The bud can store powders, toxins, and growth chemicals, making Ivysaur more hazardous than its smaller form.

keyFacts.diet: Photosynthetic omnivore with higher protein demand
keyFacts.habitat: Humid forest clearings and overgrown ruins
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 3 to 7 years. Managed: 10 to 18 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary, sometimes one guardian with juveniles

domesticationDifficulty: High. The plant partner resists restraint and can poison enclosed air.
physicalCharacteristics: Larger quadruped with stronger legs, heavier shoulders, pointed ears, red eyes, and a large unopened flower bud on its back. Vines are thicker and more muscular than Bulbasaur vines.
dietRestrictions: Avoid dry enclosure conditions, lawn chemicals, animal feed with salt, and forced high-protein diets that outpace bud development.

  "dietrestrictions": "avoid dry enclosure conditions, lawn chemicals, animal feed with salt, and forced high-protein diets that outpace bud development.",

name: Kakuna
route: Kanto Route 24, Route 25, Viridian Forest, and Pattern Bush; Johto Ilex Forest, National Park, Route 2, Routes 30 and 31, Route 47, and headbutt-tree corridors; Central Kalos Santalune Forest; Hoenn Safari Zone records; Sinnoh Eterna Forest; real-world analogue: hanging pupae in mossy Olympic Peninsula forests, orchard windbreaks, and damp national park groves
habitat: Tree trunks, branch nooks, underside of large leaves, hollow bark seams, thorny thickets, damp forest edges, and protected nursery trees guarded by Beedrill patrols
behaviorTemperament: Nearly motionless, but not passive. Kakuna hardens when disturbed, heats noticeably during internal development, and may extend a poison barb if bitten or grabbed. Wild specimens rely on camouflage, shell toughness, and adult Beedrill response rather than escape.
diet: Does not normally feed in the field. Lives on stored larval reserves while reworking tissues into the adult body.
activityPattern: Stationary. Internal activity peaks during warm daylight and humid evenings. Shell hardening is most obvious when shaken, handled, or exposed to predator scent.
bestTimeToFind: Morning or late afternoon on shaded tree trunks, under large leaves, and in branch nooks near Weedle feeding damage
observationDifficulty: Hard. Its color and rigid posture resemble leaves, seed pods, or hardened sap masses. Colonies are easier to detect by Weedle feeding signs and Beedrill patrol loops.
conservationStatus: Protected in colony nurseries and old-growth forest corridors
populationStatus: Stable in protected forests, vulnerable in parks where people remove suspicious cocoons, spray insecticide, or clear hollow nesting trees
description: A Bug and Poison pupal Pokemon that appears almost immobile while its body reorganizes for evolution. Its hard shell, internal heat, and emergency barb make it dangerous to handle even before it becomes Beedrill.
lore: For centuries, Johto foresters called Kakuna 'hanging thorns' because the shells appeared overnight after Weedle feeding surges. Superstition said that if a child counted the cocoons on an Ilex tree, the number would double by morning and the forest would remember the child's voice. The truth is less magical and more dangerous. A mature Kakuna tree is never empty. Each shell is a living alarm bell, each warm casing a Beedrill not yet born. In old Viridian records, the worst swarm attacks began when collectors pried Kakuna from bark and carried them in sacks. The shells did not scream. They only clicked, warmed, and released scent. The adults did the screaming for them.
survivalUse: A Kakuna cluster indicates a nearby Weedle nursery and adult Beedrill patrol zone. Shed shell fragments can be studied for toxin research and lightweight chitin composites, but field collection should be avoided without permits.
handlingWarning: Do not pry Kakuna from bark, do not warm them artificially, and do not store them in sealed bags. Heat stress, vibration, and separation from colony scent can trigger emergency evolution or adult swarm response.
encounterAdvice: Observe from several yards away. Avoid standing directly under cocoon branches. If one falls, do not catch it bare-handed. Mark the location, retreat, and notify a ranger or licensed bug handler.
firstAid: Treat punctures like venom stings: wash, cool, immobilize the area, and monitor for allergic reaction. Shell abrasions can carry irritant dust, so rinse eyes or open cuts immediately.
companionSuitability: Poor as a companion; suitable only as a managed metamorphosis stage in licensed colony programs
fieldBehaviors: Clings to trees for long periods
Leans into bark grooves for camouflage
May undergo rapid emergency evolution under severe stress
signsOfPresence: Hard yellow shell shapes tucked under leaves
Warm casing when viewed with thermal optics
Clusters above stripped Weedle feeding zones
Fine silk anchor bands around branch forks
Beedrill patrol loops around specific trees
Faint clicking from bark during warm afternoons
equipment: Epinephrine auto-injector for known severe allergy
Permit tag and field marker tape
fieldNote: A warm Kakuna is not dying. It is building the adult. If the shell becomes hot and the Beedrill patrol noise stops, leave immediately.
originStory: Kakuna is the line's answer to the problem of becoming something more dangerous while still alive in a forest full of predators. Weedle reaches Level 7 after storing enough leaf protein, venom precursors, and silk-chitin material. Its outer tissues stiffen into a hanging shell while the internal body softens, rearranges, and grows compound eyes, wing bases, foreleg stingers, venom sacs, and flight muscles. The heat felt through the shell is the cost of that transformation. The stage is short but critical. Without Beedrill adults, most Kakuna would be eaten before emergence. With Beedrill adults, the whole tree becomes a defended nursery.

evolutionLine.name: Weedle
evolutionLine.note: Larval venom-feeder. Evolves when the body has stored enough leaf matter, venom protein, and silk-chitin material to seal itself into Kakuna.

evolutionLine.name: Kakuna
evolutionLine.note: Immobile pupal armor stage. Evolves once internal wing muscles, foreleg stingers, compound eyes, and venom reservoirs finish forming.

evolutionLine.name: Beedrill
evolutionLine.method: Final stage; Mega Evolution with Beedrillite
evolutionLine.note: Territorial flying colony defender. Capable of Mega Evolution into Mega Beedrill when bonded to a handler and exposed to Beedrillite.

mainLine.name: Weedle
mainLine.note: Small larval forest forager. Feeds on leaves, hides in grass and bushes, and relies on its head barb for defense.

mainLine.name: Kakuna
mainLine.note: Pupal armor stage. Nearly motionless outside, but hot and biologically active inside while preparing for final metamorphosis.

mainLine.name: Beedrill
mainLine.note: Final venomous flyer. Swarm defense, high-speed strikes, nest protection, and three-stinger combat define the adult form.

target.name: Mega Beedrill
target.note: Temporary high-speed combat form. Leg structures become stingers, wing count increases, and venom delivery becomes faster and more specialized.

humanConflictRisk: Medium near colony trees, Low if left undisturbed
domesticationStatus: Managed metamorphosis stage, not a normal companion stage
prey: Does not hunt. Vulnerable to birds, bats, small mammals, and parasitic insects while immobile.
territoryRange: Stationary on an anchor tree, usually inside a Beedrill patrol zone of 100 yards to 1 mile depending on colony size
lifeCycle: Evolves from Weedle starting at Level 7, anchors to bark or leaf cover, reorganizes internally, heats during metamorphosis, and evolves into Beedrill starting at Level 10.
containmentRequirements: Anchored branch section, stable humidity, airflow, low vibration, no sealed containers, sting-safe viewing distance, emergency Beedrill containment plan, and isolation from household pets
ecologicalRole: Stores larval biomass through metamorphosis, provides a defended prey source for specialist predators, and anchors Beedrill colony reproduction around stable trees.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Fungal shell rot, mites in silk anchors, failed shedding, heat stress, and venom-barb punctures are the main risks.
reproductionNesting: Kakuna itself does not reproduce. It is attached near larval feeding sites and future Beedrill patrol routes. Colonies often place many Kakuna on the same protected trees.
predators: Pidgey, Spearow, Fearow, Zubat, Noctowl, parasitic wasps, fungus, rodents, humans collecting illegally, and hungry bug-types
emergencyResponse: If a Kakuna is damaged, do not carry it through the forest. Back away, cover exposed skin, and leave the colony path. If shell cracking and intense buzzing begin together, expect adult emergence or swarm response.
legalStatus: Protected in many parks because removing Kakuna can collapse local Beedrill colony structure and cause swarm events
stressSicknessSigns: Cold shell during warm development periods
Failure to harden when touched with a probe
lifespan: Official lifespan is not stated. Field estimate: Kakuna stage lasts days to weeks before Beedrill emergence in healthy conditions; unevolved managed specimens may persist several months, but long-term suppression is unhealthy.
socialStructure: Stationary colony nursery stage. Kakuna communicate mostly through scent, vibration, and Beedrill response rather than visible behavior.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Defensive capacity is shell hardening and venom barb extension.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Barb contact range is short, but colony alarm scent can affect Beedrill behavior across the tree.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Almost none
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: None; relies on armor
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent anchored stability if silk and bark remain intact

trainingRisk: Failed metamorphosis, premature Beedrill emergence, handler punctures, colony aggression, fungal shell rot, and stress death from rough transport
physiology: Kakuna is an armored pupal organism. The rigid outer casing protects a soft internal body that is rapidly forming adult Beedrill structures. Heat is a normal sign of intense internal activity, but excessive heat, odor, or leakage indicates danger.

keyFacts.diet: Does not normally feed during this stage
keyFacts.habitat: Tree trunks, branch nooks, leaves, and guarded forest nurseries
keyFacts.lifespan: Official not stated. Field estimate: days to weeks before evolution; several months if prevented from evolving under managed conditions.
keyFacts.groupSize: Often clustered on defended trees

domesticationDifficulty: High for normal owners. The animal appears easy because it barely moves, but the risks are hidden in temperature control, shell damage, and the adult form that follows.
physicalCharacteristics: Yellow segmented cocoon body with narrow dark eyes, rigid shell plates, pointed lower body, limited movement, hidden developing adult tissues, and a small poison barb used only under threat.
dietRestrictions: Do not feed unless directed by a specialist. Avoid sealed humid containers, dry heat lamps, insecticides, moldy bark, vibration, and forced shell cleaning.

attacks.name: Poison Sting
attacks.fieldUse: Emergency barb extension from the shell when handled or bitten.

attacks.name: String Shot
attacks.fieldUse: Residual silk discharge used around its anchor point.

attacks.name: Harden
attacks.fieldUse: Rapid shell tensioning that makes the outer casing harder and more difficult to crush.

attacks.name: Iron Defense
attacks.fieldUse: Advanced defensive training response that reinforces the shell beyond normal hardening.

sinnoh: Grand Underground caves in Shining Pearl after National Pokedex
unova: Route 12 through evolved forms or breeding
alola: Limited transfer or Route 4 for Beedrill in Ultra Moon
lumiose: Legends: Z-A Vert District, Bleu District, Magenta District, Rouge District, Jaune District, Wild Zone 1, and bug-type wild zones where available

officialEvolutionSummary: Weedle evolves into Kakuna starting at Level 7. Kakuna evolves into Beedrill starting at Level 10. Beedrill can Mega Evolve into Mega Beedrill using Beedrillite.
sourceUseNote: Core type, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, moves, and game-location anchors were checked against current online Pokemon database sources. Lifespan and real-world survival ecology are field-guide extrapolations because official lifespan is not stated.

 pokemon-kakuna.json pokemon-kakuna kakuna

name: Metapod
route: Viridian Forest, Route 24, Route 25, mulberry orchards, fence rails, barn rafters, chapel gates, schoolhouse eaves, and shaded forest-edge hardening sites
habitat: Upright sheltered surfaces with shade, stable humidity, low vibration, predator concealment, and enough height to avoid ground scavengers while the soft internal body reorganizes
behaviorTemperament: Still, reactive, and entirely defensive. It cannot flee or forage; stress is expressed through hardening, clicking contractions, and catastrophic shell damage if disturbed too often.
diet: Does not actively feed during the chrysalis stage; survives on energy stored as Caterpie
activityPattern: Stationary through day and night. Responds to vibration, pressure, temperature, and light while awaiting emergence.
bestTimeToFind: After Caterpie climbing events, under shaded rails, along orchard fences, in rafters, and beneath protected branches after wet feeding seasons
observationDifficulty: Easy to see when exposed, but difficult to assess safely. Stillness is not proof of death, and shell condition does not always reveal internal distress.
conservationStatus: Common protected transitional stage
populationStatus: Stable where Caterpie populations are stable; mass clusters occur after outbreak seasons and can suffer high mortality from mishandling, predators, mold, or relocation.
description: A hardened Bug-type chrysalis formed when Caterpie evolves. It is armored on the outside but soft and vulnerable within, relying on stillness, camouflage, and hardening until it can emerge as Butterfree.
lore:
Metapod does not sleep.

That was the first thing early naturalists got wrong.

They saw the still body, the sealed shell, the blank eyes, and the lack of limbs, and they assumed the creature had entered a simple dormant state. Farmers called them husks. Children stacked them like stones. Trainers carried them in sacks. Birds ignored the hard ones and cracked open the weak ones.

Everyone thought Metapod was waiting peacefully to become Butterfree.

It is not peaceful.

It is trapped in the loudest silence a living thing can endure.

Inside the shell, the Caterpie body is breaking itself down. Muscles soften. Organs shift. Nerves detach and reconnect. The mouth becomes useless. The gut empties. The wings form as folded wet sheets pressed against a body that can no longer run. A Metapod can feel vibration, pressure, temperature, and light through its shell. It knows when something touches it. It knows when it is moved. It knows when a beak tests the casing.

It simply cannot do much about it.

That helplessness is the heart of every Metapod story.

A Caterpie can crawl away. A Butterfree can fly. A Metapod can only endure.

Its famous hardening response is not courage. It is panic made anatomical. When threatened, Metapod contracts specialized tissues beneath the shell, increasing rigidity until the outer casing becomes difficult to crack. This works against small predators. It works against careless handling. It works against falling branches, rain, and most early trainer battles.

It does not work against hunger.

Predators learn quickly. Pidgey drop Metapod onto rocks. Rattata chew around the seams. Beedrill carry them back to hives and wedge them into tree bark until the shell weakens. Humans have historically used them as bait, weights, toys, and emergency food. In famine records from rural Kanto, boiled Metapod shells appear more than once under names like â€œgreen podsâ€ or â€œforest eggs.â€

The official explanation is starvation.

The older explanation is worse:

People ate them because they did not scream.

A Metapod may remain aware for days while trapped in a predatorâ€™s cache, a childâ€™s collection jar, or a trainerâ€™s pack. Rangers now teach that a captured Metapod must be secured upright, shaded, and left undisturbed unless moving it is necessary. Older trainers did not know this. They tossed them into bags, traded them in bundles, lined shelves with them, and wondered why so many emerged malformed or never emerged at all.

The shell remembers pressure.

If a Metapod is strapped too tightly, the wings may form twisted. If kept too dry, the internal body can adhere to the casing. If rolled, shaken, or dropped repeatedly, the developing nervous system may miswire. Such individuals sometimes emerge as Butterfree with unstable flight, damaged eyes, incomplete antennae, or strange behavioral dullness.

Old collectors called these â€œmoon-drunk Butterfree.â€

Modern researchers call them mishandled survivors.

Wild Metapod choose their hardening sites carefully. The ideal place is high enough to avoid ground predators, shaded enough to prevent drying, and hidden enough to escape birds. But mass evolution seasons change everything. When too many Caterpie transform at once, safe surfaces vanish. Metapod appear under bridges, inside barns, beneath church eaves, in attic rafters, behind shutters, under wagon seats, and once, famously, along the interior wall of a schoolhouse cloakroom.

That schoolhouse became part of Kanto warning lore.

The children thought the green shapes were toys.

By morning, thirty-seven Metapod had hardened onto coats, satchels, and hanging scarves. When the teacher tried to pull one free, the shell split. The internal body had already begun reformation. The smell made half the class ill. The surviving Metapod hardened so violently that several cracked their own casings. Only nine emerged later as healthy Butterfree.

After that, many rural schools began checking rafters during Caterpie season.

Metapod clusters create a specific kind of fear because they make familiar places feel occupied by silent witnesses. A barn full of Metapod does not sound infested. It sounds abandoned. Then one shell clicks. Then another. Not movement, exactly. Just tiny contractions in response to footsteps, lantern heat, or human voices. In darkness, a cluster may answer vibration with a wave of faint ticking, like fingernails tapping from inside walls.

This is why old houses with Metapod in the rafters were said to be â€œthinking.â€

They were not haunted.

They were waiting.

The shell is often mistaken for dead matter, but it is alive enough to bruise. If damaged, Metapod may seal small cracks with secreted resin, but larger fractures are catastrophic. A cracked Metapod attracts ants, mold, parasites, and opportunistic Bug-types. The creature cannot groom itself. It cannot flee infection. It cannot even angle its body away from rain. Once the shell opens too early, the developing form inside becomes a meal for the forest.

Some parasitic species specialize in this stage. Weedle will test soft seams. Venonat larvae may feed on leaked fluids. Certain fungi enter through shell fractures and consume the internal tissues without disturbing the exterior casing. Rangers sometimes find rows of Metapod still hanging perfectly intact, green and clean, with nothing inside but fungus threads.

Those groves are avoided.

Not because Metapod are dangerous there.

Because something has learned to eat transformation.

Despite their helplessness, Metapod are not passive in every sense. Their placement changes forests. Clusters draw predators, which draw larger predators. Their fallen shells enrich soil. Their failed bodies feed insects. Their successful emergence produces Butterfree that pollinate huge areas. In old ecological texts, Metapod is described as a â€œpause between appetites.â€ Caterpie eats the forest. Butterfree feeds the forest. Metapod pays the cost in stillness.

That cost can look like death.

Travelers sometimes find Metapod attached to grave markers, battlefield fences, or the branches above old roads where many Caterpie fed after disaster. Because of this, the chrysalis became a symbol of unfinished mourning. Families who lost someone during the Green Veil years sometimes hung carved Metapod charms near windows, not for luck, but as a request:

Stay whole until the change is done.

The field rules for Metapod are simple:

Do not shake it.

Do not peel it from a surface unless necessary.

Do not store it loose in a pack.

Do not assume stillness means death.

If the shell is dull and cold, leave it in place and mark the location.

If the shell clicks when touched, back away.

If a cluster begins clicking all at once, something larger than you is moving nearby.

And if you find a Metapod split open from the inside, empty and clean, look up before you celebrate.

Butterfree may have already emerged.

Or something else may have learned how to open them.
survivalUse: Can mark the timing of mass emergence, predator movement, recent Caterpie outbreaks, and healthy forest-edge humidity. Empty shells may indicate safe emergence, while cracked shells indicate parasite or predator activity.
handlingWarning: Do not shake, roll, strap tightly, peel, or store loose in packs. Repeated disturbance can exhaust hardening reflexes and damage wing, eye, or nervous-system development.
encounterAdvice: Leave attached specimens in place unless frost, fire, flooding, or direct human traffic will kill them. If relocation is unavoidable, move the whole branch, rail, or surface section without rotating the shell.
firstAid: For ruptured shells, avoid skin and eye contact with internal fluid, isolate from insects, keep upright and shaded, and contact a Bug-type veterinary handler. For human nausea from rupture odor, move to fresh air and wash exposed skin.
companionSuitability: Poor as an active companion; acceptable only as a protected transitional specimen for patient handlers
fieldBehaviors: Clicks faintly in response to vibration
Splits shell when Butterfree is ready
May fail emergence if repeatedly moved or dried
resistances: Light rain when shell is intact
signsOfPresence: Silk anchors from previous Caterpie stage
Predator chew marks around shell seams
equipment: Branch cutters for moving attachment surface
fieldNote: A clicking Metapod is alive and stressed. A cluster clicking together usually means vibration from a larger animal, storm movement, or human approach has reached the hardening site.
originStory:
Metapod was formally recognized in 1797, one year after Caterpie was documented during the Green Veil Summer.

Before that, Metapod was known, but misunderstood. Farmers found them on trees and called them hardworms. Children collected them as forest stones. Some villages believed they were seeds from trees that had been struck by lightning. Others believed they were failed Caterpie, punished by nature for eating too much.

The idea that Metapod was a living evolutionary stage came from a simple experiment that horrified the people who watched it.

During the Green Veil Summer of 1796, naturalist Iven Carrow marked several hundred Caterpie with harmless mineral dye to track their movement through the mulberry districts. His goal was to understand swarm spread, not evolution. But as the season progressed, many marked Caterpie climbed into rafters, tree forks, fence corners, and roof thatch. They stopped eating. They anchored themselves. Their skin tightened.

Then they hardened.

At first, Carrow assumed the marked Caterpie had died and been encased by some external resin. When he cut one open, he discovered living tissue inside, rearranging itself in a state he described as â€œneither larva nor corpse, but an argument between both.â€

That phrase was later removed from the official report.

His assistant kept it in the private notes.

Carrowâ€™s team confirmed that Metapod was not a separate species by observing marked Caterpie transform directly into the hardened form. The discovery forced naturalists to rethink PokÃ©mon evolution. This was not ordinary growth. It was not simple molting. It was a violent internal reorganization contained inside a temporary living shell.

The formal record was entered in 1797 after multiple marked Caterpie completed the transformation.

The early Caterpie-line chronology became:

1796: Caterpie formally recorded during the Green Veil Summer.

1797: Metapod confirmed after marked Caterpie hardened into chrysalis forms.

1802: Butterfree formally linked after tagged Metapod produced winged adults.

The delay between Metapod and Butterfree was caused by failure. Most of Carrowâ€™s early Metapod specimens died before emergence because researchers handled them incorrectly. They moved them too often, stored them indoors, rotated them for sketching, exposed them to lamp heat, and cut open several to study internal development. By modern standards, the early research was destructive and careless.

By the standards of the time, it was normal.

That may be worse.

The first successful observation came from a Metapod that Carrow did not touch.

It had attached itself to the underside of a broken fence rail near a mulberry orchard. The dye mark from its Caterpie stage was still visible as a faint blue stain near the lower shell. Carrow ordered the rail removed for transport. A local woman named Sera Dollen refused to let him take it. Her family had survived the sealed-house incident, and she insisted that moving hardened Caterpie killed them.

Carrow ignored much local advice, but by then he had lost enough specimens to listen.

The Metapod remained on the fence.

For nine days it did nothing.

On the tenth, the shell darkened.

On the eleventh, it clicked for nearly an hour after sunset.

On the twelfth morning, the casing split.

What emerged was not yet the beautiful Butterfree familiar to trainers. It was wet, folded, trembling, and almost grotesque. Its wings hung like soaked cloth. Its legs slipped on the rail. Its eyes were too large for the narrow head. For several minutes it looked less like a completed creature than a failed thought.

Then the wings filled.

Carrowâ€™s notes changed after that. He stopped calling Metapod inert. He began referring to it as the vulnerable intermediate. He admitted that the chrysalis was alive, aware enough to respond to danger, and highly dependent on orientation, humidity, and stability.

This became the foundation of modern Metapod handling.

The speciesâ€™ deeper origin is now linked to predator pressure. Ancient Caterpie ancestors were soft, slow, and heavily preyed upon. To survive long enough to reach winged maturity, they developed a hard transitional form. But the shell came with a tradeoff. Armor prevented escape. Safety required stillness. Stillness required camouflage and numbers.

This is why Metapod often appear in clusters.

A single Metapod is easy to find and break.

A hundred Metapod make predators choose.

The hardening reflex likely began as a simple muscular contraction beneath the skin. Over many generations, this became a specialized defensive system. Modern Metapod can temporarily increase shell rigidity far beyond its resting strength, but doing so costs energy. If threatened repeatedly, a Metapod may exhaust itself before emergence. This is why repeated trainer battles using only Harden are now discouraged in serious care manuals, even if they remain common among careless beginners.

A Metapod that hardens too often may survive the fight and fail the transformation.

That fact is not usually printed in childrenâ€™s training books.

The old villages learned it anyway.

They noticed that Metapod left alone emerged more cleanly. They noticed that clusters disturbed by children produced weak Butterfree. They noticed that Metapod removed from their chosen surfaces often failed. Over time, they built taboos around them: do not count hanging Metapod aloud, do not strike green shells with sticks, do not sleep under a clicking cluster, do not bring one indoors unless storm or frost will kill it.

Science later gave reasons for those rules.

The rules worked before science knew why.

Metapodâ€™s connection to mourning also began in this period. During the Green Veil Summer, many orchards, barns, and homes were covered in silent chrysalis forms after the worst infestations. People had lost crops, livestock, and in some cases family members. Then the same creatures that caused the damage hung motionless from the ruins, neither dead nor alive in any comforting way.

To grieving families, Metapod looked like unfinished souls.

When Butterfree finally emerged from some of those shells, the symbolism deepened. Metapod became associated with survival after stillness, but also with the fear that not every transformation succeeds. Empty shells were kept in some homes as charms. Failed shells were buried. Cracked shells were burned before parasites could spread.

Carrow dismissed most of this ritual in his public writing.

Privately, he kept one empty shell on his desk until his death.

It was from the fence-rail specimen that first proved the line.

Metapod remains one of the most underestimated PokÃ©mon because it appears weak. In truth, it represents one of the most dangerous biological strategies in nature: surrendering movement in exchange for becoming something predators cannot easily stop.

Caterpie is hunger.

Metapod is helplessness weaponized into armor.

Butterfree will be the beautiful thing that crawls out after the forest has already paid for it.

evolutionLine.name: Caterpie
evolutionLine.note: Leaf-feeding larval stage. Soft, fast-growing, and swarm-dependent. Silk output increases before hardening into Metapod.

evolutionLine.name: Metapod
evolutionLine.note: Living chrysalis stage. Motion is surrendered for shell defense while the body reorganizes toward winged emergence.

evolutionLine.name: Butterfree
evolutionLine.note: Winged adult pollinator and powder hazard. Its scales can carry sleep, paralysis, poison, and confusion risk in enclosed air.

mainLine.name: Caterpie
mainLine.note: Soft larval form. Eats leaves aggressively, climbs with suction-pad feet, releases defensive odor, and builds silk before hardening.

mainLine.name: Metapod
mainLine.note: Armored chrysalis. Its shell protects a soft, vulnerable internal body that is rebuilding itself for emergence.

mainLine.name: Butterfree
mainLine.note: Final winged pollinator. Locates nectar at great distance and sheds toxic, sleep-inducing, or stunning powders when stressed.

target.name: Gigantamax Butterfree
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat scale output, wind shear, and toxic powder dispersal as disaster-scale hazards.

humanConflictRisk: Low, but mishandling causes high mortality and malformed emergence
domesticationStatus: Managed transitional stage rather than a true domesticated companion
prey: No active prey or feeding during this stage; stored larval nutrients fuel transformation
territoryRange: No active territory. Attachment site is usually within the Caterpie feeding area and may be shared with dozens or hundreds of other Metapod.
lifeCycle: Forms when Caterpie evolves starting at Level 7, remains sealed while tissues reorganize, and evolves into Butterfree starting at Level 10 if the shell remains upright, stable, shaded, and intact.
containmentRequirements: Upright shaded mount, stable humidity, no shaking, no rolling, no tight straps, low-noise room, protective mesh against predators, and minimal observation handling
ecologicalRole: Connects the destructive larval feeding stage to the pollinating adult stage. Clusters feed predators, enrich soil through failed shells, and signal upcoming Butterfree emergence.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Cracks invite ants, mold, mites, parasitic larvae, and fungi that can consume internal tissue while the shell appears intact.
reproductionNesting: Does not reproduce. It is the sealed transitional stage between Caterpie and Butterfree.
predators: Pidgey, Spearow, Rattata, Beedrill, ants, parasitic insects, fungi, careless humans, and collectors
emergencyResponse: Mark and protect clusters in place. If shells are falling or exposed, move the attachment surface upright, provide shade, reduce vibration, and keep predators away until emergence.
legalStatus: Collection is discouraged during mass emergence periods; some ranger districts restrict transport of attached Metapod without a care permit
stressSicknessSigns: Failure to darken before expected emergence
Hardening contractions after every minor vibration
lifespan: The Metapod stage is short, usually days to a few weeks depending on conditions, before evolution into Butterfree or death from damage, predation, or infection.
socialStructure: Clustered but not social. Individuals share safe surfaces because Caterpie choose the same humidity, shade, and anchor conditions before hardening.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Defensive capacity is shell hardening and passive endurance.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Physical hazard is contact-range shell rupture or falling cluster impact.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: None; cannot move meaningfully once attached
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: None; relies on stillness and shell rigidity
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent only while properly anchored; poor if detached or rolled

trainingRisk: High risk of developmental damage if used for repeated Harden practice, pack transport, or careless beginner battles
physiology: Metapod encloses a soft reorganizing body inside a living hardened shell. The shell can stiffen under threat, but the internal tissues remain vulnerable to impact, drying, rotation, parasites, and premature cracking.

keyFacts.tailLength: No tail; shell length depends on attachment orientation
keyFacts.habitat: Shaded upright surfaces in forests, orchards, and rafters
keyFacts.lifespan: Short transitional stage before Level 10 evolution
keyFacts.groupSize: Single shells to dense hardening clusters

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. It is physically easy to contain but easy to harm. Proper orientation, humidity, and quiet matter more than trainer experience in battle.
physicalCharacteristics: Green crescent-shaped armored shell, narrow eyes, pointed ridges, hardened outer casing, soft hidden interior, and silk attachment remnants from the Caterpie stage.
dietRestrictions: Do not feed by mouth, soak, oil, heat, refrigerate, or force water through shell seams. The body is not built for active intake during this stage.
