before that, metapod was known, but misunderstood. farmers found them on trees and called them hardworms. children collected them as forest stones. some villages believed they were seeds from trees that had been struck by lightning. others believed they were failed caterpie, punished by nature for eating too much.

the idea that metapod was a living evolutionary stage came from a simple experiment that horrified the people who watched it.

during the green veil summer of 1796, naturalist iven carrow marked several hundred caterpie with harmless mineral dye to track their movement through the mulberry districts. his goal was to understand swarm spread, not evolution. but as the season progressed, many marked caterpie climbed into rafters, tree forks, fence corners, and roof thatch. they stopped eating. they anchored themselves. their skin tightened.

then they hardened.

at first, carrow assumed the marked caterpie had died and been encased by some external resin. when he cut one open, he discovered living tissue inside, rearranging itself in a state he described as â€œneither larva nor corpse, but an argument between both.â€

that phrase was later removed from the official report.

his assistant kept it in the private notes.

carrowâ€™s team confirmed that metapod was not a separate species by observing marked caterpie transform directly into the hardened form. the discovery forced naturalists to rethink pokã©mon evolution. this was not ordinary growth. it was not simple molting. it was a violent internal reorganization contained inside a temporary living shell.

the formal record was entered in 1797 after multiple marked caterpie completed the transformation.

the early caterpie-line chronology became:

1796: caterpie formally recorded during the green veil summer.

1797: metapod confirmed after marked caterpie hardened into chrysalis forms.

1802: butterfree formally linked after tagged metapod produced winged adults.

the delay between metapod and butterfree was caused by failure. most of carrowâ€™s early metapod specimens died before emergence because researchers handled them incorrectly. they moved them too often, stored them indoors, rotated them for sketching, exposed them to lamp heat, and cut open several to study internal development. by modern standards, the early research was destructive and careless.

by the standards of the time, it was normal.

that may be worse.

the first successful observation came from a metapod that carrow did not touch.

it had attached itself to the underside of a broken fence rail near a mulberry orchard. the dye mark from its caterpie stage was still visible as a faint blue stain near the lower shell. carrow ordered the rail removed for transport. a local woman named sera dollen refused to let him take it. her family had survived the sealed-house incident, and she insisted that moving hardened caterpie killed them.

carrow ignored much local advice, but by then he had lost enough specimens to listen.

the metapod remained on the fence.

for nine days it did nothing.

on the tenth, the shell darkened.

on the eleventh, it clicked for nearly an hour after sunset.

on the twelfth morning, the casing split.

what emerged was not yet the beautiful butterfree familiar to trainers. it was wet, folded, trembling, and almost grotesque. its wings hung like soaked cloth. its legs slipped on the rail. its eyes were too large for the narrow head. for several minutes it looked less like a completed creature than a failed thought.

then the wings filled.

carrowâ€™s notes changed after that. he stopped calling metapod inert. he began referring to it as the vulnerable intermediate. he admitted that the chrysalis was alive, aware enough to respond to danger, and highly dependent on orientation, humidity, and stability.

this became the foundation of modern metapod handling.

the speciesâ€™ deeper origin is now linked to predator pressure. ancient caterpie ancestors were soft, slow, and heavily preyed upon. to survive long enough to reach winged maturity, they developed a hard transitional form. but the shell came with a tradeoff. armor prevented escape. safety required stillness. stillness required camouflage and numbers.

this is why metapod often appear in clusters.

a single metapod is easy to find and break.

a hundred metapod make predators choose.

the hardening reflex likely began as a simple muscular contraction beneath the skin. over many generations, this became a specialized defensive system. modern metapod can temporarily increase shell rigidity far beyond its resting strength, but doing so costs energy. if threatened repeatedly, a metapod may exhaust itself before emergence. this is why repeated trainer battles using only harden are now discouraged in serious care manuals, even if they remain common among careless beginners.

a metapod that hardens too often may survive the fight and fail the transformation.

that fact is not usually printed in childrenâ€™s training books.

the old villages learned it anyway.

they noticed that metapod left alone emerged more cleanly. they noticed that clusters disturbed by children produced weak butterfree. they noticed that metapod removed from their chosen surfaces often failed. over time, they built taboos around them: do not count hanging metapod aloud, do not strike green shells with sticks, do not sleep under a clicking cluster, do not bring one indoors unless storm or frost will kill it.

science later gave reasons for those rules.

the rules worked before science knew why.

metapodâ€™s connection to mourning also began in this period. during the green veil summer, many orchards, barns, and homes were covered in silent chrysalis forms after the worst infestations. people had lost crops, livestock, and in some cases family members. then the same creatures that caused the damage hung motionless from the ruins, neither dead nor alive in any comforting way.

to grieving families, metapod looked like unfinished souls.

when butterfree finally emerged from some of those shells, the symbolism deepened. metapod became associated with survival after stillness, but also with the fear that not every transformation succeeds. empty shells were kept in some homes as charms. failed shells were buried. cracked shells were burned before parasites could spread.

carrow dismissed most of this ritual in his public writing.

privately, he kept one empty shell on his desk until his death.

it was from the fence-rail specimen that first proved the line.

metapod remains one of the most underestimated pokã©mon because it appears weak. in truth, it represents one of the most dangerous biological strategies in nature: surrendering movement in exchange for becoming something predators cannot easily stop.

caterpie is hunger.

metapod is helplessness weaponized into armor.

butterfree will be the beautiful thing that crawls out after the forest has already paid for it.",

name: Pidgeot
route: High-canopy Kanto and Johto forest territories, coastal hunting lanes, river mouths with Magikarp activity, Kalos woodland ridges, old-growth parks, cliff thermals, and remote roost trees above established Pidgey and Pidgeotto ranges
habitat: Large mature forest, coastal bluffs, high ridges, tall roost trees, open water edges, and broad hunting corridors with enough airspace for high-speed dive recovery
classification: Bird Pokemon / Final Stage High-Speed Aerial Predator
behaviorTemperament: Confident, intelligent, territorial, and visually oriented. Wild adults may ignore distant humans but react strongly to nest intrusion, prey theft, wounded flock members, drones, or aircraft-like noise.
diet: Fish such as Magikarp, rodents, large insects, small birds, reptiles, carrion, berries during low prey periods, and high-energy managed feed
activityPattern: Diurnal with dawn thermals, midday high-altitude patrols, and fast evening hunting passes over water or open fields.
bestTimeToFind: Dawn and late afternoon near cliff updrafts, high canopy roosts, river mouths, lake edges, and old forest meadows where smaller birds avoid the sky
observationDifficulty: Hard. Adults patrol from high altitude and may be noticed only after prey animals panic, shadows pass over the field, or sudden wind hits the grass.
conservationStatus: Protected final-stage aerial predator
populationStatus: Rare and locally sensitive. Adults require large territories, mature roost trees, and prey-rich hunting corridors.
description: The final form of the Pidgey line, a powerful Normal and Flying-type aerial predator with sweeping wings, high-speed pursuit ability, and talons capable of turning a hunting pass into a medical emergency.
lore: Kanto lighthouse keepers gave the first consistent accounts of Pidgeot: a golden-crested bird arriving with the morning wind, dropping over the water, and leaving with a Magikarp before the splash closed. People admired the plumage, then feared the wings. A startled Pidgeot can turn a quiet trail into flying bark, grit, and broken branches with only a few beats. Old ranger ledgers list broken fingers from young adults, facial lacerations from nesting pairs, and search teams scattered by sudden gale pressure under the canopy. A calm distant Pidgeot signals a stable aerial territory. A Pidgeot flying directly toward you is not weather. It is a decision.
survivalUse: Can perform long-range scouting, aerial messaging, storm warning, predator deterrence, prey retrieval, and emergency route finding from above the canopy.
handlingWarning: Do not approach roost cliffs, nest trees, downed chicks, or fresh kills. Avoid drones, loose flags, shining lures, and aircraft-like buzzing near its territory.
encounterAdvice: Move under cover, protect eyes and neck, secure loose gear, and retreat slowly out of open space. If it begins repeated dives, lie low beside a tree trunk or rock until it breaks off.
firstAid: Treat talon wounds as deep puncture trauma. Control bleeding, irrigate, immobilize injured limbs, and seek urgent medical care. Wind-thrown debris can cause eye injury and concussion.
companionSuitability: Excellent for elite handlers with aerial facilities; poor for casual ownership
fieldBehaviors: Escorts trained handlers over long routes
signsOfPresence: Deep talon gouges on branch bark
High scream calls at territory edge
fieldNote: A high circling Pidgeot is surveying. A low silent Pidgeot that folds its wings, fixes both eyes on one target, and drops below the tree line is beginning a strike.
originStory: Pidgeot is the completed outcome of the Pidgey line. Pidgey survives by memory and dust. Pidgeotto survives by territory and talons. Pidgeot survives by owning the air itself. Evolution at Level 36 appears to require final expansion of the chest, air sacs, wing joints, optic nerves, and cardiovascular system. In habitats where Pidgey once hid from every predator, a resident Pidgeot reverses the relationship and decides when the forest moves.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgey
evolutionLine.note: Small grass-edge bird. It survives by hiding, homing back to safe nests, and throwing sand or dust with rapid wingbeats.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgeotto
evolutionLine.note: Territorial patrol hunter. Stronger wings, sharper vision, and developed talons let it carry prey and defend a broad range.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgeot
evolutionLine.method: Final stage; Mega Evolution with Pidgeotite
evolutionLine.note: Final aerial predator. Large wings, extreme speed, and heavy talons make it a serious field and airspace hazard.

mainLine.name: Pidgey
mainLine.note: Tiny field bird. Common in tall grass, forest edges, parks, farms, and early travel routes.

mainLine.name: Pidgeotto
mainLine.note: Large territorial bird. Patrols wide hunting lanes and attacks intruders near nests.

mainLine.name: Pidgeot
mainLine.note: High-speed aerial predator. Rules large canopy, cliff, river, and coastal hunting territories.

target.name: Mega Pidgeot
target.note: Temporary form with greater speed, precision, and storm-force wing control. Treat as extreme aerial hazard.

domesticationStatus: Elite trained aerial companion in controlled programs, otherwise wild protected predator
prey: Magikarp, rodents, large insects, reptiles, small birds, weakened prey animals, carrion, and occasional berries
territoryRange: Usually 10 to 40 square miles around roosts, hunting water, thermal ridges, and nesting trees
lifeCycle: Evolves from Pidgeotto starting at Level 36 after full flight maturity. Adults pair, claim large roost territories, and may Mega Evolve temporarily with Pidgeotite under rare trained or battle conditions.
containmentRequirements: Large flight mews, high reinforced perches, open training field, professional falconry-style equipment, GPS tracking, double-gate entry, prey-safe barriers, and no public access to nest areas
ecologicalRole: Top-level aerial predator that controls fish, rodents, and rival flying-type pressure while shaping the behavior of smaller birds across entire ecosystems.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Large bird parasites, respiratory fungal disease, crop trauma, stress feather damage, and talon infections are concerns. Human injuries from claws and debris are more common than disease transmission.
reproductionNesting: Nests high in old trees, cliff ledges, and protected canopy platforms. Pair bonds are often strong during breeding. Chicks remain vulnerable to cold rain and nest disturbance until flight muscles mature.
predators: Stronger electric-types, ice-types, rock-types, rival Pidgeot, Fearow groups, humans, and rare apex flying or dragon predators
emergencyResponse: Clear people from open ground, stop drones or vehicles causing agitation, secure loose objects, and shelter under hard cover. For injured humans, prioritize bleeding control and evacuation from dive lanes.
legalStatus: Final-stage aerial predator permits required in most settled areas. Nest disturbance, feather trade, and unlicensed transport are typically restricted.
lifespan: Wild: 8 to 18 years. Managed care: 25 to 35 years with expert veterinary support and safe flight range.
socialStructure: Usually mated pairs or solitary adults over a territory. Pidgey and Pidgeotto may forage under the safety umbrella of a dominant adult range.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Wind shear, talons, sonic pressure, and dive impact are primary hazards.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Dangerous wing-pressure and debris zone can reach 30 to 100 ft depending on terrain.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Extreme dive speed; field guidance treats direct high-speed passes as vehicle-level impact risk
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Exceptional in open air, moderate in tight forest
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Powerful ground or perch launch, often 6 to 10 ft before full wing lift
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent in flight, high perches, and cliff winds

trainingRisk: Dive injury, handler laceration, overbonding, airspace conflict, wing damage from poor facilities, prey-drive accidents, and public safety violations
physiology: Pidgeot has a broad-winged avian body, large flight muscles, high-capacity air sacs, sharp binocular tracking, and crest feathers used for display. Its talons and beak are dangerous, but its most unusual field feature is the ability to move large volumes of air with precise wingbeats.

keyFacts.wingspan: Approx. 9 to 12 ft in field specimens
keyFacts.lifestyle: Final-stage aerial predator and high-territory guardian
keyFacts.diet: Fish, rodents, birds, reptiles, large insects, and high-energy managed feed
keyFacts.habitat: Old forests, cliffs, open water edges, ridges, and broad hunting lanes
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 8 to 18 years. Managed: 25 to 35 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary adult or mated pair, with tolerated lower stages nearby

domesticationDifficulty: Hard. Requires elite training, high space, professional equipment, and legal authorization. Not suitable for casual companions or small homes.
physicalCharacteristics: Large brown-and-cream bird with powerful wings, colorful crest feathers, long tail plumage, sharp talons, keen eyes, and a broad chest built for sustained high-speed flight.
dietRestrictions: Avoid spoiled meat, poor calcium balance, confinement without flight, smoke, lead fragments in prey, tangled synthetic cord, and overtraining during molt.

attacks.name: Growl
attacks.fieldUse: Threat display and flock communication that can interrupt a smaller animal before contact.

attacks.name: Tackle
attacks.fieldUse: Short body strike used at ground level or during low flight.

attacks.name: Gust
attacks.fieldUse: Wing-driven air burst that throws dust, leaves, loose grit, and small targets.

attacks.name: Quick Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Sudden low pass used to strike or break contact before a threat reacts.

attacks.name: Twister
attacks.fieldUse: Tight rotating air column produced by rapid wing coordination.

attacks.name: Wing Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Direct wing strike capable of bruising, knocking down, or stunning small creatures.

attacks.name: Whirlwind
attacks.fieldUse: Powerful displacement burst used to drive intruders out of nesting space.

attacks.name: Agility
attacks.fieldUse: Brief surge of coordination and flight speed.

attacks.name: Air Slash
attacks.fieldUse: Compressed cutting air from a fast wing stroke.

attacks.name: Feather Dance
attacks.fieldUse: Distracting plumage display that disrupts a predator or rival.

attacks.name: Hurricane
attacks.fieldUse: Violent air vortex. Dangerous around loose gear, branches, and people.

attacks.name: Brave Bird
attacks.fieldUse: High commitment dive impact with severe injury risk to target and user.

attacks.name: Aerial Ace
attacks.fieldUse: Accurate turning strike used against evasive targets.

attacks.name: Fly
attacks.fieldUse: High climb followed by a descending strike or transport movement.

attacks.name: U-turn
attacks.fieldUse: Hit-and-leave maneuver used to retreat back toward cover or flock support.

attacks.name: Heat Wave
attacks.fieldUse: Rare trained technique that pushes hot dry air through a wingbeat front.

Kanto: Routes 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 12, 13, 14, 15, 24, and 25 in many Kanto records
Viridian Forest and nearby forest-edge grassland in related game records
Early travel corridors between Pallet Town, Viridian City, Pewter City, and Cerulean City
Johto: Ilex Forest
National Park north of Goldenrod City, especially morning and daytime grass
Kalos: Santalune Forest and nearby beginner-route woodland
realWorldBlend: Temperate oak, cedar, and maple woodland similar to Olympic foothill forests
Urban-edge parks, berry hedges, old farms, powerline cuts, river margins, and coastal forest edges
FinalStageNotes: Most stable Pidgeot records come from evolution rather than common wild capture.
Adults are expected above mature Pidgey and Pidgeotto ranges rather than in beginner grass itself.
Coastal and river hunting lanes are likely where the species searches for fish such as Magikarp.

officialEvolutionSummary.pidgeyToPidgeotto: Pidgey evolves into Pidgeotto starting at Level 18.
officialEvolutionSummary.pidgeottoToPidgeot: Pidgeotto evolves into Pidgeot starting at Level 36.
officialEvolutionSummary.specialForm: Pidgeot can Mega Evolve into Mega Pidgeot with Pidgeotite where Mega Evolution is available.

sourceUseNote: Core typing, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, and move names are based on official-style Pokedex/game database records. Lifespan, field medicine, containment, habitat ecology, and real-world behavior are survival-guide extrapolations.

 pokemon-pidgeot.json pokemon-pidgeot pidgeot

name: Pidgeotto
route: Kanto forest routes and open fields, Johto Route 37 and National Park airspace, Ilex Forest margins, Kalos beginner-route woodland edges, farm corridors, river roads, and ridge-top hunting lanes
habitat: Large territories with high roosts, open hunting corridors, dense prey shrubs, clear wind lanes, and safe nest trees away from heavy foot traffic
behaviorTemperament: Alert, proud, and strongly territorial. Pidgeotto patrols rather than hides. It may tolerate quiet travelers at the edge of its range but attacks near nests, captured Pidgey, or prey sites.
diet: Insects, rodents, small reptiles, Exeggcute-sized prey, berries, carrion scraps, and fish taken near shallow water
activityPattern: Diurnal with strong morning patrols and late-afternoon hunting passes.
bestTimeToFind: Clear mornings along meadow edges, river bends, orchard rows, tall fence posts, and high branches overlooking grass where smaller Pidgey suddenly scatter
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Large enough to spot in flight, but difficult to approach because it notices movement from high altitude.
conservationStatus: Protected territorial bird-type species
populationStatus: Common in healthy forest-edge habitats, less common near cities where nest removal, window strikes, and illegal feather collection reduce breeding success.
description: The evolved hunting stage of Pidgey, with strong vision, developed claws, and a broad territorial patrol range.
lore: In Johto farming towns, a lone Pidgeotto circling over a field was welcomed because it meant rodents and crop insects would be cleared by dusk. That respect changed whenever a nest tree was cut. Injury maps from old ranger ledgers show territory edges drawn by scratched arms, torn hats, missing messenger birds, and sudden silence around nesting oaks. Modern guides call Pidgeotto the warning stage of the family. If it circles you without calling, you have already entered the map it keeps in its head.
survivalUse: Can scout weather fronts, flush prey, guide travelers by homing behavior, warn of intruders, and control rodents or insect swarms near farms.
handlingWarning: Never approach a nest tree, collect feathers below an active roost, throw stones, or handle Pidgey near an adult. Keep face and eyes protected if it circles low.
encounterAdvice: Stop advancing, lower your profile, cover your eyes, and back away along your original route. Do not run across open ground because it may trigger pursuit.
firstAid: For talon cuts, control bleeding, wash thoroughly, and seek care for deep punctures. Eye injuries from grit or wing pressure need irrigation and medical review.
companionSuitability: Fair for experienced handlers only; loyal when raised carefully but unsuitable for cramped homes
signsOfPresence: Pidgey flocks avoiding a marked area
equipment: Flag tape to mark territory edge
fieldNote: A Pidgeotto flying lazy circles may be hunting. A Pidgeotto that circles silently, lowers its legs, and folds its wings for repeated short dives is defending territory.
originStory: Pidgeotto is the pressure point where a harmless grass bird becomes a territorial predator. Larger Pidgey gained access to richer prey and safer nest trees. The body enlarged, talons strengthened, and homing memory expanded into a defended patrol range. Pidgeotto evolves from Pidgey starting at Level 18 because the juvenile must develop enough muscle and navigation to hunt alone. It evolves into Pidgeot starting at Level 36 when the skeleton, heart, air sacs, and flight feathers can support long-range high-speed pursuit.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgey
evolutionLine.note: Small grass-edge bird. It survives by hiding, homing back to safe nests, and throwing sand or dust with rapid wingbeats.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgeotto
evolutionLine.note: Territorial patrol hunter. Stronger wings, sharper vision, and developed talons let it carry prey and defend a broad range.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgeot
evolutionLine.method: Final stage; Mega Evolution with Pidgeotite
evolutionLine.note: Final aerial predator. Large wings, extreme speed, and heavy talons make it a serious field and airspace hazard.

mainLine.name: Pidgey
mainLine.note: Tiny field bird. Common in tall grass, forest edges, parks, farms, and early travel routes.

mainLine.name: Pidgeotto
mainLine.note: Large territorial bird. Patrols wide hunting lanes and attacks intruders near nests.

mainLine.name: Pidgeot
mainLine.note: High-speed aerial predator. Rules large canopy, cliff, river, and coastal hunting territories.

target.name: Mega Pidgeot
target.note: Temporary form with greater speed, precision, and storm-force wing control. Treat as extreme aerial hazard.

domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated hunting and messenger species, but territorial adults often require permits
prey: Rodents, large insects, Exeggcute-sized prey, small reptiles, fledgling birds, berries, and carrion scraps
territoryRange: Usually 1 to 8 square miles depending on prey density, nest height, and breeding pressure
lifeCycle: Develops from Pidgey after Level 18, leaves the low grass flock, claims a hunting loop, and evolves into Pidgeot starting at Level 36 after reaching full flight endurance.
containmentRequirements: Large outdoor aviary, high perches, protected flight corridor, reinforced nest platform, double-door entry, visual barriers, and separation from small prey species
ecologicalRole: Controls rodents and large insects, regulates small bird populations, spreads berry seeds, and shapes where Pidgey flocks can forage safely.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Talon wounds may infect easily. Feather mites, respiratory disease, crop impaction, and stress feather-plucking occur in poor housing.
reproductionNesting: Builds or takes over high nest platforms in old trees, barns, cliff shrubs, and tall park evergreens. Adults defend the nest and patrol farther during chick growth.
predators: Fearow, Arbok, larger Pidgeot, electric-types, ice-types, humans, and rival raptors competing for roosts
emergencyResponse: During an attack, cover eyes and head, retreat under tree cover or a vehicle, and leave the territory edge. Clean punctures and seek care.
legalStatus: Territorial bird-type permit recommended for adult keeping, falconry-style handling, transport, or nest relocation.
lifespan: Wild: 3 to 8 years. Managed care: 12 to 18 years if flown safely and kept in clean aviary conditions.
socialStructure: Pairs or small family groups during nesting, otherwise solitary patrol behavior over a loose network of Pidgey flocks.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Wind force, talons, and beak impact are primary hazards.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Dive and wing-pressure hazard about 15 to 40 ft around the bird.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast low dive about 45 to 70 mph in short attack passes
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent around trees and fence lines
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Powerful perch launch, usually 3 to 6 ft before wing lift
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent aerial and perch balance

trainingRisk: Talon injury, territorial aggression, broken feathers, escape pursuit, window strike, handler eye injury, and prey-drive accidents around small animals
physiology: Pidgeotto has a larger chest, stronger flight muscles, expanded air sacs, sharper talons, and far better long-distance vision than Pidgey. Its brain appears specialized for territory mapping and motion tracking.

keyFacts.wingspan: Approx. 5 to 7 ft in field specimens
keyFacts.lifestyle: Diurnal territorial raptor and patrol hunter
keyFacts.diet: Small prey, insects, berries, and carrion scraps
keyFacts.habitat: Forest edges, farms, parks, high roosts, and open hunting lanes
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 3 to 8 years. Managed: 12 to 18 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually pairs, solitary adults, or a family group around a nest

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate to hard. Best with experienced bird-type handlers. Requires space, flight work, prey control, and respect for territory boundaries.
physicalCharacteristics: Large brown-and-cream raptor-like bird with red crest feathers, sharp eyes, pinkish beak and legs, developed talons, broad wings, and red-yellow tail plumage.
dietRestrictions: Avoid overfeeding fatty scraps, spoiled meat, moldy grain, cramped cages, smoke exposure, loose synthetic strings, and long-term confinement without flight.

name: Pidgey
route: Kanto and Johto beginner-route tall grass, Viridian Forest edges, Ilex Forest, National Park meadows, Kalos Route 2 and Route 3 woodlots, Santalune Forest margins, farm hedges, berry thickets, and urban-edge parks
habitat: Temperate grass, low brush, young forest, field margins, orchard edges, fence lines, and sheltered nesting shrubs with dry dust or sand patches nearby
classification: Tiny Bird Pokemon / Base Stage Directional Field Bird
behaviorTemperament: Usually docile, watchful, and conflict-avoidant. Wild Pidgey hide in grass and use wing-thrown dust or sand to blind attackers long enough to escape. Cornered nest birds can still strike back with pecks and wing slaps.
diet: Seeds, berries, tender shoots, insects, worms, loose grain, orchard pests, and occasional settlement scraps
activityPattern: Diurnal. Most active in morning and late afternoon, with midday rest in shrubs or low branches.
bestTimeToFind: Morning light in tall grass, field paths, berry hedges, forest road edges, and quiet park margins where dry dust shows small three-toed tracks
observationDifficulty: Easy. Common, vocal, and visible in open grass, but resting birds blend well with leaf litter.
conservationStatus: Common but protected from large-scale nest clearing in many settled regions
populationStatus: Stable around farms, parks, and lowland forest. Local groups decline when hedgerows are removed or feral predators pressure nest sites.
description: A small Normal and Flying-type field bird known for homing instinct, docile behavior, and defensive dust-flinging wingbeats.
lore: Old Kanto trail wardens once treated sudden dust clouds in grass as a weather sign until they found neat circular scatter marks around berry shrubs and empty insect burrows. The birds responsible were so plain that most travelers ignored them, but messenger handlers soon learned that a Pidgey released far from home could return with unsettling accuracy. Villages trained flocks for messages and weather warnings, while predators learned to follow those same routes back to human food stores. A calm Pidgey is a sign of healthy habitat. A silent cluster of hidden Pidgey often means a larger hunter is already nearby.
survivalUse: Can help locate trails, flush insects, warn of predators, carry light message tags, and reveal wind direction through flock movement.
handlingWarning: Do not grab from above, squeeze the chest, or hold the wings shut. Keep eyes protected when handling because dust bursts can blind humans briefly.
encounterAdvice: Crouch low, approach from the side, and leave a clear escape path. If multiple Pidgey go silent at once, stop and scan for a larger predator or evolved form.
firstAid: Flush dust or grit from eyes with clean water. Treat scratches as contaminated bird wounds. Check for eye abrasion after repeated dust bursts.
companionSuitability: Good for beginner handlers, message work, and low-risk field observation
fieldBehaviors: Kicks up dust with rapid wingbeats
Returns to familiar nests from long distance
signsOfPresence: Loose sand sprayed outward from cover
equipment: Field notebook for homing release points
fieldNote: A Pidgey that hides but keeps calling is usually avoiding contact. A Pidgey flock that goes silent and points its attention upward is often warning of a larger flying predator.
originStory: The Pidgey line likely began as small forest-edge seed birds that survived by memorizing landmarks, returning to safe nests, and kicking dust into predator eyes. Human roads, farms, and berry hedges expanded its habitat. Raptor pressure favored stronger wings, sharper vision, and larger defended territories. Pidgey evolves into Pidgeotto starting at Level 18 when the body can support patrol flight and active prey capture. Pidgeotto evolves into Pidgeot starting at Level 36 when the chest, air sacs, heart, and flight feathers can support high-speed hunting.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgey
evolutionLine.note: Small grass-edge bird. It survives by hiding, homing back to safe nests, and throwing sand or dust with rapid wingbeats.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgeotto
evolutionLine.note: Territorial patrol hunter. Stronger wings, sharper vision, and developed talons let it carry prey and defend a broad range.

evolutionLine.name: Pidgeot
evolutionLine.method: Final stage; Mega Evolution with Pidgeotite
evolutionLine.note: Final aerial predator. Large wings, extreme speed, and heavy talons make it a serious field and airspace hazard.

mainLine.name: Pidgey
mainLine.note: Tiny field bird. Common in tall grass, forest edges, parks, farms, and early travel routes.

mainLine.name: Pidgeotto
mainLine.note: Large territorial bird. Patrols wide hunting lanes and attacks intruders near nests.

mainLine.name: Pidgeot
mainLine.note: High-speed aerial predator. Rules large canopy, cliff, river, and coastal hunting territories.

target.name: Mega Pidgeot
target.note: Temporary form with greater speed, precision, and storm-force wing control. Treat as extreme aerial hazard.

domesticationStatus: Common companion and field messenger species
prey: Insects, worms, small grubs, berries, grain, and fallen fruit
territoryRange: Usually 0.1 to 0.5 square miles around a nest cluster, food patch, and dust-bathing area
lifeCycle: Hatches in a shrub or low-tree nest, follows adults through grass, develops homing memory and dust-defense behavior, and evolves into Pidgeotto starting at Level 18 with adequate flight strength.
containmentRequirements: Quiet aviary, low perches, clean dust-bath tray, soft flight netting, predator-proof nest box, fresh water, and enough room for short flight loops
ecologicalRole: Controls insects, spreads berry seeds, warns small animals of predators, and serves as prey for larger raptors, snakes, and evolved bird Pokemon.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Feather mites, respiratory irritation from dirty dust baths, crop infection, and Salmonella-like contamination are the main concerns.
reproductionNesting: Builds small nest cups in shrubs, hedges, low trees, and sheltered park plantings. Eggs need steady warmth, low disturbance, and protection from ground predators.
predators: Spearow, Fearow, Ekans, Arbok, Persian, Meowth, larger Pidgeotto, humans, and nest-raiding Bug or Poison types
emergencyResponse: For eye exposure, stop moving, rinse with clean water, and avoid rubbing. For trapped indoor Pidgey, darken the room, open one exit, and do not chase it.
legalStatus: Generally legal to observe and keep in small numbers where bird-type permits allow. Nest removal is restricted during breeding season.
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 4 years, often shorter due to predation. Managed care: 8 to 12 years with clean housing and safe flight space.
socialStructure: Loose flocks with pair bonds during nesting. Juveniles gather in grass clusters and use alarm calls for coordinated escape.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Air pressure and dust are the main hazards.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Dust burst hazard usually 3 to 8 ft.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Quick ground launch about 15 to 25 mph in short escape bursts
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High for its size, especially through brush
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Low hop launch, usually under 2 ft before wing lift
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent branch and fence-line balance

trainingRisk: Eye injury from dust, stress fractures from poor handling, escape loss, predator panic, and respiratory illness in dirty enclosures
physiology: Pidgey has a lightweight avian body, strong homing sense, sharp eyes, and wings able to move loose grit with surprising force. It relies on warning, memory, dust defense, and flock awareness more than direct combat.

keyFacts.wingspan: Approx. 1.8 to 2.4 ft in field specimens
keyFacts.lifestyle: Diurnal flock bird and grass-edge forager
keyFacts.diet: Seeds, berries, insects, and small invertebrates
keyFacts.habitat: Tall grass, forest edge, parks, hedges, and farms
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 4 years. Managed: 8 to 12 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually pairs, small flocks, or loose grass clusters

domesticationDifficulty: Easy to moderate. Friendly when raised young, but needs flight space and calm handling. Wild-caught birds panic easily in cages.
physicalCharacteristics: Small brown bird with cream face and underside, dark eye markings, short beak, pinkish feet, compact wings, and soft feathering suited for brush camouflage.
dietRestrictions: Avoid moldy grain, salty human food, smoke-filled rooms, oily seeds as a staple, and dusty bedding that irritates breathing.

  "lore": "old kanto trail wardens once treated sudden dust clouds in grass as a weather sign until they found neat circular scatter marks around berry shrubs and empty insect burrows. the birds responsible were so plain that most travelers ignored them, but messenger handlers soon learned that a pidgey released far from home could return with unsettling accuracy. villages trained flocks for messages and weather warnings, while predators learned to follow those same routes back to human food stores. a calm pidgey is a sign of healthy habitat. a silent cluster of hidden pidgey often means a larger hunter is already nearby.",
  "survivaluse": "can help locate trails, flush insects, warn of predators, carry light message tags, and reveal wind direction through flock movement.",
  "handlingwarning": "do not grab from above, squeeze the chest, or hold the wings shut. keep eyes protected when handling because dust bursts can blind humans briefly.",
  "encounteradvice": "crouch low, approach from the side, and leave a clear escape path. if multiple pidgey go silent at once, stop and scan for a larger predator or evolved form.",
  "firstaid": "flush dust or grit from eyes with clean water. treat scratches as contaminated bird wounds. check for eye abrasion after repeated dust bursts.",
  "companionsuitability": "good for beginner handlers, message work, and low-risk field observation",

name: Pikachu
route: Forest edges, grassy routes, storm-prone woodland
habitat: Woodlands, open grass, abandoned power areas, and regions with frequent electrical activity.
behaviorTemperament: Alert, social, curious, and skittish when surprised.
diet: Berries, fruit, seeds, and small field foods.
activityPattern: Mostly diurnal, but activity increases before storms.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning, late afternoon, or storm weather.
observationDifficulty: Moderate
conservationStatus: Stable in known regions
populationStatus: Stable
description: A small electric creature known for storing electrical charge in its cheek sacs.
lore: Pikachu are alert, social, and highly reactive when frightened.
survivalUse: Can warn of storms and electrical disturbance.
handlingWarning: Do not grab barehanded. Avoid cheeks and tail.
encounterAdvice: Keep distance, stay calm, avoid sudden movement.
firstAid: Treat shock as serious and seek medical help.
companionSuitability: Potential companion with careful trust-building
fieldNote: If injured, keep calm, dry, and away from metal tools.
originStory: Early ranger accounts place the first confirmed Pikachu encounter near storm-charged caves.

name: Raticate
region: Kanto, Johto, Sinnoh, Unova, Alola, and food-rich settlement corridors
route: Kanto Routes 16, 17, 18, 21 and Pokemon Mansion records; Johto Routes 7, 9, 10, 26, 27, 38, 39, 42, 43, 47, Burned Tower, Mt. Mortar, Tohjo Falls, and Union Cave; Sinnoh Routes 225 and 226 with spacious cave habitats; Unova Dreamyard, Relic Passage, and Strange House records; Alolan routes, cities, beaches, and outskirts tied to the regional line
habitat: Riverbanks, sewers, barns, grain stores, abandoned mansions, ruined towers, cave passages, ferry ports, old mills, bridge supports, coastal villages, and field edges with both food and hard material for tooth wear
classification: Mouse Pokemon / Final Stage River-Crossing Gnawer
behaviorTemperament: Aggressive, food-driven, and more confident than Rattata. Raticate will bluff, hiss, lunge, and bite if its whiskers, nest, young, or food cache are threatened. Its swimming ability lets it escape across rivers and raid islands or ships.
diet: Grain, fruit, roots, insects, eggs, fish, small mammals, carrion, stored food, refuse, bark, hard seeds, and anything that helps grind its teeth
activityPattern: Nocturnal to crepuscular, with daytime movement near water, ruins, and heavy cover. Adults become bold in quiet buildings or during food shortage.
bestTimeToFind: Late evening near river crossings, gnawed beams, food stores, ferry docks, old mansion walls, cave pools, and ruined tower floors with thick tooth marks
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Large enough to detect by tracks and damage, but smart enough to avoid open contact unless guarding food or a nest.
conservationStatus: Common nuisance and infrastructure-damage species; managed rather than protected in many settled areas
populationStatus: Stable to high near farms, ports, and routes. Adults decline where sanitation, predators, and hard exclusion barriers are maintained.
description: A large Normal-type mouse Pokemon with constantly growing fangs, balance-sensitive whiskers, webbed hind feet, and enough bite force to damage wood, wall material, and soft stone.
lore: Raticate became infamous in Kanto when early road crews realized that bridge damage near Routes 16 through 18 was not weathering. The beams had been tested from below, shaved night after night by adults crossing between food corridors. Johto temple records describe a different problem. Raticate in old towers did not merely feed. They changed the building, cutting hidden routes through floors, nesting between shrine walls, and using the noise of wind bells to cover movement. The most dangerous adult is not the loudest one. It is the quiet animal that has already mapped exits, water, food, and the human schedule. In Alola, the regional line sharpened this behavior into organized night foraging under dominant adults. Standard Raticate are less ceremonial but just as practical. They gnaw, swim, breed, and survive. A settlement that ignores one adult may inherit a colony.
survivalUse: Can indicate hidden water routes, structural weakness, food contamination, and old tunnels. In wilderness survival, a Raticate trail can lead to grain caches, river exits, or sheltered crawlways.
handlingWarning: Never touch the whiskers, offer fingers near the mouth, or assume a friendly posture means safe handling. Even calm Raticate may bite if their facial whiskers are brushed or their food cache is moved.
encounterAdvice: Do not challenge at a nest entrance. Back away slowly, keep packs closed, avoid kneeling near its face, and watch for smaller Rattata moving behind you toward food.
firstAid: Treat bites as high-risk punctures. Flush, disinfect, bandage, and seek medical care for deep wounds, hand bites, tendon pain, numbness, fever, or signs of infection.
companionSuitability: Poor for homes, useful only for experienced handlers with secure gnaw-proof containment and pest-control training
fieldBehaviors: Gnaws logs, rocks, walls, and pipes
Swims across rivers with webbed hind feet
Commands or displaces smaller Rattata around food
Expands dens through walls and soft stone
signsOfPresence: Whisker rub marks on dusty wall gaps
fieldNote: Whiskers are not decorative. If damaged, balance and temperament worsen quickly. A Raticate that lets you near its face may still bite reflexively if a whisker is brushed.
originStory: Raticate is the adult answer to the Rattata strategy. Where the base form survives by speed and numbers, the evolved form survives by access. Bigger teeth open barriers. Webbed hind feet cross rivers. Stronger jaws defend caches. Whiskers maintain balance in darkness and tight spaces. Level 20 evolution marks the shift from scattered juvenile scavenger to adult route-maker. In real-world terms, this line evolved beside human movement: roads, barns, towers, ships, caves, bridges, and waste streams. Every human shortcut became a Rattata corridor, and every corridor eventually produced Raticate large enough to defend it.

evolutionLine.name: Rattata
evolutionLine.note: Base stage mouse Pokemon. Evolves through rapid growth once its teeth, senses, and food drive can support adult territory competition.

evolutionLine.name: Raticate
evolutionLine.note: Adult stage mouse Pokemon. Larger fangs, stronger jaws, webbed hind feet, and broader range let it cross water and claim food corridors.

mainLine.name: Rattata
mainLine.note: Small, fast, highly adaptable gnawing rodent. Lives wherever food, cover, and nesting gaps exist.

mainLine.name: Raticate
mainLine.methodToNext: Final stage; regional form variants only
mainLine.note: Large adult river-crossing rodent. Strong fangs, webbed hind feet, and aggressive territory defense make it a serious settlement pest.

target.name: Alolan Rattata
target.note: Night-active urban forager adapted to settlement raiding, fresh food theft, and boss-led nest behavior.

target.name: Alolan Raticate
target.note: Large command animal of night-raiding nests. Directs groups of Rattata toward food sources and defends stored food aggressively.

domesticationStatus: Undomesticated nuisance species with limited trained use in controlled pest-detection programs
prey: Insects, eggs, fish, small mammals, young birds, carrion scraps, grain pests, and smaller Pokemon when food is scarce
territoryRange: Usually 0.5 to 2 square miles around food, water crossings, nest chambers, and hard gnawing surfaces. Urban adults may hold several buildings or an entire dockside lane.
lifeCycle: Rattata evolves into Raticate starting at Level 20. Adults claim routes, caches, river crossings, and nest cavities, then breed where food and gnawing surfaces can support young.
containmentRequirements: Large chew-proof metal enclosure, lockable service doors, concrete or steel-lined edges, tooth-wear blocks, water basin, covered hide, double food containment, and strict waste removal
ecologicalRole: Consumes carrion and waste, controls insects and small pests, disperses some seeds, feeds large predators, and opens den routes that smaller scavengers later use.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate to high. Adults move between refuse, water, walls, and food stores. Main concerns are contaminated bites, fleas, mites, droppings, and airborne dust from old nests.
reproductionNesting: Builds deeper chambers than Rattata, often near water or food caches. Adults defend young violently and may relocate nests through wall voids, riverbank roots, or abandoned foundations.
predators: Fearow, Pidgeot, Arbok, Persian, larger carnivorous Pokemon, humans, and rival Raticate in dense feeding territory
emergencyResponse: For an indoor adult, remove food, close side rooms, protect hands, and create one dark exit. For an attacking Raticate, use a barrier board rather than attempting a grab.
legalStatus: Often classified as a managed pest around food storage and infrastructure. Transport and release are usually restricted because relocation can start new colonies.
stressSicknessSigns: Labored swimming or dragging hind feet
lifespan: Wild: 2 to 5 years. Managed care: 6 to 10 years if dental wear, sanitation, and stress are controlled.
socialStructure: Adults may be solitary around prime food, but tolerate or dominate clusters of Rattata. In dense settlements, one adult often anchors several juvenile routes and hidden caches.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Major hazards are bite trauma, contamination, infrastructure damage, and colony defense.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Lunge and bite hazard within 3 to 5 ft; colony scatter can affect an entire room.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast sprint roughly 20 to 28 mph over short cluttered paths
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High, especially around walls, beams, and water edges
fieldCapabilities.balance: Whiskers are critical for ledges, pipes, rafters, and riverbank roots

trainingRisk: Severe bites, escape, equipment destruction, food guarding, nest aggression, and disease exposure
physiology: Raticate is built around teeth, whiskers, and hind-foot mobility. The fangs grow continuously and require hard wear. The whiskers serve as balance organs and close-range navigation tools. Webbed hind feet turn water into an escape route rather than a barrier.

keyFacts.diet: Opportunistic omnivore with strong gnawing need
keyFacts.habitat: Riverbanks, barns, food stores, ruins, docks, caves, and old towers
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 2 to 5 years. Managed: 6 to 10 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary adult or dominant animal with nearby Rattata colony

domesticationDifficulty: High. Needs gnaw-proof housing, bite-safe procedures, and constant sanitation. Best handled as a working animal, not a pet.
physicalCharacteristics: Large tawny rodent-like Pokemon with cream underside, ragged ears, narrow eyes, long whiskers, powerful incisors, short forelimbs, webbed hind feet, and a long scaly tail.
dietRestrictions: Avoid soft-only diets, unsecured grain, moldy refuse, sugary scraps, high-salt food, and diets that do not support natural tooth wear.

attacks.name: Quick Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Fast opening lunge used from wall edges or food cover.

attacks.name: Scary Face
attacks.fieldUse: Threat display with raised fur, exposed incisors, and warning hiss.

attacks.name: Swords Dance
attacks.fieldUse: Heightened battle posture that makes bites more precise and dangerous.

attacks.name: Bite
attacks.fieldUse: Deep fang strike used in close defense or predation.

attacks.name: Crunch
attacks.fieldUse: Full-force jaw compression capable of breaking hard materials.

attacks.name: Sucker Punch
attacks.fieldUse: Ambush bite against approaching handlers or predators.

attacks.name: Super Fang
attacks.fieldUse: Signature severe gnawing attack that can cripple prey or barriers.

attacks.name: Double-Edge
attacks.fieldUse: Desperate body charge used when escape routes are cut.

Kanto: Routes 16, 17, 18, and 21 in classic records
Pokemon Mansion and related abandoned structures
Cycling Road and river-adjacent route corridors
Johto: Routes 7, 9, 10, 26, 27, 38, 39, 42, 43, 47, and 48 in various records
Burned Tower, Mt. Mortar, Tohjo Falls, and Union Cave
Old tower and cave habitat connected to Rattata corridors
Sinnoh: Spacious Cave, Grassland Cave, Riverbank Cave, Still-Water Cavern, and Sunlit Cavern records
Unova: Dreamyard, Relic Passage, and Strange House records
Alola: Routes 10, 11, 14, 15, 16, 17, Akala Outskirts, Malie City, Poni Wilds, Ancient Poni Path, Poni Grove, Poni Plains, and Poni Gauntlet records for the Alolan line

officialEvolutionSummary.rattataToRaticate: Rattata evolves into Raticate starting at Level 20.
officialEvolutionSummary.regionalForm: Alolan Rattata evolves into Alolan Raticate starting at Level 20 at night.
officialEvolutionSummary.finalStage: Raticate is the final standard stage of the line.

sourceUseNote: Core typing, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, game location anchors, and move names are based on official-style Pokedex and game database records. Lifespan, ecology, first aid, containment, and real-world field behavior are interpretive survival-guide material for this passion project.

name: Rattata
region: Kanto, Johto, Sinnoh, Unova, Alola, and related settled corridors
route: Kanto Routes 1, 2, 4, 9, 16, 21, and 22; Johto routes and old towers such as Sprout Tower, Burned Tower, Bell Tower, Union Cave, Mt. Mortar, and Tohjo Falls; Sinnoh Routes 225 and 226; Unova Dreamyard and Relic Passage records; Alolan settlement edges, farms, ports, and night food routes
habitat: Urban edges, grain stores, barns, hedgerows, abandoned buildings, riverbanks, dry wall cavities, cave mouths, old towers, ship holds, alleys, farms, and any food-rich habitat with small gaps for nesting
classification: Mouse Pokemon / Base Stage Survival Rodent
behaviorTemperament: Restless, bold around food, and extremely cautious when cornered. Wild Rattata rely on speed, scent, hearing, gnawing, and rapid reproduction. They bite first when trapped and often appear alone even when many more are nearby.
diet: Seeds, grain, fruit, roots, insects, eggs, carrion scraps, stored food, refuse, leather, bark, mushrooms, and nearly any edible settlement waste
activityPattern: Nocturnal to crepuscular in settlements and towers, but active at any hour when food is scarce or a nest has been disturbed.
bestTimeToFind: Dusk near grain stores, old walls, hedgerows, wharves, barns, cave entrances, burned structures, and tower stairwells marked by fresh tooth grooves
observationDifficulty: Easy. Rattata are common and noisy around food, but individuals vanish quickly into cracks and can be hard to count accurately.
conservationStatus: Common nuisance species with local control restrictions near protected heritage towers and natural predator corridors
populationStatus: Abundant and able to expand explosively wherever food waste, grain, berry crops, or abandoned structures are available. Local declines occur only where predators, sanitation, and sealed storage are consistent.
description: A small Normal-type mouse Pokemon known for extreme vitality, fast breeding, sharp incisors, and the ability to live almost anywhere food can be found.
lore: Early Kanto settlements did not treat Rattata as monsters at all. They were just the sound in the walls after harvest and the purple blur under a pantry shelf. That mistake lasted until entire grain rooms began losing structural beams to tooth marks. Field surveyors later discovered that Rattata were not merely eating stored food. They were testing every surface around it: rope, wood, bone, cloth, doors, baskets, and soft stone. In Johto, records from Sprout Tower and the Burned Tower describe night watchmen hearing hundreds of tiny feet above them before the floor beams failed. In Alola, the same lineage adapted to urban food chains, moving under cover of darkness and following dominant nest leaders toward fresher food. In a real-world field guide, the main danger is not one Rattata. It is assuming there is only one. A single sighting often means a hidden colony has mapped the building, located food, and begun cutting new routes through anything its teeth can wear down.
survivalUse: Can reveal hidden food stores, structural gaps, low crawlspaces, old tunnel routes, and nearby water by their travel lanes. In survival settings, their alarm movement can warn of larger predators entering a settlement.
handlingWarning: Do not grab barehanded, corner in a small room, or block every exit at once. A trapped Rattata can bite through gloves, wiring, light containers, and soft wood. Control food first, then guide the animal out.
encounterAdvice: Stand still, remove exposed food, and open one escape route. If multiple animals appear or squeaking comes from walls, leave the area and treat it as a nest site rather than a single animal encounter.
firstAid: Wash bites immediately with soap and clean water, control bleeding, cover with a sterile dressing, and seek medical care for deep punctures, tendon pain, swelling, fever, or suspected contamination.
companionSuitability: Poor for casual handlers but useful for trained scent, tunnel, and pest-detection programs
fieldBehaviors: Gnaws constantly to wear down incisors
Runs along edges instead of open floor
Caches food in dry wall voids
Breeds quickly near stable food sources
fieldNote: If you see one Rattata crossing open ground in daylight, assume stress, nest crowding, or food shortage. Healthy colonies prefer edges, gaps, shadows, and repeated hidden paths.
originStory: The Rattata line likely began as small edge-dwelling rodents that survived by eating anything, breeding rapidly, and wearing down their teeth on hard surfaces. Human routes through Kanto and Johto accidentally created perfect corridors: farms, towers, ports, cave rest stops, and berry routes. Evolution at Level 20 reflects the point where a juvenile can no longer survive by speed alone. Its teeth, muscles, whiskers, and hind feet enlarge into the Raticate form, allowing river crossing, direct combat, wall damage, and command over a broader feeding range. Alolan forms show how the same survival engine can shift under island urban pressure, becoming more nocturnal, food-selective, and socially organized around dominant nest animals.

evolutionLine.name: Rattata
evolutionLine.note: Base stage mouse Pokemon. Evolves through rapid growth once its teeth, senses, and food drive can support adult territory competition.

evolutionLine.name: Raticate
evolutionLine.note: Adult stage mouse Pokemon. Larger fangs, stronger jaws, webbed hind feet, and broader range let it cross water and claim food corridors.

mainLine.name: Rattata
mainLine.note: Small, fast, highly adaptable gnawing rodent. Lives wherever food, cover, and nesting gaps exist.

mainLine.name: Raticate
mainLine.methodToNext: Final stage; regional form variants only
mainLine.note: Large adult river-crossing rodent. Strong fangs, webbed hind feet, and aggressive territory defense make it a serious settlement pest.

target.name: Alolan Rattata
target.note: Night-active urban forager adapted to settlement raiding, fresh food theft, and boss-led nest behavior.

target.name: Alolan Raticate
target.note: Large command animal of night-raiding nests. Directs groups of Rattata toward food sources and defends stored food aggressively.

domesticationStatus: Not truly domesticated; tolerated in controlled research, scent work, and pest-detection settings
prey: Insects, eggs, grubs, small nestlings, carrion scraps, and food caches; also scavenges human stores aggressively
territoryRange: Usually a building cluster, hedgerow, barn, or 0.1 to 0.3 square miles around stable food. Colonies expand along walls, ditches, routes, and tunnels.
lifeCycle: Born in hidden nests of shredded plant fiber, cloth, and dry refuse. Juveniles begin gnawing early, follow adult scent trails, and evolve into Raticate starting at Level 20 when jaw strength, body mass, and territorial behavior mature.
containmentRequirements: Escape-proof metal enclosure, chew-proof food bowls, double-door handling box, deep bedding, hard gnawing blocks, sealed feed storage, and strict sanitation to prevent colony smells from drawing wild animals
ecologicalRole: Consumes waste, spreads seeds, feeds raptors and snakes, opens micro-tunnels used by insects, and rapidly converts unsecured food into scavenger population growth.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Dirty nests may carry mites, fleas, fungal spores, contaminated droppings, and bite-wound bacteria. Respiratory protection is recommended during nest cleanup.
reproductionNesting: Builds hidden nests in walls, brush piles, barns, towers, ship holds, and tunnel edges. Litters are frequent when food is steady, and young disperse through tight gaps before humans notice the full colony.
predators: Spearow, Fearow, Pidgeotto, Pidgeot, Ekans, Arbok, Meowth, Persian, larger Raticate, owls, fox-like fire types, and humans
emergencyResponse: If a colony erupts from a wall or storage room, do not chase scattered animals. Remove people, secure food, open controlled exits, and call trained pest or Pokemon control handlers.
legalStatus: Observation is generally unrestricted, but live capture, relocation, and colony destruction may be regulated in cities, protected towers, and ecological reserves.
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years, often shorter due to predation and control efforts. Managed care: 4 to 7 years with tooth care and clean housing.
socialStructure: Loose colonies based around food routes rather than stable family groups. Individuals forage alone but use shared trails, scent marks, alarm squeaks, and hidden nest chambers.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Main hazards are speed, teeth, contamination, and nest density.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Bite range is close contact, but colony disturbance can create a 10 to 30 ft scatter hazard.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Very fast short runs, roughly 18 to 25 mph in cluttered terrain
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent at edge turns, wall gaps, and sudden reversals
fieldCapabilities.balance: Whiskers and tail help navigate walls, pipes, rafters, and narrow ledges

trainingRisk: Bites, escape, chewing through equipment, contamination, stress breeding, and attraction of wild colony members
physiology: Rattata has continuously growing incisors, sensitive whiskers, sharp hearing, a compact digestive system, and high tolerance for poor environments. Its survival advantage is not strength. It is speed, appetite, tooth growth, reproduction, and constant environmental testing through chewing.

keyFacts.habitat: Settlements, routes, towers, fields, barns, caves, and ports
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 3 years. Managed: 4 to 7 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: One visible animal often signals 10 to 40 nearby individuals in food-rich areas

domesticationDifficulty: High. Small size makes handling look easy, but chewing, disease risk, escape skill, and colony attraction make long-term care difficult.
physicalCharacteristics: Small purple rodent-like Pokemon with cream underside, large ears, long whiskers, red eyes, sharp incisors, clawed feet, and a curled tail used for balance.
dietRestrictions: Avoid unlimited grain, moldy food, sugary waste, soft-only diets, salty scraps, and low-fiber feeding that fails to wear the teeth naturally.

attacks.name: Tackle
attacks.fieldUse: Close body hit used when fleeing through tight ground lanes.

attacks.name: Tail Whip
attacks.fieldUse: Threat and distraction display before escape or bite.

attacks.name: Quick Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Sudden lunge from cover or fast escape strike.

attacks.name: Bite
attacks.fieldUse: Contaminating fang bite used when trapped or feeding.

attacks.name: Assurance
attacks.fieldUse: Follow-up strike against injured prey or distracted enemies.

attacks.name: Crunch
attacks.fieldUse: Adult-level jaw behavior foreshadowed in older juveniles with severe bite risk.

attacks.name: Super Fang
attacks.fieldUse: Powerful gnawing attack that can disable prey or damage barriers.

attacks.name: Endeavor
attacks.fieldUse: Last-effort survival behavior when injured but still mobile.

Kanto: Routes 1, 2, 4, 9, 16, 21, and 22 in classic records
Pokemon Mansion and settlement-edge structures
Common early-route grass and building margins
Johto: Sprout Tower, Burned Tower, Bell Tower, Union Cave, Mt. Mortar, and Tohjo Falls
Old tower floors, cave edges, and route-side food corridors
Unova: Dreamyard, Relic Passage, and Strange House records through the line
Alola: Urban and route records connected to the Alolan form, especially nighttime food routes

officialEvolutionSummary.rattataToRaticate: Rattata evolves into Raticate starting at Level 20.
officialEvolutionSummary.regionalForm: Alolan Rattata is Dark / Normal and evolves into Alolan Raticate starting at Level 20 at night.
officialEvolutionSummary.finalStage: Raticate is the final standard stage of the line.

name: Spearow
region: Kanto, Johto, Sinnoh, Alola, and dry open-route habitats
route: Kanto Routes 3, 4, 9, 10, 11, 16, 17, 18, 22, and 23; Johto Routes 3, 4, 7, 9, 10, 22, 29, 30, 31, 32, 33, 35, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46, 47, Azalea Town, and Vermilion records; Sevii Island roads including Kindle Road and Mt. Ember; Sinnoh Routes 225 and 226; Alola Routes 2 and 3 with grassland and farm-edge records
habitat: Tall grass, dry fields, canyon paths, rocky route edges, orchard borders, town outskirts, pasture fences, scrubland, low cliffs, and bug-rich farm margins
classification: Tiny Bird Pokemon / Base Stage Territorial Grass Raptor
behaviorTemperament: Short-tempered, territorial, loud, and far more reckless than its size suggests. Spearow use harsh cries, fast wingbeats, and stabbing beak strikes to defend grass patches and nest shrubs from intruders.
diet: Bugs, grubs, beetles, worms, small berries, seeds, field insects, crop pests, and occasional scraps from roads or farmyards
activityPattern: Diurnal. Most active shortly after sunrise and again before dusk, when insects move through grass and territory calls carry across open routes.
bestTimeToFind: Morning in tall grass, dry route edges, fields troubled by Bug-type Pokemon, rocky fence lines, and sunlit canyon paths where repeated harsh calls echo
observationDifficulty: Easy to moderate. Calls are obvious, but the bird is small, quick, and often hidden low in grass until it attacks or flees.
conservationStatus: Common route bird with local protection during nesting season
populationStatus: Stable where grassland, insects, and edge habitat remain. Declines where pesticides remove prey or larger raptors claim nesting lanes.
description: A small Normal and Flying-type bird Pokemon known for loud alarm calls, aggressive territorial behavior, short wings, and quick low-altitude flight.
lore: Kanto travelers learned to respect Spearow before they learned to name it. The warning was not the bird itself but the sound: one sharp cry, then several more answering from grass and fence posts. Early route builders treated these calls as nuisance noise until pack animals began bolting from sudden pecking attacks. Spearow rarely kill anything large, but their courage is disproportionate to their size. A flock defending a nesting patch will strike eyes, ears, hands, and exposed food until the intruder retreats. Johto farmers came to tolerate them because they devour crop pests, especially Bug-type swarms that trouble fields near Azalea and the open routes. The problem is that Spearow do not know the difference between a threat and a passerby who walked too close to the wrong grass. In real-world field use, the call is valuable. If every Spearow in a field screams at once, something moved through the grass before you did.
survivalUse: Can warn of predators, reveal insect-rich grass, indicate open route edges, and help control crop pests. Their alarm calls can mark movement through tall grass before humans see it.
handlingWarning: Do not grab from above, expose eyes, approach nests, or assume small size means low risk. Beak strikes to the face and hands are common in defensive encounters.
encounterAdvice: Keep hands low, protect eyes, and avoid walking straight into repeated warning calls. If Spearow dive from both sides, lower your posture and retreat from the nest zone.
firstAid: Clean peck wounds with soap and water. Flush eye contact immediately. Seek care for deep punctures, eyelid injury, infection signs, or repeated strikes around the face.
companionSuitability: Moderate for experienced bird handlers; poor for children or anyone needing a calm companion
fieldBehaviors: Perches on fences and route signs
Chases small bug Pokemon out of crops
fieldNote: A lone Spearow call is a warning. Multiple answering calls usually mean the whole territory is awake and tracking your movement.
originStory: The Spearow line likely split from gentler field birds through pressure from open-route living. Grasslands, canyons, farms, and rocky roads favored birds that reacted first and questioned later. Short wings gave Spearow quick bursts but poor endurance, so territorial calls and aggression replaced long-distance escape. Evolution at Level 20 produces Fearow, a form that solves the endurance problem with broad wings, a longer neck, a spear-like beak, and the ability to patrol much larger food ranges. In survival terms, Spearow is the alarm wire and Fearow is the aerial enforcer.

evolutionLine.name: Spearow
evolutionLine.note: Base stage tiny bird Pokemon. Evolves when wing endurance, beak strength, and territorial drive become strong enough for long patrols.

evolutionLine.name: Fearow
evolutionLine.note: Adult beak Pokemon. Built for distance flight, high dives, riverbank hunting, and aggressive aerial territory defense.

mainLine.name: Spearow
mainLine.note: Small territorial grassland bird. Loud alarm calls, rapid wingbeats, and reckless pecking protect its range.

mainLine.name: Fearow
mainLine.note: Long-beaked endurance raptor. Patrols roads, cliffs, fields, and coastlines while hunting insects, fish, and small prey.

domesticationStatus: Semi-tame in farm pest-control programs but not a gentle household bird
prey: Grubs, beetles, caterpillars, worms, field insects, small Bug-type Pokemon, and exposed scraps
territoryRange: Usually 0.1 to 0.5 square miles around a nest shrub, fence line, bug-rich field, or rocky route edge
lifeCycle: Hatches in low shrubs or grass-edge nests, follows adult alarm calls, learns rapid pecking and bug hunting, and evolves into Fearow starting at Level 20 when wing strength and hunting range increase.
containmentRequirements: Secure aviary with covered sides, low perches, visual barriers, nest privacy, insect feeding enrichment, room for fast short flights, and safe handling doors that protect eyes and hands
ecologicalRole: Controls crop insects, warns other animals of movement, spreads small seeds, and serves as prey or rival to larger bird Pokemon.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Feather mites, crop infection, respiratory irritation, and contaminated peck wounds are the main issues.
reproductionNesting: Builds low, guarded nests in shrubs, route grasses, fence tangles, and farm edges. Adults use loud calls and repeated dive strikes to keep intruders away.
predators: Fearow, Pidgeotto, Pidgeot, Arbok, Persian, larger raptors, nest-raiding rodents, and humans near farms
emergencyResponse: For a defensive flock, cover eyes, leave the nest zone, and avoid swinging at birds. For indoor capture, dim lights, close curtains, open one exit, and use a towel only if trained.
legalStatus: Generally legal to observe. Nest disturbance may be restricted during breeding season, especially near farms that rely on them for insect control.
lifespan: Wild: 2 to 4 years. Managed care: 8 to 12 years with safe flight space and proper diet.
socialStructure: Pairs or loose territorial clusters. Groups coordinate warning calls, but individuals compete aggressively for nest sites and insect-rich grass.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Main hazards are beak strikes, eye injury, and flock alarm behavior.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Dive and peck hazard usually within 3 to 12 ft of the defended area.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast low flight roughly 30 to 40 mph in short bursts
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent in grass and around fences, weaker in long open pursuit
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Short ground hop and rapid takeoff from grass
fieldCapabilities.balance: Good perch grip on fences, branches, and route signs

trainingRisk: Face pecks, eye injury, stress from confinement, territorial biting, cage striking, and escape during feeding
physiology: Spearow has compact flight muscles, short rapid-beating wings, a hooked stabbing beak, keen sight, and a loud call system. It is not built for graceful soaring. It is built to explode from grass, attack, warn others, and disappear into cover.

keyFacts.habitat: Tall grass, dry routes, farms, scrubland, cliffs, and fence lines
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 2 to 4 years. Managed: 8 to 12 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Pairs, loose clusters, or alarm-calling flocks

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate to high. Useful and hardy, but easily stressed, loud, and quick to strike faces or hands.
physicalCharacteristics: Small brown bird Pokemon with rough head plumage, cream underside, narrow eyes, pink hooked beak, pinkish-red wings, black back feathers, and light feet with three forward-gripping toes.
dietRestrictions: Avoid seed-only diets, spoiled insects, pesticide-exposed bugs, salty scraps, smoke, and cramped cages that damage flight feathers.

attacks.name: Growl
attacks.fieldUse: Alarm call that warns the flock and discourages smaller threats.

attacks.name: Peck
attacks.fieldUse: Basic beak strike used against insects and hands.

attacks.name: Leer
attacks.fieldUse: Fixed territorial stare before a dive or peck.

attacks.name: Assurance
attacks.fieldUse: Follow-up strike against an already disrupted target.

attacks.name: Fury Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Rapid repeated pecking used during nest defense.

attacks.name: Aerial Ace
attacks.fieldUse: Clean diving strike that is difficult to dodge at close range.

attacks.name: Wing Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Short wing-driven hit used in low flight.

attacks.name: Drill Peck
attacks.fieldUse: Developed piercing attack seen in older Spearow approaching evolution.

Kanto: Sevii Island records including Canyon Entrance, Cape Brink, Kindle Road, Mt. Ember, Ruin Valley, Treasure Beach, and Water Path
Dry route grass, canyon approaches, and road edges
Johto: Azalea Town and Vermilion City records
Farm edges and town grass where bug prey is common

officialEvolutionSummary.spearowToFearow: Spearow evolves into Fearow starting at Level 20.
officialEvolutionSummary.finalStage: Fearow is the final stage of the Spearow line.
officialEvolutionSummary.specialForm: No standard Mega Evolution or regional form is currently part of the main line.

sourceUseNote: Core typing, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, game location anchors, and move names are based on official-style Pokedex and game database records. Lifespan, ecology, first aid, containment, and real-world field behavior are interpretive survival-guide material for this passion project.
