name: Squirtle
route: Freshwater banks, reed beds, flooded cellars, old canals, abandoned river towns, storm-flooded ruins, and quiet coastal inlets
habitat: Shallow freshwater or brackish shelter with mud banks, submerged debris, air pockets, and safe nesting ledges above floodwater
classification: Tiny Turtle Pokemon / Juvenile Shoreline Chelonian
behaviorTemperament: Curious, social, and deceptively gentle when young. Wild colonies are playful at the surface but territorial beneath it.
diet: Aquatic plants, algae, small fish, insects, mollusks, carrion scraps, and food stolen from camps near the waterline
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular near safe banks. More active at dusk, during rain, and after floods.
bestTimeToFind: Dusk near reed beds, old docks, flooded ruins, or shallow pools where shells tap against stone
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Individuals may approach humans, but colony boundaries are difficult to recognize from shore.
conservationStatus: Protected starter-class Water species
populationStatus: Uncommon in settled waterways but locally stable where ruins, marshes, and protected nesting sites remain
description: A small aquatic reptilian Pokemon with a hardening shell, webbed limbs, lungs, and powerful mouth-spray ability. In real-world field terms, it is safest when treated as a social shoreline animal rather than a toy-like starter.
lore:
Squirtle is the reason old river villages kept bells beside their wells.

Not for fire. Not for raiders. Not for storms.

For splashing.

A single splash in the night meant a child had wandered too close to the bank. Two splashes meant something had come out of the water. Three meant the village was already counting its missing.

Modern starter programs describe Squirtle as loyal, playful, social, and easier to handle than most young Water-types. That is true. It is also the safest lie ever printed in a beginnerÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s field guide. Squirtle is friendly because it is young. It is curious because it has not yet learned fear. It follows humans because humans are warm, noisy, and careless around shorelines.

A wild Squirtle will often approach camps at dusk, eyes wide, shell wet, little hands raised as if asking for food. It may chirp, splash, or roll onto its back to appear harmless. Many are harmless. Some have been hand-fed by fishermen for generations. Some will sleep under docks, chase thrown pebbles, and nudge lost objects back toward shore.

But Squirtle live in colonies, and colonies have rules older than kindness.

The first rule is that anything near the water belongs partly to the water.

Food dropped near a bank is taken. Nets left overnight are cut or dragged under. Boots, knives, toys, bones, and wedding rings all vanish into the reeds. People once believed Squirtle collected shiny objects out of mischief. Rangers now believe these objects are boundary markers. Squirtle colonies arrange stolen items beneath the surface in half-circles around nesting zones, creating underwater warnings that only they recognize.

A person who crosses those markers may never know they have entered protected ground.

Squirtle rarely kill by biting. Their teeth are not the primary danger. Their danger is patience, water, and numbers. A lone Squirtle will retreat from an adult human. A group will not. They circle silently beneath the surface, using their shells as shields and their small bodies as moving stones. Then one surfaces close enough to be seen.

The mistake is looking at that one.

The others take the ankles.

Survivors describe the sensation as weeds tightening around their legs. Then the bank gives way. Then the water closes over the ears and everything becomes muffled except the tapping of shells against rocks.

Squirtle do not understand drowning as murder. To them, water is safety. If a creature thrashes, it must be frightened. If it breathes badly, it must be held still. If it screams, it must be taken deeper where the colony can surround and calm it. This instinct makes them excellent rescuers when trained and terrible hazards when wild.

Many old drownings blamed on weeds, mud holes, or river spirits are now suspected Squirtle defensive incidents.

The darkest reports involve children.

Not because Squirtle hunt them, but because Squirtle seem to mistake them for vulnerable colony members. Small children playing near freshwater may be approached, splashed, and gently herded toward shallow pools. If the child laughs and follows, the Squirtle may attempt to Ã¢â‚¬Å“teachÃ¢â‚¬Â them to submerge. In several rural Kanto cases, children were found alive in reed beds after hours missing, soaked, hypothermic, and surrounded by Squirtle that refused to let adults approach.

In worse cases, only the toys came back.

Villagers once carved small wooden shells and hung them over doors after a child drowned. The charm was not meant to ward Squirtle away. It was an apology. It meant: this house has already paid the river.

Squirtle itself carries a strange innocence through all of this. A captured wild specimen may become deeply attached to a handler, especially one who feeds it, polishes its shell, and gives it safe water access. It learns names quickly. It recognizes routines. It will sleep near boots, packs, or bedding, guarding possessions the same way it guards river stones. Many trainers consider Squirtle one of the most emotionally reliable starter PokÃƒÂ©mon.

That bond is real.

But so is the pull of the water.

Even domesticated Squirtle may become agitated during heavy rain. They scratch doors, stare at drains, and press their faces against windows when runoff begins flowing in the street. In flood season, a Squirtle may attempt to drag bedding, food, and even sleeping humans toward what it considers safer ground. Usually that means higher ground if trained well.

If trained poorly, it means water.

The shell is another source of folklore. A young SquirtleÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s shell is soft at hatching and hardens over time. Old coastal families believed a Squirtle carried its first home on its back because the sea had stolen the place where it was born. There is a reason that belief lasted. Squirtle often appear after floods, tsunamis, dam failures, and shipwrecks. They gather where human structures break and water enters places it was never meant to reach.

To a Squirtle, a flooded cellar is a cave.

A sunken chapel is shelter.

A drowned town is a reef.

Divers exploring abandoned settlements in old river basins have reported Squirtle nests inside upstairs bedrooms, schoolhouses, and collapsed kitchens. The creatures decorate these nests with bright fragments of human life: buttons, marbles, spoons, coins, belt buckles, glass beads, religious medals, and teeth polished smooth by current.

One ranger found a nursery colony living inside the second floor of a submerged farmhouse. The nest was lined with childrenÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s shoes.

No bodies were recovered.

The official warning for Squirtle habitats is simple:

Do not camp below the flood mark.

Do not follow splashing after dark.

Do not let children feed wild specimens.

If a Squirtle gives you something from the water, accept it and leave.

If it takes something from you, do not reach in after it.

If the water goes quiet, step back before you look down.

loreSummary: A river-child species associated with wells, drowned villages, and colony boundary objects beneath the surface.
survivalUse: Can provide clean water pressure for rinsing tools, assist with shallow-water rescue, retrieve floating supplies, deter small predators, and help locate air pockets in flooded structures.
handlingWarning: Never let children feed wild Squirtle. Never stand between a Squirtle and deep water, and never reach into water after an object it has taken.
encounterAdvice: Stay above the flood mark, accept returned objects without bending over the water, and back away if splashing suddenly stops.
firstAid: Treat near-drowning, hypothermia, ankle trauma, and aspiration first. Remove the person from the waterline before assessing bites or shell impact bruising.
companionSuitability: Good for careful handlers with water access and strict shoreline safety rules
fieldBehaviors: Herds children or weak animals toward shallow pools
Presses shell against trusted companions for reassurance
signsOfPresence: Stolen objects arranged below clear water
Tiny blue shapes in upper windows of flooded structures
Bubbles rising from reed beds at dusk
equipment: First-aid kit for hypothermia and drowning response
fieldNote: A lone Squirtle on the bank may be harmless. A quiet bank with one visible Squirtle and no bird noise should be treated as an active colony edge.
originStory:
Squirtle was formally recorded in 1812, five years after Bulbasaur entered the scientific record and seven years before Ivysaur was confirmed.

Unlike Bulbasaur, Squirtle was not discovered deep in forest territory. It was documented in a place humans had already ruined.

The first accepted report came from the drowned lower district of Old Harrow Quay, a Kanto river settlement abandoned after a series of spring floods destroyed its levees. For years afterward, fishermen claimed small blue Ã¢â‚¬Å“shell childrenÃ¢â‚¬Â lived in the upper windows of submerged houses. They were said to knock from inside the flooded rooms at night, tapping on glass from below the waterline.

Naturalists dismissed the stories as grief folklore. The flood had taken dozens of people, including children, and the idea of little faces in the water was considered a local mourning myth.

Then the bells started ringing.

Old Harrow Quay still had a chapel tower standing above the floodwater. Its bell rope hung down into the drowned nave, unreachable from the outside. On windless nights, the bell rang anyway. Not loudly. Just once or twice, soft enough to make listeners doubt themselves. Villagers believed the dead were asking to be counted.

In 1812, a survey crew entered the chapel by boat.

They found the rope pulled through a broken side window and tied around a nest.

Inside the flooded chapel, beneath the floating remains of hymn boards and rotted pews, lived a colony of small shelled PokÃƒÂ©mon. Their eggs were hidden in the bell loft where floodwater could not reach them. Young Squirtle slept in pockets of air beneath the rafters. Adults, or what researchers then assumed were adults, guarded the lower levels and pushed floating debris into barricades.

The bell had not been rung by ghosts.

It had been rung by Squirtle tugging the rope whenever predators entered the chapel.

The first captured specimen was small, blue, and remarkably calm. It did not bite. It did not flee. It sat in a basin of water aboard the survey boat and watched the researchers with a patient, almost solemn expression. When one sailor coughed and leaned over the rail, the Squirtle sprayed water into his face, then chirped until he stepped back from the edge.

The crew interpreted this as play.

The next day, the same Squirtle dragged a sleeping deckhand by the sleeve toward a rain barrel during a storm.

That was the first recorded hint of the speciesÃ¢â‚¬â„¢ rescue-drowning confusion.

Early anatomy work revealed that Squirtle occupied a rare middle ground between land and water. It possessed lungs, strong swimming muscles, webbed hands and feet, and a shell that hardened with age. Its body was built for shorelines, not open ocean. It could walk, climb muddy banks, hide in reeds, and launch pressurized water from its mouth with enough force to knock loose bark from trees.

The shell was the key.

Researchers initially believed the shell was simple armor. Later studies showed it functioned as protection, buoyancy control, water storage support, and social identity. Squirtle recognize one another by shell marks, scratches, mineral stains, and growth patterns. In wild colonies, shell-touching is a greeting, a warning, and a form of reassurance. A frightened Squirtle may press its shell against another member of the group to calm itself.

This is why captured Squirtle often become attached to packs, crates, helmets, shields, and human furniture. They instinctively treat hard curved objects as social substitutes.

The Old Harrow specimens were not named Squirtle at first. The earliest field notes called them Ã¢â‚¬Å“Quay-shells,Ã¢â‚¬Â Ã¢â‚¬Å“river turtles,Ã¢â‚¬Â or Ã¢â‚¬Å“water imps.Ã¢â‚¬Â The modern name came later from dockworkers who described the young ones as Ã¢â‚¬Å“squirting turtlesÃ¢â‚¬Â after they learned to spray anyone who tried to steal fish from the drying racks.

By 1815, Squirtle colonies had been identified in several river mouths, marshlands, and coastal ruins. Many appeared in places with recent flooding. This led to the first major theory of Squirtle origin: that their ancestors were small freshwater reptiles displaced by repeated river disasters. Those ancestors survived by becoming amphibious scavengers, nesting in hollow logs, beaver dams, cave mouths, and eventually human structures.

But the darker theory came from Old Harrow Quay.

A naturalist named Mara Vell proposed that Squirtle did not merely adapt to flooded human spaces. They learned from them. The species became successful because human settlements created perfect artificial reefs: walls to block current, upper floors for eggs, cellars for cool water, chimneys for air pockets, and debris fields full of hiding places.

In other words, Squirtle may have spread because people kept building towns where water wanted to return.

This theory remains controversial, but modern records support part of it. Squirtle populations often rise after controlled reservoirs, abandoned canals, broken aqueducts, and flooded mines become available. They are not drawn to tragedy in a supernatural sense. They are drawn to shelter, water, and objects that can be arranged into colony boundaries.

Humans call these places ruins.

Squirtle call them nurseries.

The 1812 Old Harrow colony also explains why later evolutions took longer to document. Most Squirtle observed by humans were young shoreline members. Larger evolved forms withdrew into deeper water, older canals, sea caves, and protected nesting routes. Wartortle would not be formally separated from Squirtle until later, when researchers realized the long-tailed blue guardians seen around older colonies were not a regional variant, but the next stage of the line.

Blastoise would take longer still.

People had stories of cannon-backed monsters before they had proof. Sailors swore that some reefs fired water hard enough to break masts. Harbor guards reported iron plates bent inward by invisible pressure. Shipwreck survivors described a low shape beneath the waves with two black openings on its back.

For years, science ignored them.

Science often does that when the ocean keeps the evidence.

The accepted Squirtle-line chronology now begins here:

1812: Squirtle formally recorded at Old Harrow Quay after the chapel-bell colony survey.

The later records will build from that flood site, because the Squirtle line is not just a water evolution family.

It is a history of what happens when living things inherit the places humans lose to water.

Squirtle is the child at the edge of the river.

Wartortle is the thing watching from the reeds.

Blastoise is the reason old sailors stopped laughing at drowned bells.
originSummary: First formally recorded in 1812 at Old Harrow Quay after a chapel-bell colony survey revealed nesting Squirtle inside flooded human ruins.

evolutionLine.name: Squirtle
evolutionLine.note: Tiny Turtle Water-type. Juvenile shoreline colony member with shell withdrawal and pressurized mouth-spray behavior.

evolutionLine.name: Wartortle
evolutionLine.note: Turtle Water-type. Territorial guardian stage with stronger limbs, longer tail, ear plumes, and heavier water-pressure biology.

evolutionLine.name: Blastoise
evolutionLine.note: Shellfish Water-type. Final armored pressure-cannon stage with Mega and Gigantamax battle phenomena.

mainLine.name: Squirtle
mainLine.note: Juvenile shoreline survivor. Shell hardens after hatching and water projection begins early.

mainLine.name: Wartortle
mainLine.note: Guardian stage. Ear and tail plumes help balance and current-sensing during fast swimming.

mainLine.name: Blastoise
mainLine.note: Final heavy-shell stage. Carries high-pressure shell cannons and functions as a living harbor weapon.

target.name: Mega Blastoise
target.note: Temporary form with intensified cannon output, Mega Launcher ability, and increased shell mass.

target.name: Gigantamax Blastoise
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat water output and recoil forces as disaster-scale hazards.

domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated under Water-type starter programs
prey: Small fish, aquatic insects, mollusks, tadpoles, exposed eggs, carrion scraps, and camp food
territoryRange: Usually 0.25 to 2 square miles of connected bank, reed, dock, cellar, or flooded ruin habitat
lifeCycle: Hatches with a soft shell that hardens quickly, grows through social colony behavior, and evolves into Wartortle at level 16.
containmentRequirements: Secure pond or large aquatic enclosure, dry basking platform, escape-safe drain covers, no child access without supervision, and no direct connection to public stormwater.
ecologicalRole: Controls aquatic insects and small fish, recycles flood carrion, moves seeds between wetlands, and converts ruined human structures into nesting habitat.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Shell rot, waterborne bacteria, algae overgrowth, respiratory stress in dirty water, and zoonotic contamination are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are hidden above floodwater in air pockets, bell lofts, upper rooms, hollow logs, or raised reed mats. Colonies defend nest routes more strongly than individuals.
predators: Fearow, Arbok, large fish Pokemon, Blastoise-line territorial rivals, humans, and flood-displaced carnivores
emergencyResponse: Move victims away from the bank, count all people twice, avoid entering the water, use throw ropes instead of wading, and call trained Water-type handlers for colony retrieval.
legalStatus: Water-type handling permit recommended or required near public waterways
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 12 years before evolution or predation. Managed care: often longer with clean water and shell care.
socialStructure: Colony juveniles under Wartortle guardians. Young specimens bond strongly to caretakers but remain influenced by water and group boundaries.

fieldCapabilities.waterPressureLevel: Juvenile but hazardous at close range
fieldCapabilities.effectiveSprayRange: About 10 to 20 ft for defensive spray
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast short swim and quick shoreline dash
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High in water, moderate on land
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Low, but can launch from banks or rocks
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent in water due to shell shape and tail control
fieldCapabilities.breathHolding: Extended submersion capability compared with ordinary reptiles

trainingRisk: Near-drowning incidents, boundary aggression, theft behavior, drain fixation, rescue-drowning confusion, shell disease, and water contamination
physiology: Squirtle converts stored breath and muscular shell compression into pressurized water bursts. Its shell functions as armor, buoyancy control, and social identity marker, while webbed limbs and a curled tail aid swimming.

keyFacts.tailLength: Short curled tail used for balance and water display
keyFacts.habitat: Freshwater banks, flooded ruins, reed beds, and old docks
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 12 years before evolution or predation. Managed: variable with water quality.
keyFacts.groupSize: Small colonies with Wartortle guardians

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Strong bonding potential but high water-safety management required.
physicalCharacteristics: Small blue bipedal reptilian body, light belly, red-brown shell, webbed limbs, curled tail, broad eyes, and a shell that hardens after hatching.
dietRestrictions: Avoid polluted water, excess salted human food, dry-only housing, dirty tanks, and low-calcium diets that weaken shell development.

realismNotes.interpretation: Game powers are treated as biological pressure, shell, swimming, and weather-response systems that could plausibly exist in a field-guide version of the real world.
realismNotes.keptGameRequirements: Water type, Torrent, Rain Dish as hidden ability, Squirtle to Wartortle at level 16, Wartortle to Blastoise at level 36, and Blastoise special forms.
realismNotes.safetyFraming: Water projection and shell impact are treated as physical hazards, with drowning and structural damage handled as the primary real-world risks.

researchSources.name: Pokemon.com PokÃƒÂ©dex
researchSources.usedFor: Official species category, height, weight, type, and listed standard ability.

researchSources.name: PokÃƒÂ©mon Database
researchSources.usedFor: National numbers, hidden ability, base stats, type defenses, move references, and evolution levels.

researchSources.name: User provided lore
researchSources.usedFor: Dark lore, origin stories, historical discovery chronology, and real-life field-guide tone.

researchSources.name: User provided sample JSON
researchSources.usedFor: Required structure, field names, and Field PokÃƒÂ©dex style.

divers exploring abandoned settlements in old river basins have reported squirtle nests inside upstairs bedrooms, schoolhouses, and collapsed kitchens. the creatures decorate these nests with bright fragments of human life: buttons, marbles, spoons, coins, belt buckles, glass beads, religious medals, and teeth polished smooth by current.

one ranger found a nursery colony living inside the second floor of a submerged farmhouse. the nest was lined with childrenã¢â‚¬â„¢s shoes.

no bodies were recovered.

the official warning for squirtle habitats is simple:

do not camp below the flood mark.

do not follow splashing after dark.

do not let children feed wild specimens.

if a squirtle gives you something from the water, accept it and leave.

if it takes something from you, do not reach in after it.

if the water goes quiet, step back before you look down.",
  "darklore": "squirtle is the reason old river villages kept bells beside their wells.

not for fire. not for raiders. not for storms.

for splashing.

a single splash in the night meant a child had wandered too close to the bank. two splashes meant something had come out of the water. three meant the village was already counting its missing.

modern starter programs describe squirtle as loyal, playful, social, and easier to handle than most young water-types. that is true. it is also the safest lie ever printed in a beginnerã¢â‚¬â„¢s field guide. squirtle is friendly because it is young. it is curious because it has not yet learned fear. it follows humans because humans are warm, noisy, and careless around shorelines.

a wild squirtle will often approach camps at dusk, eyes wide, shell wet, little hands raised as if asking for food. it may chirp, splash, or roll onto its back to appear harmless. many are harmless. some have been hand-fed by fishermen for generations. some will sleep under docks, chase thrown pebbles, and nudge lost objects back toward shore.

but squirtle live in colonies, and colonies have rules older than kindness.

the first rule is that anything near the water belongs partly to the water.

food dropped near a bank is taken. nets left overnight are cut or dragged under. boots, knives, toys, bones, and wedding rings all vanish into the reeds. people once believed squirtle collected shiny objects out of mischief. rangers now believe these objects are boundary markers. squirtle colonies arrange stolen items beneath the surface in half-circles around nesting zones, creating underwater warnings that only they recognize.

a person who crosses those markers may never know they have entered protected ground.

squirtle rarely kill by biting. their teeth are not the primary danger. their danger is patience, water, and numbers. a lone squirtle will retreat from an adult human. a group will not. they circle silently beneath the surface, using their shells as shields and their small bodies as moving stones. then one surfaces close enough to be seen.

the mistake is looking at that one.

the others take the ankles.

survivors describe the sensation as weeds tightening around their legs. then the bank gives way. then the water closes over the ears and everything becomes muffled except the tapping of shells against rocks.

squirtle do not understand drowning as murder. to them, water is safety. if a creature thrashes, it must be frightened. if it breathes badly, it must be held still. if it screams, it must be taken deeper where the colony can surround and calm it. this instinct makes them excellent rescuers when trained and terrible hazards when wild.

many old drownings blamed on weeds, mud holes, or river spirits are now suspected squirtle defensive incidents.

the darkest reports involve children.

not because squirtle hunt them, but because squirtle seem to mistake them for vulnerable colony members. small children playing near freshwater may be approached, splashed, and gently herded toward shallow pools. if the child laughs and follows, the squirtle may attempt to ã¢â‚¬å“teachã¢â‚¬â them to submerge. in several rural kanto cases, children were found alive in reed beds after hours missing, soaked, hypothermic, and surrounded by squirtle that refused to let adults approach.

in worse cases, only the toys came back.

villagers once carved small wooden shells and hung them over doors after a child drowned. the charm was not meant to ward squirtle away. it was an apology. it meant: this house has already paid the river.

squirtle itself carries a strange innocence through all of this. a captured wild specimen may become deeply attached to a handler, especially one who feeds it, polishes its shell, and gives it safe water access. it learns names quickly. it recognizes routines. it will sleep near boots, packs, or bedding, guarding possessions the same way it guards river stones. many trainers consider squirtle one of the most emotionally reliable starter pokãƒâ©mon.

that bond is real.

but so is the pull of the water.

even domesticated squirtle may become agitated during heavy rain. they scratch doors, stare at drains, and press their faces against windows when runoff begins flowing in the street. in flood season, a squirtle may attempt to drag bedding, food, and even sleeping humans toward what it considers safer ground. usually that means higher ground if trained well.

if trained poorly, it means water.

the shell is another source of folklore. a young squirtleã¢â‚¬â„¢s shell is soft at hatching and hardens over time. old coastal families believed a squirtle carried its first home on its back because the sea had stolen the place where it was born. there is a reason that belief lasted. squirtle often appear after floods, tsunamis, dam failures, and shipwrecks. they gather where human structures break and water enters places it was never meant to reach.

to a squirtle, a flooded cellar is a cave.

a sunken chapel is shelter.

a drowned town is a reef.

divers exploring abandoned settlements in old river basins have reported squirtle nests inside upstairs bedrooms, schoolhouses, and collapsed kitchens. the creatures decorate these nests with bright fragments of human life: buttons, marbles, spoons, coins, belt buckles, glass beads, religious medals, and teeth polished smooth by current.

one ranger found a nursery colony living inside the second floor of a submerged farmhouse. the nest was lined with childrenã¢â‚¬â„¢s shoes.

no bodies were recovered.

the official warning for squirtle habitats is simple:

do not camp below the flood mark.

do not follow splashing after dark.

do not let children feed wild specimens.

if a squirtle gives you something from the water, accept it and leave.

if it takes something from you, do not reach in after it.

if the water goes quiet, step back before you look down.",

  "originstory": "squirtle was formally recorded in 1812, five years after bulbasaur entered the scientific record and seven years before ivysaur was confirmed.

unlike bulbasaur, squirtle was not discovered deep in forest territory. it was documented in a place humans had already ruined.

the first accepted report came from the drowned lower district of old harrow quay, a kanto river settlement abandoned after a series of spring floods destroyed its levees. for years afterward, fishermen claimed small blue ã¢â‚¬å“shell childrenã¢â‚¬â lived in the upper windows of submerged houses. they were said to knock from inside the flooded rooms at night, tapping on glass from below the waterline.

naturalists dismissed the stories as grief folklore. the flood had taken dozens of people, including children, and the idea of little faces in the water was considered a local mourning myth.

then the bells started ringing.

old harrow quay still had a chapel tower standing above the floodwater. its bell rope hung down into the drowned nave, unreachable from the outside. on windless nights, the bell rang anyway. not loudly. just once or twice, soft enough to make listeners doubt themselves. villagers believed the dead were asking to be counted.

in 1812, a survey crew entered the chapel by boat.

they found the rope pulled through a broken side window and tied around a nest.

inside the flooded chapel, beneath the floating remains of hymn boards and rotted pews, lived a colony of small shelled pokãƒâ©mon. their eggs were hidden in the bell loft where floodwater could not reach them. young squirtle slept in pockets of air beneath the rafters. adults, or what researchers then assumed were adults, guarded the lower levels and pushed floating debris into barricades.

the bell had not been rung by ghosts.

it had been rung by squirtle tugging the rope whenever predators entered the chapel.

the first captured specimen was small, blue, and remarkably calm. it did not bite. it did not flee. it sat in a basin of water aboard the survey boat and watched the researchers with a patient, almost solemn expression. when one sailor coughed and leaned over the rail, the squirtle sprayed water into his face, then chirped until he stepped back from the edge.

the crew interpreted this as play.

the next day, the same squirtle dragged a sleeping deckhand by the sleeve toward a rain barrel during a storm.

that was the first recorded hint of the speciesã¢â‚¬â„¢ rescue-drowning confusion.

early anatomy work revealed that squirtle occupied a rare middle ground between land and water. it possessed lungs, strong swimming muscles, webbed hands and feet, and a shell that hardened with age. its body was built for shorelines, not open ocean. it could walk, climb muddy banks, hide in reeds, and launch pressurized water from its mouth with enough force to knock loose bark from trees.

the shell was the key.

researchers initially believed the shell was simple armor. later studies showed it functioned as protection, buoyancy control, water storage support, and social identity. squirtle recognize one another by shell marks, scratches, mineral stains, and growth patterns. in wild colonies, shell-touching is a greeting, a warning, and a form of reassurance. a frightened squirtle may press its shell against another member of the group to calm itself.

this is why captured squirtle often become attached to packs, crates, helmets, shields, and human furniture. they instinctively treat hard curved objects as social substitutes.

the old harrow specimens were not named squirtle at first. the earliest field notes called them ã¢â‚¬å“quay-shells,ã¢â‚¬â ã¢â‚¬å“river turtles,ã¢â‚¬â or ã¢â‚¬å“water imps.ã¢â‚¬â the modern name came later from dockworkers who described the young ones as ã¢â‚¬å“squirting turtlesã¢â‚¬â after they learned to spray anyone who tried to steal fish from the drying racks.

by 1815, squirtle colonies had been identified in several river mouths, marshlands, and coastal ruins. many appeared in places with recent flooding. this led to the first major theory of squirtle origin: that their ancestors were small freshwater reptiles displaced by repeated river disasters. those ancestors survived by becoming amphibious scavengers, nesting in hollow logs, beaver dams, cave mouths, and eventually human structures.

but the darker theory came from old harrow quay.

a naturalist named mara vell proposed that squirtle did not merely adapt to flooded human spaces. they learned from them. the species became successful because human settlements created perfect artificial reefs: walls to block current, upper floors for eggs, cellars for cool water, chimneys for air pockets, and debris fields full of hiding places.

in other words, squirtle may have spread because people kept building towns where water wanted to return.

this theory remains controversial, but modern records support part of it. squirtle populations often rise after controlled reservoirs, abandoned canals, broken aqueducts, and flooded mines become available. they are not drawn to tragedy in a supernatural sense. they are drawn to shelter, water, and objects that can be arranged into colony boundaries.

humans call these places ruins.

squirtle call them nurseries.

the 1812 old harrow colony also explains why later evolutions took longer to document. most squirtle observed by humans were young shoreline members. larger evolved forms withdrew into deeper water, older canals, sea caves, and protected nesting routes. wartortle would not be formally separated from squirtle until later, when researchers realized the long-tailed blue guardians seen around older colonies were not a regional variant, but the next stage of the line.

blastoise would take longer still.

people had stories of cannon-backed monsters before they had proof. sailors swore that some reefs fired water hard enough to break masts. harbor guards reported iron plates bent inward by invisible pressure. shipwreck survivors described a low shape beneath the waves with two black openings on its back.

for years, science ignored them.

science often does that when the ocean keeps the evidence.

the accepted squirtle-line chronology now begins here:

1812: squirtle formally recorded at old harrow quay after the chapel-bell colony survey.

the later records will build from that flood site, because the squirtle line is not just a water evolution family.

it is a history of what happens when living things inherit the places humans lose to water.

squirtle is the child at the edge of the river.

wartortle is the thing watching from the reeds.

blastoise is the reason old sailors stopped laughing at drowned bells.",

name: Venusaur
route: Ancient forest basins, protected botanical reserves, old battlefields reclaimed by vegetation, and isolated sunlit groves
habitat: Large humid clearings with deep soil, stable water, and enough sunlight to support a fully flowering dorsal plant
classification: Seed Pokemon / Mature Symbiotic Flower Beast
behaviorTemperament: Calm until territory or young are threatened. Venusaur prefer stillness, using scent, pollen, and vine pressure before physical pursuit. A threatened adult can turn a clearing into a chemical hazard.
diet: Sunlight, large volumes of water, mineral-rich soil, fruits, woody plants, insects, and occasional carrion composted into soil around its resting site
activityPattern: Mostly diurnal and sedentary. Most movement occurs at dawn, dusk, or before storms.
bestTimeToFind: From a distance at sunrise when the flower opens for light and dew
observationDifficulty: Moderate to hard. Large body is visible, but old individuals resemble a mound of vegetation until they move.
conservationStatus: Strictly protected keystone species
populationStatus: Rare, usually one dominant adult per large territory
description: The fully developed Bulbasaur line. A Venusaur is less mobile than its younger stages but far more dangerous because the flower can release dense pollen, spores, and chemical signals over a wide area.
lore: Some villages built cemeteries around Venusaur groves because the flowers bloomed brightest over old soil and the adults kept scavengers away.
survivalUse: Can create shade, reveal water tables, stabilize damaged soil, deter predators, and support pollinators. Its presence can also make an area unsafe for unmasked humans.
handlingWarning: Never enter a Venusaur grove without respiratory protection and permission from the animal. Do not cut flowers or leaves.
encounterAdvice: Observe from high ground and upwind. If the flower turns toward you before the head does, leave immediately and quietly.
firstAid: For pollen inhalation, move to clean air and seek medical care. For vine crush injuries, immobilize limb and treat for shock. For sap exposure, wash thoroughly.
companionSuitability: Poor for private companionship, excellent as a sanctuary guardian when respected
fieldNote: Silence in a Venusaur grove is not peace. It often means the pollen has already driven smaller animals away.
originStory: The grim origin is a symbiosis that became inheritance. What began as a seed feeding from a host eventually became a body that could not live without the animal beneath it. A Venusaur is less a beast with a flower than a mobile grave-garden that learned loyalty.

evolutionLine.name: Venusaur
evolutionLine.note: Venusaur does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms are treated as temporary or rare special forms, not normal wild maturity.

mainLine.name: Venusaur
mainLine.note: Venusaur does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms are treated as temporary or rare special forms, not normal wild maturity.

variants.method: Special catalyst, battle phenomenon, or Gigantamax factor

target.name: Mega Venusaur; Gigantamax Venusaur
target.note: Venusaur does not evolve further by level. Mega and Gigantamax forms are treated as temporary or rare special forms, not normal wild maturity.

domesticationStatus: Not domesticated. Sanctuary-managed only.
prey: Insects, plant material, fruits, small animals caught by vines, and nutrient compost
territoryRange: 5 to 20 square miles depending on water and sunlight
lifeCycle: Final standard form of the Bulbasaur line after Ivysaur reaches level 32. May access Mega or Gigantamax forms only under special conditions.
containmentRequirements: A preserve, not a cage. Requires deep soil, root barriers, drainage, pollinator exclusion zones, spore monitoring, and emergency evacuation lanes.
ecologicalRole: Keystone shade maker, soil builder, pollinator signal hub, seed bank protector, and invasive plant suppressor
diseaseParasiteRisk: High for allergens, fungal blooms, mites, contaminated pollen, and secondary infections after vine injury
reproductionNesting: Females lay guarded eggs in composted plant beds. Adults shade and chemically defend the nesting area until hatchlings disperse.
predators: Very few natural predators. Charizard, Arcanine packs, severe drought, disease, and humans are the main threats.
emergencyResponse: Treat pollen clouds as hazardous atmosphere. Move upwind, rinse exposed areas, monitor breathing, and call trained grass-poison response handlers.
legalStatus: Restricted keystone Pokemon. Capture or transport normally requires environmental authority approval.
lifespan: Wild: 7 to 10 years in high-conflict regions. Managed sanctuary: 30 years or more.
socialStructure: Solitary matriarch or patriarch with tolerated young and lower-stage relatives on the edge of the territory

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Elemental output is heavy pollen, vines, seed discharge, and solar-stored growth energy.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: Pollen and spore influence can affect a whole grove, often 50 to 150 ft or more with wind
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Slow burst, but vine reach is fast at close range
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Low body dodge, high vine interception
fieldCapabilities.balance: Extremely stable due to mass and low stance

trainingRisk: Pollen poisoning, vine crushing, sleep powder, territorial aggression, and enclosure overgrowth
physiology: The plant and animal portions are deeply integrated. The flower produces pollen, growth hormones, and chemical deterrents. Thick vascular roots inside the body draw water and minerals while the host heart supports both systems.

keyFacts.habitat: Ancient forest clearings and botanical preserves
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 7 to 10 years. Sanctuary: 30 years or more.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary adult with tolerated young nearby

domesticationDifficulty: Extreme. Requires land management, botanical hazard plans, and veterinary-botanical care.
physicalCharacteristics: Massive quadruped with thick legs, broad head, heavy body, large leaves, and a pink flowering crown. The flower may track sunlight or face threats independently of the head.
dietRestrictions: Avoid drought, concrete confinement, road salt, herbicides, heavy metals, and forced movement during flowering season.

name: Wartortle
route: Old docks, marsh channels, drowned basements, reed beds, irrigation cuts, submerged roads, and protected Squirtle nesting ruins
habitat: Connected freshwater territory with patrol channels, air pockets, submerged boundary markers, and young colony members nearby
behaviorTemperament: Deliberate, territorial, observant, and protective. Wartortle may save a human from drowning but may also refuse to release them until it decides the threat has passed.
diet: Aquatic plants, fish, shellfish, insects, small amphibians, carrion, stolen food, and prey driven into water channels
activityPattern: Crepuscular and nocturnal patrol activity, with daylight basking in hidden reeds or submerged rooms
bestTimeToFind: Dawn near old flood ruins, quiet docks, and reed lanes where pale ears or a tail drift at the surface
observationDifficulty: High. Wartortle usually notices observers first and can control the encounter from underwater.
conservationStatus: Protected evolved Water guardian species
populationStatus: Low-density but stable in mature Squirtle colonies and protected floodplain preserves
description: A bipedal blue turtle Pokemon with a reinforced shell, large tail, ear plumes, and expanded water-storage anatomy. In realistic field terms, it is a territorial water sentinel.
lore:
Wartortle is what the river keeps after it stops playing.

A Squirtle may splash at a child from the reeds. A Wartortle watches the childÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s parents first.

That is the difference.

The young stage is curious, social, and dangerously innocent. Wartortle is not innocent. It remembers. Not in the human way, perhaps, but in the old animal way that remembers which banks collapse, which boats carry nets, which voices bring food, and which footsteps mean hooks. A wild Wartortle can recognize a shoreline it has not visited in years. It can return to the same submerged doorway after floods rearrange the whole riverbed. It can find the bone pile under silt.

Old fishermen called them blue widowers because they appeared after drownings, sitting half-submerged in the reeds while searchers dragged the water. Their pale tails and ears would float like weed fronds, barely moving. Sometimes they watched for days. Sometimes, after the family gave up, the body would surface.

Sometimes it would not.

Modern rangers believe Wartortle often recover the drowned and move them away from active nesting sites. Villagers believed something darker: that Wartortle decided which dead were allowed to return.

A WartortleÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s shell is more than armor. It is a pressure vessel, a memory vault, and a warning drum. When threatened, Wartortle can pull water into internal chambers and release it in sudden bursts powerful enough to break ribs, shatter wooden oars, or knock a grown person backward into deep current. But before it attacks, it usually drums.

Three hollow knocks from beneath a dock.

A slow tapping against a boat hull.

A sound like stones rolling inside an empty well.

If you hear that at night, you are already too close.

Unlike Squirtle, Wartortle do not always retreat from humans. They assess. They circle. They position themselves between intruders and water exits, forcing panic in the direction they want. A lone Wartortle can herd a person across mud flats with short jets of water at the feet. A pair can flip a canoe without surfacing. A colony guardian can keep an entire search party away from a nest simply by striking the underside of the dock until the old boards begin to fail.

They rarely waste effort.

This is what frightens experienced handlers most. Wartortle does not rage like some predators. It does not shriek, posture, or bluff for long. Its violence is deliberate. It has learned that water does not need anger to kill.

The long ears and tail are often mistaken for decoration, especially by children. In truth, they are sensory organs and stabilizers. A resting Wartortle may let the plumes drift on the surface, reading minute changes in current. Footsteps on a dock, a fish struggling in a net, a human slipping on mud, a child dragging a stick through shallows: all of it reaches the plumes before it reaches the eyes.

This gives rise to one of the oldest shoreline sayings:

If you can see a WartortleÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s eyes, it is watching you.

If you can only see its tail, it already knows where you are.

Wartortle colonies form around old Squirtle nests, but they do not behave like simple family groups. The young splash, steal, chirp, and explore. Wartortle patrol the borders. They move through culverts, drowned basements, reed channels, flooded mine shafts, and irrigation ditches with the patience of things that know every route by touch.

In abandoned settlements, they become especially strange.

Divers have found Wartortle nesting in places where people once stored records, money, weapons, or keepsakes. Their dens often include objects arranged by weight and shape: locks, plates, tools, dolls, prayer beads, belt buckles, broken eyeglasses, shell casings, and polished stones. No one knows whether they understand these objects or simply use them for structure.

One ranger found an old Wartortle den in the flooded records office of Old Harrow Quay. The nest was built from ledger books, roof tiles, and the brass nameplates of the dead.

At the center was a childÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s school slate, scrubbed clean by water.

Wartortle are also known for rescue behavior, but it is more unsettling than SquirtleÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s. A Squirtle may drag a struggling human toward water because it mistakes submersion for safety. A Wartortle understands that humans breathe air. It will lift a drowning person toward the surface if it chooses to. It will push them toward shore. It will hold their head above water with surprising gentleness.

But it may also hold them there and not let them leave.

Several survivors of river accidents describe waking on mud banks with a Wartortle sitting nearby, one heavy forelimb resting across their chest like a restraint. When they tried to rise, the Wartortle pressed harder. When rescuers approached, it hissed and sprayed water at their feet. Some were held until dawn. Some until rain stopped. Some until another Wartortle arrived and inspected them.

One survivor wrote: Ã¢â‚¬Å“It saved me like I was property.Ã¢â‚¬Â

This is the heart of Wartortle lore. It protects what it claims. That protection may save a life, preserve a nest, defend a ruin, or imprison a trespasser until the river decides what they are worth.

Old Kanto communities treated Wartortle with cautious respect. They left fish at certain stones. They rang bells before crossing flooded roads. They taught children never to whistle at pale tails in the water. In some villages, if a person drowned and the body was not recovered within three days, the family would place a bowl of clean water on the doorstep and whisper the personÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s name into it. The belief was that a Wartortle might carry the name downstream and release the dead.

Field researchers dismiss this as mourning ritual.

Rangers are less quick to laugh.

There are too many cases where bodies surfaced after the bowl was placed.

Too many where the water outside the house was found disturbed by small, webbed tracks.

Too many where something blue watched from the ditch until sunrise.

The official field rules for Wartortle territory are harsher than for Squirtle:

Do not enter old flood ruins alone.

Do not step over lines of shells, stones, or stolen metal objects.

Do not follow tapping under docks.

Do not assume a rescued person is free to leave until the Wartortle leaves first.

And never strike a Squirtle in sight of a Wartortle.

The young may forgive.

The guardian stage remembers.

loreSummary: A guardian stage tied to drowned ruins, recovered bodies, boundary rituals, and the idea that a trainer becomes territory that walks.
survivalUse: Can defend camps near water, tow injured swimmers, locate underwater routes, break light debris with pressure bursts, and warn of flood-channel movement.
handlingWarning: Do not cross object lines, touch young Squirtle, enter old flood ruins alone, or try to pull away from a guarding Wartortle by force.
encounterAdvice: Stop, lower your hands, back away from the waterline, and leave by the same path. If it blocks you, choose another path rather than challenging it.
firstAid: Assess rib trauma, ankle injuries, hypothermia, near-drowning, and blunt-force impact from pressure bursts or shell strikes.
companionSuitability: Good for experienced handlers who can accept territorial behavior
fieldBehaviors: Positions itself between intruders and exits
Uses tail and ears to read currents
Moves bodies or objects away from nests
Guards injured humans like claimed property
Removes survey flags or unfamiliar markers
signsOfPresence: Pale ear plumes drifting like weeds
Large webbed tracks beside smaller Squirtle tracks
Fresh shell scrapes on submerged doors
Quiet water where birds avoid landing
fieldNote: If you can only see the tail or ear plumes, assume it already knows your location and is measuring your movement through the water.
originStory:
Wartortle was formally documented in 1824, twelve years after the Squirtle colony at Old Harrow Quay was accepted into the scientific record.

For years after the 1812 survey, researchers believed the larger blue figures seen near Squirtle nesting grounds were simply old Squirtle. This mistake nearly killed the second Harrow expedition.

The original Squirtle colony had been monitored from a distance after the chapel-bell discovery. Young specimens were seen in flooded houses, reed channels, and the collapsed market district. But local fishermen continued reporting something else: larger shapes that moved only at dawn, with pale ears like torn flags and tails that trailed behind them in the current.

The fishermen insisted these were not Squirtle.

The naturalists insisted fishermen exaggerated.

In winter of 1823, three researchers entered the drowned district to collect eggshell fragments from the chapel loft. They waited until the Squirtle colony left to feed. By their own account, the site was quiet. Too quiet. No splashing. No chirping. No young visible in the rafters.

The first warning was a hollow knock from beneath their boat.

The second was another knock from the opposite side.

The third came from inside the chapel.

A Wartortle surfaced between the pews.

The expedition notes describe it as Ã¢â‚¬Å“a mature shelled water beast of Squirtle character, but with a longer tail, enlarged auditory plumes, heavier shell, and a stare more deliberate than any juvenile specimen.Ã¢â‚¬Â It did not attack immediately. It watched them remove the eggshell fragments. Then it struck the boat from below hard enough to split a plank.

One researcher drowned in the chapel nave.

A second survived by clinging to the bell rope.

The third was found at dawn on the roof of the old post office, alive but hypothermic, with bruises around both ankles. He claimed the Wartortle had dragged him there after the boat sank. He also claimed it sat beside him all night and struck him with water every time he tried to climb down.

His account was dismissed as shock.

Then the shell marks were found in the mud below.

In 1824, the Crown Natural Society returned with better equipment, shore guards, and one of the original Squirtle handlers from the 1812 survey. This time, they did not enter the chapel first. They waited at the boundary and placed fish on a half-submerged stone used by locals as an offering site.

At sunset, a Wartortle surfaced.

The handler recognized it by a notch in its shell. It had been one of the juvenile Squirtle observed twelve years earlier.

This proved that the larger shoreline guardian was not a separate species and not merely an aged variant. It was the next evolutionary stage.

The discovery established the second point in the Squirtle-line chronology:

1812: Squirtle formally recorded at Old Harrow Quay.

1824: Wartortle confirmed after a marked juvenile from the original colony was identified as an evolved guardian.

The early Wartortle studies revealed several important changes from Squirtle. The shell thickened and became more structurally complex. The limbs strengthened for both swimming and short land movement. The tail and ears developed into current-sensitive plumes. The water-storage system expanded, allowing stronger bursts and longer patrols away from open water.

Behavior changed even more dramatically.

Squirtle behaved like colony children.

Wartortle behaved like border guards.

They patrolled outer channels, repaired nest structures, recovered stolen eggs, and drove off predators. They also showed selective tolerance toward humans. Fishermen who left offerings were ignored. Egg collectors were attacked. Children were watched but not always harmed. Armed surveyors were met with immediate threat displays.

One early naturalist wrote that Wartortle possessed Ã¢â‚¬Å“the moral character of a locked gate.Ã¢â‚¬Â

This was meant as mockery, but later ranger manuals adopted the phrase.

The species seemed to understand boundaries. Not laws, not property, not human rights, but boundaries in the old territorial sense. Nest. Bank. Ruin. Current. Young. Dead. Stranger. Threat. Claimed.

Everything belonged to one of those categories.

The hardest part of studying Wartortle was that the PokÃƒÂ©mon changed the study site faster than humans could map it. Flooded rooms were blocked with debris. New channels appeared overnight. Marked stones vanished. Survey flags were removed and stacked neatly on the offering rock. Once, a researcherÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s notebook disappeared from a dock and reappeared three days later inside a Squirtle nest, packed in mud with the pages pressed flat.

No one admitted being impressed.

Everyone was impressed.

The origin theory that developed after 1824 expanded Mara VellÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s earlier Squirtle hypothesis. If Squirtle evolved to exploit flooded human spaces as nurseries, Wartortle evolved to defend those nurseries from everything that came after: predators, scavengers, egg thieves, surveyors, and returning humans who wanted their ruins back.

This made Wartortle one of the first PokÃƒÂ©mon formally described as a post-disaster territorial specialist.

It did not simply survive in ruined habitats.

It organized them.

The long-standing folklore of Ã¢â‚¬Å“water sentriesÃ¢â‚¬Â began to make sense. Villagers had not imagined guardian spirits after floods. They had seen Wartortle patrolling the borders of new aquatic territory. The bells, offerings, carved shells, and doorstep water bowls were not random superstition. They were human attempts to communicate with an animal that already understood ritual through repetition.

Feed this stone, pass safely.

Cross that marker, be warned.

Take from the nest, be taken.

The most controversial record from this period concerns the drowned chapel bell. After the 1824 expedition, researchers tried to remove the bell for preservation. The local villagers objected, saying the bell no longer belonged to them. The Society ignored them.

That night, every boat tied near the old quay was cut loose.

The next morning, the bell had fallen through the rotten chapel floor into the flooded nave below. Its bronze surface was later found wedged beneath collapsed stone, surrounded by shells, bones, and stolen metal objects arranged in a ring.

The Society marked the bell as unrecoverable.

The villagers marked the place as settled.

Wartortle remains the stage where the Squirtle line stops being charming and starts being sovereign. It is still capable of loyalty. It can still bond with trainers. It may defend a human with terrifying dedication. But the bond is never simple ownership. To a Wartortle, a trainer is not master.

A trainer is territory that walks.

And territory must be guarded, corrected, retrieved, and sometimes dragged back from places it should not have gone.

Squirtle is the child at the riverÃ¢â‚¬â„¢s edge.

Wartortle is the hand around your ankle.

Blastoise is what waits where the river meets the sea.
originSummary: Confirmed in 1824 after a marked juvenile from the 1812 Old Harrow Quay Squirtle colony was identified as an evolved guardian.

evolutionLine.name: Squirtle
evolutionLine.note: Tiny Turtle Water-type. Juvenile shoreline colony member with shell withdrawal and pressurized mouth-spray behavior.

evolutionLine.name: Wartortle
evolutionLine.note: Turtle Water-type. Territorial guardian stage with stronger limbs, longer tail, ear plumes, and heavier water-pressure biology.

evolutionLine.name: Blastoise
evolutionLine.note: Shellfish Water-type. Final armored pressure-cannon stage with Mega and Gigantamax battle phenomena.

mainLine.name: Squirtle
mainLine.note: Juvenile shoreline survivor. Shell hardens after hatching and water projection begins early.

mainLine.name: Wartortle
mainLine.note: Guardian stage. Ear and tail plumes help balance and current-sensing during fast swimming.

mainLine.name: Blastoise
mainLine.note: Final heavy-shell stage. Carries high-pressure shell cannons and functions as a living harbor weapon.

target.name: Mega Blastoise
target.note: Temporary form with intensified cannon output, Mega Launcher ability, and increased shell mass.

target.name: Gigantamax Blastoise
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat water output and recoil forces as disaster-scale hazards.

domesticationStatus: Trainable but territorial, best handled as a partner-guardian rather than an obedient pet
prey: Fish, amphibians, aquatic insects, small mammals near water, carrion, and food taken from shoreline camps
territoryRange: Usually 2 to 10 square miles of connected water, bank, culvert, ruin, and nursery channels
lifeCycle: Evolves from Squirtle at level 16, expands water-storage organs and sensory plumes, and evolves into Blastoise at level 36.
containmentRequirements: Large aquatic enclosure with patrol routes, visual boundaries, dry basking ledges, no public shoreline access, and enrichment objects that can be arranged without harming plumbing.
ecologicalRole: Defends Squirtle colonies, controls aquatic scavengers, organizes flood debris, and stabilizes nesting boundaries in post-disaster wetlands.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Tail-fur fungus, algae imbalance, shell infections, waterborne parasites, and joint strain from poor land surfaces are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Does not serve as the primary breeding stage in field colonies, but defends egg routes, nursery chambers, and juvenile feeding lanes.
predators: Large Water-types, coordinated humans, territorial Blastoise, and rare apex river predators
emergencyResponse: Do not enter the water to negotiate. Remove bystanders, retreat from the marked line, wait for Water-type handlers, and avoid lifting claimed objects from the nest zone.
legalStatus: High-risk Water-type permit recommended or required, especially near public water infrastructure
lifespan: Wild: several years before evolution or territorial displacement. Managed care: long-lived with clean water, shell care, and low-stress boundaries.
socialStructure: Guardian of Squirtle colonies. Less playful than the juvenile stage, but capable of intense pair-bonding with trusted handlers.

fieldCapabilities.waterPressureLevel: Strong enough to injure at close range and break weak wood
fieldCapabilities.effectiveSprayRange: About 20 to 40 ft for aimed pressure bursts
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Strong aquatic sprint with moderate land movement
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent in water, limited on dry land
fieldCapabilities.balance: Highly stable in water due to tail and ear plume control

trainingRisk: Restraint injuries, near-drowning, overprotective guarding, pressure-burst trauma, tail-fur disease, and territorial refusal to release people or objects
physiology: Wartortle has a reinforced shell, expanded water-storage chambers, stronger limbs, and sensory ear and tail plumes that detect current changes. Its pressure bursts are more deliberate than Squirtle spray.

keyFacts.tailLength: Long furred tail used for balance, signaling, and current sensing
keyFacts.habitat: Flood ruins, marsh channels, docks, and nursery waterways
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild and managed lifespans vary, but folklore links this stage with extreme longevity.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually one or a few guardians per Squirtle nursery zone

domesticationDifficulty: High. Bonds deeply but treats people, gear, and paths as controllable territory.
physicalCharacteristics: Indigo-blue bipedal turtle body, cream belly, heavier shell, clawed limbs, pale ear plumes, and a large furred tail that deepens with age.
dietRestrictions: Avoid polluted standing water, excessive salt, low-protein diets during growth, and confined tanks that damage the tail or shell edges.

realPokemonData.evolutionRequirement: Evolves from Squirtle at level 16 and into Blastoise at level 36

realismNotes.interpretation: Game powers are treated as biological pressure, shell, swimming, and weather-response systems that could plausibly exist in a field-guide version of the real world.
realismNotes.keptGameRequirements: Water type, Torrent, Rain Dish as hidden ability, Squirtle to Wartortle at level 16, Wartortle to Blastoise at level 36, and Blastoise special forms.
realismNotes.safetyFraming: Water projection and shell impact are treated as physical hazards, with drowning and structural damage handled as the primary real-world risks.

researchSources.name: Pokemon.com PokÃƒÂ©dex
researchSources.usedFor: Official species category, height, weight, type, and listed standard ability.

researchSources.name: PokÃƒÂ©mon Database
researchSources.usedFor: National numbers, hidden ability, base stats, type defenses, move references, and evolution levels.

researchSources.name: User provided lore
researchSources.usedFor: Dark lore, origin stories, historical discovery chronology, and real-life field-guide tone.

researchSources.usedFor: Required structure, field names, and Field PokÃƒÂ©dex style.

{
  "id": "pokemon-wartortle",
  "entryid": "pkmn-0008",
  "pokedexnumber": "#0008",
  "name": "wartortle",
  "category": "animals",
  "type": "water creature",
  "elementtype": "water",
  "dangerlevel": "high",
  "rarity": "rare",
  "region": "kanto",
  "route": "old docks, marsh channels, drowned basements, reed beds, irrigation cuts, submerged roads, and protected squirtle nesting ruins",
  "habitat": "connected freshwater territory with patrol channels, air pockets, submerged boundary markers, and young colony members nearby",
  "classification": "turtle pokemon / shoreline guardian chelonian",
  "height": "3 ft 3 in",
  "weight": "49.6 lbs",
  "behaviortemperament": "deliberate, territorial, observant, and protective. wartortle may save a human from drowning but may also refuse to release them until it decides the threat has passed.",
  "diet": "aquatic plants, fish, shellfish, insects, small amphibians, carrion, stolen food, and prey driven into water channels",
  "activitypattern": "crepuscular and nocturnal patrol activity, with daylight basking in hidden reeds or submerged rooms",
  "besttimetofind": "dawn near old flood ruins, quiet docks, and reed lanes where pale ears or a tail drift at the surface",
  "observationdifficulty": "high. wartortle usually notices observers first and can control the encounter from underwater.",
  "conservationstatus": "protected evolved water guardian species",
  "populationstatus": "low-density but stable in mature squirtle colonies and protected floodplain preserves",
  "description": "a bipedal blue turtle pokemon with a reinforced shell, large tail, ear plumes, and expanded water-storage anatomy. in realistic field terms, it is a territorial water sentinel.",
  "lore": "wartortle is what the river keeps after it stops playing.

a squirtle may splash at a child from the reeds. a wartortle watches the childã¢â‚¬â„¢s parents first.

that is the difference.

the young stage is curious, social, and dangerously innocent. wartortle is not innocent. it remembers. not in the human way, perhaps, but in the old animal way that remembers which banks collapse, which boats carry nets, which voices bring food, and which footsteps mean hooks. a wild wartortle can recognize a shoreline it has not visited in years. it can return to the same submerged doorway after floods rearrange the whole riverbed. it can find the bone pile under silt.

old fishermen called them blue widowers because they appeared after drownings, sitting half-submerged in the reeds while searchers dragged the water. their pale tails and ears would float like weed fronds, barely moving. sometimes they watched for days. sometimes, after the family gave up, the body would surface.

sometimes it would not.

modern rangers believe wartortle often recover the drowned and move them away from active nesting sites. villagers believed something darker: that wartortle decided which dead were allowed to return.

a wartortleã¢â‚¬â„¢s shell is more than armor. it is a pressure vessel, a memory vault, and a warning drum. when threatened, wartortle can pull water into internal chambers and release it in sudden bursts powerful enough to break ribs, shatter wooden oars, or knock a grown person backward into deep current. but before it attacks, it usually drums.

three hollow knocks from beneath a dock.

a slow tapping against a boat hull.

a sound like stones rolling inside an empty well.

if you hear that at night, you are already too close.

unlike squirtle, wartortle do not always retreat from humans. they assess. they circle. they position themselves between intruders and water exits, forcing panic in the direction they want. a lone wartortle can herd a person across mud flats with short jets of water at the feet. a pair can flip a canoe without surfacing. a colony guardian can keep an entire search party away from a nest simply by striking the underside of the dock until the old boards begin to fail.

they rarely waste effort.

this is what frightens experienced handlers most. wartortle does not rage like some predators. it does not shriek, posture, or bluff for long. its violence is deliberate. it has learned that water does not need anger to kill.

the long ears and tail are often mistaken for decoration, especially by children. in truth, they are sensory organs and stabilizers. a resting wartortle may let the plumes drift on the surface, reading minute changes in current. footsteps on a dock, a fish struggling in a net, a human slipping on mud, a child dragging a stick through shallows: all of it reaches the plumes before it reaches the eyes.

this gives rise to one of the oldest shoreline sayings:

if you can see a wartortleã¢â‚¬â„¢s eyes, it is watching you.

if you can only see its tail, it already knows where you are.

wartortle colonies form around old squirtle nests, but they do not behave like simple family groups. the young splash, steal, chirp, and explore. wartortle patrol the borders. they move through culverts, drowned basements, reed channels, flooded mine shafts, and irrigation ditches with the patience of things that know every route by touch.

in abandoned settlements, they become especially strange.

divers have found wartortle nesting in places where people once stored records, money, weapons, or keepsakes. their dens often include objects arranged by weight and shape: locks, plates, tools, dolls, prayer beads, belt buckles, broken eyeglasses, shell casings, and polished stones. no one knows whether they understand these objects or simply use them for structure.

one ranger found an old wartortle den in the flooded records office of old harrow quay. the nest was built from ledger books, roof tiles, and the brass nameplates of the dead.

at the center was a childã¢â‚¬â„¢s school slate, scrubbed clean by water.

wartortle are also known for rescue behavior, but it is more unsettling than squirtleã¢â‚¬â„¢s. a squirtle may drag a struggling human toward water because it mistakes submersion for safety. a wartortle understands that humans breathe air. it will lift a drowning person toward the surface if it chooses to. it will push them toward shore. it will hold their head above water with surprising gentleness.

but it may also hold them there and not let them leave.

several survivors of river accidents describe waking on mud banks with a wartortle sitting nearby, one heavy forelimb resting across their chest like a restraint. when they tried to rise, the wartortle pressed harder. when rescuers approached, it hissed and sprayed water at their feet. some were held until dawn. some until rain stopped. some until another wartortle arrived and inspected them.

one survivor wrote: ã¢â‚¬å“it saved me like i was property.ã¢â‚¬â

this is the heart of wartortle lore. it protects what it claims. that protection may save a life, preserve a nest, defend a ruin, or imprison a trespasser until the river decides what they are worth.

old kanto communities treated wartortle with cautious respect. they left fish at certain stones. they rang bells before crossing flooded roads. they taught children never to whistle at pale tails in the water. in some villages, if a person drowned and the body was not recovered within three days, the family would place a bowl of clean water on the doorstep and whisper the personã¢â‚¬â„¢s name into it. the belief was that a wartortle might carry the name downstream and release the dead.

field researchers dismiss this as mourning ritual.

rangers are less quick to laugh.

there are too many cases where bodies surfaced after the bowl was placed.

too many where the water outside the house was found disturbed by small, webbed tracks.

too many where something blue watched from the ditch until sunrise.

the official field rules for wartortle territory are harsher than for squirtle:

do not enter old flood ruins alone.

do not step over lines of shells, stones, or stolen metal objects.

do not follow tapping under docks.

do not assume a rescued person is free to leave until the wartortle leaves first.

and never strike a squirtle in sight of a wartortle.

the young may forgive.

this proved that the larger shoreline guardian was not a separate species and not merely an aged variant. it was the next evolutionary stage.

the discovery established the second point in the squirtle-line chronology:

1812: squirtle formally recorded at old harrow quay.

1824: wartortle confirmed after a marked juvenile from the original colony was identified as an evolved guardian.

the early wartortle studies revealed several important changes from squirtle. the shell thickened and became more structurally complex. the limbs strengthened for both swimming and short land movement. the tail and ears developed into current-sensitive plumes. the water-storage system expanded, allowing stronger bursts and longer patrols away from open water.

behavior changed even more dramatically.

squirtle behaved like colony children.

wartortle behaved like border guards.

they patrolled outer channels, repaired nest structures, recovered stolen eggs, and drove off predators. they also showed selective tolerance toward humans. fishermen who left offerings were ignored. egg collectors were attacked. children were watched but not always harmed. armed surveyors were met with immediate threat displays.

one early naturalist wrote that wartortle possessed ã¢â‚¬å“the moral character of a locked gate.ã¢â‚¬â

this was meant as mockery, but later ranger manuals adopted the phrase.

the species seemed to understand boundaries. not laws, not property, not human rights, but boundaries in the old territorial sense. nest. bank. ruin. current. young. dead. stranger. threat. claimed.

everything belonged to one of those categories.

the hardest part of studying wartortle was that the pokãƒâ©mon changed the study site faster than humans could map it. flooded rooms were blocked with debris. new channels appeared overnight. marked stones vanished. survey flags were removed and stacked neatly on the offering rock. once, a researcherã¢â‚¬â„¢s notebook disappeared from a dock and reappeared three days later inside a squirtle nest, packed in mud with the pages pressed flat.

no one admitted being impressed.

everyone was impressed.

the origin theory that developed after 1824 expanded mara vellã¢â‚¬â„¢s earlier squirtle hypothesis. if squirtle evolved to exploit flooded human spaces as nurseries, wartortle evolved to defend those nurseries from everything that came after: predators, scavengers, egg thieves, surveyors, and returning humans who wanted their ruins back.

this made wartortle one of the first pokãƒâ©mon formally described as a post-disaster territorial specialist.

it did not simply survive in ruined habitats.

it organized them.

the long-standing folklore of ã¢â‚¬å“water sentriesã¢â‚¬â began to make sense. villagers had not imagined guardian spirits after floods. they had seen wartortle patrolling the borders of new aquatic territory. the bells, offerings, carved shells, and doorstep water bowls were not random superstition. they were human attempts to communicate with an animal that already understood ritual through repetition.

feed this stone, pass safely.

cross that marker, be warned.

take from the nest, be taken.

the most controversial record from this period concerns the drowned chapel bell. after the 1824 expedition, researchers tried to remove the bell for preservation. the local villagers objected, saying the bell no longer belonged to them. the society ignored them.

that night, every boat tied near the old quay was cut loose.

the next morning, the bell had fallen through the rotten chapel floor into the flooded nave below. its bronze surface was later found wedged beneath collapsed stone, surrounded by shells, bones, and stolen metal objects arranged in a ring.

the society marked the bell as unrecoverable.

the villagers marked the place as settled.

wartortle remains the stage where the squirtle line stops being charming and starts being sovereign. it is still capable of loyalty. it can still bond with trainers. it may defend a human with terrifying dedication. but the bond is never simple ownership. to a wartortle, a trainer is not master.

a trainer is territory that walks.

and territory must be guarded, corrected, retrieved, and sometimes dragged back from places it should not have gone.

squirtle is the child at the riverã¢â‚¬â„¢s edge.

wartortle is the hand around your ankle.

blastoise is what waits where the river meets the sea.",
